title: Training Methodologies (Enablement Patterns)
description: The human-and-agent enablement dimension: methodologies for upskilling learners through experience, located on the Craft Path. Sibling to methodologies.json (which builds AI systems).

patterns.name: Build Clinic
patterns.summary: A recurring session where participants bring one real task and leave with a working automation. The goal is a concrete time-saving tool produced in the session, not a demo.
patterns.intent: Turn one real recurring task per participant into a working automation inside a single facilitated session.
patterns.when_to_apply: Use when a team has adopted AI tools but individual workflows remain manual, and short peer sessions are more realistic than a formal course. Especially effective when different team members have different tasks — the bring-your-own-problem format keeps every session immediately relevant.

inputs.name: Nominated task
inputs.description: A specific recurring task submitted by each participant at least 24 hours before the session, scoped to something buildable in under two hours.

inputs.name: Facilitated build environment
inputs.description: A shared workspace with access to no-code tools (Zapier, Make, n8n) or AI assistants (Claude, ChatGPT), plus a facilitator who can unblock participants live.

inputs.name: Shared prompt or recipe library
inputs.description: An existing or nascent library where finished automations are deposited so the team benefits beyond the session.

outputs.name: A more capable learner
outputs.description: A participant who has personally built and tested one automation and can repeat the build process independently for their next task.

outputs.name: Working automation (Masterpiece)
outputs.description: A live, tested tool that handles a specific recurring task, verified in the session and saving measurable time within one week.

outputs.name: Library contribution
outputs.description: A documented prompt or automation blueprint added to the team's shared library within 24 hours of the clinic.

steps.name: Pre-session task nomination
steps.description: Participants submit one recurring task at least 24 hours before the clinic. The facilitator reviews submissions and helps anyone whose scope is too large to fit a single session, trimming the problem to a buildable slice.

steps.name: Scoped build
steps.description: Each participant builds their own automation using no-code or AI tools chosen to match the task. The facilitator floats, unblocking stuck participants without taking over — the learner drives. The session ends when the tool passes a live test on real data.

steps.name: Live test and retro
steps.description: Participants demo their automation to the group, noting what worked and what needs refinement. Retro is brief (5 minutes per participant); the goal is capturing one improvement action per tool. Finished prompts and blueprints are posted to the shared library before the session closes.

principles: Every participant ships something that runs on real data before leaving.
The facilitator unblocks; the learner drives — taking over to finish for someone erases the learning.
A working tool, however small, beats a polished demo of something not yet running.
Library contributions compound team capability; skipping the deposit wastes the clinic's value.
patterns.threshold: The participant can operate the automation they built without facilitation assistance and can explain what trigger, action, and edge case it handles.
patterns.masterpiece: A deployed automation that eliminates a specific recurring manual task, has passed a live test on real data during the clinic, and is documented in the team's shared library for others to adapt.

known_uses.name: AI Workshop — individual hands-on automation build
known_uses.quote: Every person on your team leaves with a working AI system in their specific workflow—not a group demo, but individual builds.
known_uses.note: Half-day format; same-day working system per participant.

known_uses.name: Soraia AI & Automation Workshop
known_uses.quote: The hands-on experience was incredibly practical and useful, providing real solutions to business needs.
known_uses.note: 4-hour hybrid workshop (1h theory + 3h hands-on), multilingual (IT/ES/FR/EN).

known_uses.name: Vision Base 生成AI・AIエージェント 2日間集中セミナー
known_uses.quote: 自社業務を即日プロトタイピング
known_uses.note: Japanese enterprise: same-day prototyping of company operations; 2-day intensive, Tokyo. lang: ja

known_failure_modes.note: The anti-pattern of the facilitator finishing a participant's build when they get stuck. This erases the hands-on learning and leaves the participant unable to build independently next time.
Accepting over-sized tasks without trimming at nomination stage means participants leave with half-built automations and no working tool — the opposite of the pattern's intent.
Finishing the session without depositing prompts and blueprints to the shared library. The anti-pattern converts a team-wide learning event into one-off individual wins that cannot be reused.

sources.quote: You leave with something running inside your business.
1 hour theory + 3 hours hands-on
自社業務を即日プロトタイピング

patterns.example_scenario: A marketing coordinator at a 60-person SaaS company attends a Tuesday Build Clinic. She nominated her task the day before: every Monday morning she manually copies campaign performance numbers from three spreadsheets into a summary email for her manager. During the 90-minute session she builds a Make scenario that pulls from Google Sheets, formats a summary, and sends it via Gmail at 8 AM every Monday. The facilitator helps her fix a date-formatting issue without touching her keyboard. At the live test, the scenario fires correctly against last Monday's real data. She adds the blueprint to the team library and tags it 'reporting'. Her manager notices the automation the following Monday and asks her to extend it for two other reports. The clinic produced one capable builder, one running tool, and one reusable recipe — all in under two hours.

patterns.name: Build Sprint
aliases: AIエージェントハッカソン
patterns.summary: A time-boxed intensive (48–72 hours, or a structured 5-day sprint) where mixed teams build and ship a working automation or tool by the end. Mentors float to unblock; the output is a live tool plus shared prompts, not a slide deck.
patterns.intent: Produce working automations that survive into production by compressing the build-ship-test cycle into a time-boxed team sprint with clear output gates.
patterns.when_to_apply: Use when an organisation wants to rapidly convert broad AI ambition into a tested portfolio of automations, and when the learning investment needs to pay back as live tools rather than awareness. Most effective when there is executive sponsorship, diverse team composition (technical and non-technical), and a shared problem space identified before the event.

inputs.name: Problem space brief
inputs.description: A pre-identified set of automation opportunities in current workflows, shared with participants before the event so teams arrive with context rather than spending the first hours on discovery.

inputs.name: Mixed-function teams
inputs.description: Groups of 4–5 participants spanning job functions, with at least one technical participant per team. Cross-functional composition ensures built tools are both technically sound and practically useful.

inputs.name: Infrastructure starter pack
inputs.description: API keys, compute credits, starter templates, and tool access provided upfront to remove setup friction from the first hours of the sprint.

inputs.name: Mentor pool
inputs.description: Technical and domain mentors available at a ratio of one pair per 2–3 teams, floating to unblock without taking over.

outputs.name: A more capable learner
outputs.description: Each participant who built something exits with hands-on experience scoping, building, and testing a real automation under realistic time pressure.

outputs.name: Deployed working automation (Masterpiece)
outputs.description: A live tool built and tested during the sprint, actively used in the week following the event — not a prototype archived after demo day.

outputs.name: Shared prompt and blueprint library entries
outputs.description: Winning automation prompts and workflow blueprints deposited into the organisation's shared library, so the sprint's learning compounds beyond the event.

steps.name: Pre-event preparation
steps.description: Organise teams by job function; share the problem space brief; provision infrastructure (API keys, tool access, starter templates); run a brief orientation to confirm all participants can access tools before the sprint clock starts.

steps.name: Alternating workshop and build days
steps.description: Use a structured rhythm — for example, Monday and Wednesday for facilitated workshops on problem framing and AI tool use, with Tuesday and Thursday as free build blocks. This prevents back-to-back passive learning and gives teams time to convert ideas into running tools.

steps.name: Mid-sprint checkpoint
steps.description: At the halfway point, each team demos their progress to a mentor pair. The checkpoint surfaces teams that are stuck on scope or tooling before it is too late to recover. Mentors redirect; they do not build.

steps.name: Demo day and library deposit
steps.description: Each team presents a working tool to a judging panel; evaluation criteria weight practical business value over technical complexity. Winning teams deposit prompts and blueprints into the shared library the same day. The panel confirms that at least one tool will be in active production use the following week.

principles: The deliverable is a deployed tool, not a slide deck — if it does not run on real data at demo day, it does not count.
Cross-functional teams build tools people actually use; homogeneous technical teams build tools nobody adopts.
Remove infrastructure friction before the clock starts; time spent on access and setup is time not spent building.
Post-sprint library deposits are mandatory, not optional — they convert individual sprint wins into lasting organisational capability.
patterns.threshold: Each participant can independently describe the automation their team built, explain the design choices made, and operate the tool without mentor assistance after the sprint ends.
patterns.masterpiece: A deployed automation that runs on live organisational data in the week after the sprint, whose prompt or blueprint is documented in the shared library, and whose business value is measurable by the team that owns the problem.

known_uses.name: Virtasant 6-Week Internal AI Hackathon Guide
known_uses.quote: 40-50 employees from the country's largest music licensing company (STIM) gathered for an impossible challenge: create functional AI solutions in just two days. By the end of 48 hours, they had built three working AI systems.
known_uses.note: Detailed enterprise playbook; mentor ratio, team sizing, budget, evaluation framework (Three I's: Idea, Implementation, Impact).

known_uses.name: Workshop Wednesday — AI Sprint: Ship in One Week
known_uses.quote: Every sprint ends with an automation your team runs next Monday.
known_uses.note: 5-day alternating workshop/build format; deliverables: deployed automation + 100-day rollout plan.

known_uses.name: Domo Enterprise AI Hackathon — Workflows
known_uses.quote: Building enterprise AI through hackathons delivers working workflows across customer service, marketing, product design, and sales in just 48 hours.
known_uses.note: SODA framework: Standardize → Optimize → Digitize → Automate.

known_uses.name: AngelHack AI Hackathon Guide for Teams
known_uses.quote: The quality of your submissions depends partly on how well you prepare participants before they start building.
known_uses.note: Covers virtual (1–4 weeks) and in-person (2-day) formats; judging criteria: technical execution, innovation, impact, presentation.

known_failure_modes.note: The anti-pattern of polishing a pitch for an unfinished prototype rather than shipping a running tool. Judging criteria that reward presentation over working code directly cause this failure.
Stacking teams with only technical participants produces tools the business does not adopt. The anti-pattern appears when managers decline to send non-technical staff to a 'technical event'.

Concluding the sprint without depositing winning blueprints into the shared library. The anti-pattern means the sprint's learning evaporates; six months later a new team reinvents the same automation.

sources.quote: Organizing participants into teams of five based on job function

Monday (Workshop): Problem definition; Tuesday (Build): Three working proof-of-concepts developed; Wednesday (Workshop): demos; Thursday (Build): chosen prototype refined; Friday: 100-day rollout planning
AI hackathons are one of the fastest ways for business leaders to turn broad AI ambition into a focused portfolio of tested use cases.

patterns.example_scenario: A 200-person financial services firm runs a 5-day Build Sprint with 30 participants across operations, compliance, and engineering. Teams receive a pre-brief: three known pain points around client onboarding. On Monday, a workshop establishes what AI tools can and cannot do in a regulated context. Tuesday is a build day; a mixed team of two compliance analysts and one developer builds a workflow that classifies incoming client documents and routes them to the correct review queue using Claude and Make. On Wednesday, a mid-sprint checkpoint reveals that a second team is still designing and has built nothing; a mentor helps them scope down to one trigger and one action. By Friday, four teams demo working tools on real onboarding data. Two tools are deployed to a test queue the following Monday. All four blueprints go into the firm's shared prompt library. Six months later, three more tools are built by other teams using those blueprints as starting points.

patterns.name: No-Code Workflow Track
patterns.summary: A structured, self-paced course track (2–8 hours) that teaches a non-technical person to build their first real automation using a no-code workflow tool. Ends with a badge or certification proving basic automation competency.
patterns.intent: Take a non-technical learner from zero to a working multi-app automation and a shareable certification, entirely through self-paced guided practice.
patterns.when_to_apply: Use when a learner needs to build practical automation competency on their own schedule, without attending a facilitated session, and when a credential that signals capability to employers or managers is a useful motivator. Most effective as a prerequisite or complement to Build Clinics and Sprints — the track gives the vocabulary and first hands-on experience; the clinic puts it to work on a real task.

inputs.name: Platform account
inputs.description: A free or trial account on the chosen no-code tool (n8n, Make, Zapier, or equivalent), giving the learner access to the same interface they will use in production.

inputs.name: Structured module sequence
inputs.description: A platform-provided or third-party course that introduces concepts one at a time and immediately requires the learner to build a small workflow demonstrating that concept.

inputs.name: Knowledge quiz
inputs.description: An end-of-track assessment (typically requiring 80% or higher to pass) that gatekeeps the certification and confirms comprehension is not just passive.

outputs.name: A more capable learner
outputs.description: A non-technical person who can independently build a trigger-action workflow connecting at least three real apps and can explain what each node in that workflow does.

outputs.name: Certification badge (Masterpiece)
outputs.description: A shareable credential (Credly badge, platform certificate, or LinkedIn certification) that documents automation competency and motivates continued practice.

outputs.name: Running workflow
outputs.description: A completed automation built during the final module that runs on a live schedule or trigger, proving the skill is operational not just theoretical.

steps.name: Concept modules with immediate build
steps.description: Each module introduces one concept (trigger, action, filter, iterator) and immediately requires building a small workflow that uses it. Passive video watching without building produces short-lived knowledge; the platform enforces the build before unlocking the next module.

steps.name: Progressively complex workflow build
steps.description: The learner builds a single workflow across several modules, adding complexity step by step. The n8n Level 1 course, for example, builds a 7-node order-processing workflow across the full track rather than starting fresh each module. This approach makes the final workflow genuinely useful.

steps.name: Knowledge quiz and certification
steps.description: The learner completes an end-of-track quiz (typically 10–20 questions, 80%+ to pass) that tests understanding of core concepts, not just recall of steps performed. Passing earns a badge or certificate shareable on LinkedIn or in email.

principles: Every module ends with a working node or workflow — concept without immediate build does not stick.
The final workflow must be genuinely useful to the learner's own work, not a toy example; this is what motivates completion.
The certification only earns its value if the quiz is genuinely gated at a meaningful pass threshold — a rubber-stamp cert teaches learners that the credential is cheap.
Self-pace does not mean no accountability; a Build Clinic follow-up within two weeks of badge completion converts passive competency into active use.
patterns.threshold: The learner can build a new workflow connecting three apps they have not used in the course, without consulting tutorial materials, and explain every node's role.
patterns.masterpiece: A completed, running automation that integrates at least three real applications and performs a useful recurring business task, accompanied by a shareable platform certification that a peer or manager can verify.

known_uses.name: n8n Beginner's Course Level 1 (free, certified)
known_uses.quote: At the end of the course, you can take a quiz to test your knowledge and get a badge, proving to the world your automation skills in n8n.
known_uses.note: ~2 hours; builds a 7-node order-processing workflow; 80%+ quiz threshold for badge. Two-level course system.

known_uses.name: Make Academy — Automation to AI Agents: Foundation (6 courses + badge)
known_uses.quote: Make Academy emphasizes hands-on learning, ensuring you can immediately implement AI into your automation workflows.
known_uses.note: 6 courses + 1 assessment; earns 'AI Automation Explorer' Credly badge; covers LLMs → AI agents in Make.

known_uses.name: Zapier Academy — A to Zap learning path
known_uses.quote: Automate even the most complex workflows using multi-step Zaps.
known_uses.note: Three-course free path: Automate your work → Build your first Zap → Customize your Zap.

known_uses.name: 패스트캠퍼스 — n8n 하나로 끝내는 AI 자동화의 모든 것
known_uses.quote: 자동화가 처음이어도! 이 강의 하나면, 직접 나만의 자동화 시스템을 만들 수 있게 됩니다.
known_uses.note: Korean; 25-hour, 6-part course; 6 hands-on projects; integrates 23 business and AI tools. lang: ko

known_failure_modes.note: The anti-pattern of watching all modules without building. Platforms that allow progress without executing build steps produce learners who pass the quiz on pattern recognition but cannot build a novel workflow.
Final workflows that use fictional data or apps the learner does not actually use. The anti-pattern means the learner finishes the track but cannot immediately apply the skill to their real work.

sources.quote: This free, online, hands-on course is the first in a series of courses that train you to become a low-code automation expert
We combine the theoretical information about basic concepts and components of n8n with practical (business) workflows
Step by step, you'll move from theory to hands-on practice, gaining both understanding and practical skills.

patterns.example_scenario: A customer support team lead at an e-commerce company has no programming background. Her manager asks her to reduce the time the team spends manually updating a tracking spreadsheet after every order status change. She starts the n8n Beginner's Course Level 1 on a Monday afternoon. Each module takes 15–20 minutes and ends with her executing a node in a real n8n canvas. By module 5, her workflow monitors a webhook for order status events, looks up the customer record, and appends a row to a Google Sheet — the same task her team does manually 40 times a day. She finishes the 7-node workflow by Wednesday and takes the quiz on Thursday, scoring 90%. She earns the badge, adds it to her LinkedIn profile, and demonstrates the running workflow to her manager. The automation saves the team roughly 90 minutes per day. Two weeks later she brings the workflow blueprint to the team's monthly Build Clinic as a contribution to the shared library.

patterns.name: Shared Move Library
patterns.summary: A team-maintained, searchable collection of proven prompts and automation blueprints that anyone can reuse and improve. It democratizes AI expertise and locks in gains from Build Clinics and Build Sprints.
patterns.intent: Preserve and multiply the value of individual AI discoveries by making proven prompts and automation blueprints searchable and reusable across a team.
patterns.when_to_apply: Use when a team runs any recurring AI build activity — clinics, sprints, or individual experimentation — and wants the gains from each session to compound rather than stay siloed in one person's workflow. Most valuable when team members share similar tasks but reach different quality levels because they each prompt from scratch.

inputs.name: Proven prompts and automation blueprints
inputs.description: Tested, working prompts and workflow configurations produced during Build Clinics, Build Sprints, or individual practice — the raw material the library organises.

inputs.name: Shared platform with permissions
inputs.description: A searchable platform (Notion, SharePoint, TeamAI, PromptFluent, or equivalent) with folder categories, naming conventions, and role-based edit gates. Anyone can read and use; only reviewers can add or modify.

inputs.name: Review role assignment
inputs.description: At least one named person per team or department who vets new submissions for quality, accuracy, and security before they appear in the searchable library.

outputs.name: A more capable learner
outputs.description: A team member who finds and adapts an existing library entry arrives at a working result faster and learns what good prompting looks like by reading tested examples, not just their own attempts.

outputs.name: Reusable prompt or blueprint (Masterpiece)
outputs.description: A documented, reviewed, production-tested entry in the shared library that another team member has successfully reused to solve their own task without asking the original author for help.

outputs.name: Organisational AI memory
outputs.description: A living body of team-specific AI knowledge that survives staff turnover and makes the organisation incrementally more capable each time a good prompt is added.

steps.name: Choose platform and set structure
steps.description: Select a platform with search, categories, and role-based permissions. Agree on a naming convention (role + task + tool, e.g. 'ops-weekly-report-make') before the first entry is added; retrofitting structure onto a chaotic library is painful. Create an initial folder taxonomy by role or task type.

steps.name: Populate from existing events
steps.description: Immediately after every Build Clinic or Sprint, winning prompts and blueprints are deposited within 24 hours while context is fresh. The deposit window must be mandatory, not optional — the review role checks and approves within one business day.

steps.name: Version and maintain
steps.description: Every entry carries a version number and a last-tested date. The review role runs a quarterly audit: entries untested in the last 90 days are marked 'needs review'; entries failing a re-test are archived. Outdated entries that stay in the library quietly erode trust.

steps.name: Recurring demo ritual
steps.description: Run an optional 15-minute 'Prompt Friday' or equivalent slot where one team member demos a new or notably improved library entry to the group. Keeps the library visible, surfaces the best recent additions, and creates a lightweight social incentive to contribute quality entries.

principles: A prompt that only one person knows is an asset at risk; a prompt in the library is organisational capability.
Quality gates beat quantity goals — 20 reviewed, working entries are more valuable than 200 untested ones.
Iteration is policy: the first library entry for any task is a starting point, not a finished product; version tracking makes improvement visible.
Outdated entries erode trust faster than an empty library; archive relentlessly.
patterns.threshold: A team member new to a task can find a relevant library entry, adapt it to their specific need without asking the original author for help, and produce a result that meets their quality bar on the first or second attempt.
patterns.masterpiece: A library entry that has been reused and adapted by at least three team members across different but related tasks, reducing their time-to-first-good-result compared to prompting from scratch — demonstrable by version history and usage notes.

known_uses.name: Wharton Generative AI Labs Prompt Library
known_uses.quote: Prompting with LLMs always begins with a goal in mind. What do you want to accomplish? What does success look like?
known_uses.note: CC BY 4.0 licensed; evidence-based templates with instructions, use cases, model options. Widely referenced as enterprise starting point.

known_uses.name: TeamAI Shared Prompt Libraries
known_uses.quote: A library gives you more control over quality because you decide which inputs are added and set rules for details like style and tone.
known_uses.note: Platform with role-based permissions; department-specific libraries and company-wide shared sets.

known_uses.name: Kartaca Corporate Prompt Library
known_uses.quote: Shared Drives ensure that prompts belong to the team, not an individual.
known_uses.note: RCTC framework for reusable prompt templates; governance via naming conventions and iteration-as-policy.

known_failure_modes.note: The anti-pattern of allowing anyone to add untested prompts without review. The library fills quickly but becomes unreliable; team members stop consulting it after two or three bad results.

Publishing entries without version tracking or a staleness audit. Prompt quality degrades silently as models update and workflows change; the anti-pattern creates a library full of entries that look authoritative but fail in practice.

The anti-pattern of keeping effective prompts private to protect individual productivity advantage. Common in competitive team cultures; it directly prevents the library from reaching the density needed to be useful.

sources.quote: Test your prompt multiple times to ensure consistent results
Set permissions within your organization so everyone can view and use ideas, but only certain people can change them.
Leaders must establish 'Iteration as a Policy,' teaching teams that the first AI response is a starting point, not the final product.

patterns.example_scenario: A 45-person consultancy runs monthly Build Clinics for six months. After each clinic, the facilitator ensures participants deposit their best prompt to a Notion database organised by service line and task type. By month three the library has 28 reviewed entries covering proposal drafting, meeting summary formatting, and client briefing generation. A new analyst joins in month four; on her second day she searches 'proposal' and finds three entries, adapts the highest-rated one to her client's industry, and delivers a first draft in 40 minutes rather than three hours. She notes a small improvement in the comments; the review role incorporates it as version 1.1. By month six the library has 60 entries and the firm stops running onboarding sessions on 'how to prompt' — new hires learn from the library itself. The masterpiece is not any single entry but the library's aggregate effect: the organisation's average time-to-first-good-result on covered tasks has visibly shortened.

patterns.name: Automation Sprint Bootcamp
patterns.summary: A part-time, multi-week bootcamp (4–12 weeks) that takes a non-engineer from zero to building and deploying AI-powered automations. Structured as sprints with real business deliverables; ends with a capstone project.
patterns.intent: Move a non-engineer from zero to independently deploying AI-powered automations through a structured sprint sequence, culminating in a capstone that solves a real business problem.
patterns.when_to_apply: Use when a learner needs more than a single track or clinic can provide. Specifically, use it when they need to progress from basic workflow automation through prompt engineering and into lightweight AI agent use, and when a multi-week commitment with a capstone credential is viable. Also the right choice when a team wants to produce internal automation champions who can lead future clinics and sprints.

inputs.name: Enrolled learner
inputs.description: A non-technical or operations-oriented professional motivated to build AI automation skills, with a real workflow problem in mind that can serve as the capstone project.

inputs.name: Sprint curriculum
inputs.description: A structured sequence of 2-week sprints covering: task identification, prompt engineering, chatbot building, no-code workflow tools, webhook and API integrations, RPA, and introductory AI agents.

inputs.name: Capstone problem brief
inputs.description: A real departmental or role-specific automation problem, either nominated by the learner or selected from a curated set, that provides meaningful scope for the final project.

inputs.name: Peer cohort or tutoring
inputs.description: Optional live tutoring sessions and peer cohort access that provide accountability, unblocking support, and the social context that sustains multi-week commitment.

outputs.name: A more capable learner
outputs.description: A professional who can independently identify automation opportunities, scope them, build working solutions using no-code tools and AI, and evaluate where risk or human review is required.

outputs.name: Deployed capstone automation (Masterpiece)
outputs.description: A deployed, documented AI automation that addresses a real business workflow problem and has passed a review gate — the tangible proof that the learner can operate at the automator level.

outputs.name: Completion certificate and portfolio
outputs.description: A shareable certificate and a skills portfolio of 5–7 projects built across the sprints, demonstrating progression from simple triggers to AI agent integrations.

steps.name: Sprint 1 — Task mapping
steps.description: The learner identifies and maps three to five recurring tasks, calculates the current weekly time cost for each, and selects one as the running project for the bootcamp. This sprint establishes the habit of quantifying automation value before building.

steps.name: Sprints 2–3 — No-code build
steps.description: The learner builds AI-powered chatbots, multi-step workflows, and simple integrations using no-code tools. Each sprint delivers one working project that could be put into immediate use. Concepts are introduced only when the build requires them.

steps.name: Sprint 4 — RPA and edge cases
steps.description: The learner addresses processes that cannot be handled by no-code tools alone: screen-scraping legacy interfaces, scheduled data pulls from systems without APIs, and handling exceptions. Sprint ends with one RPA workflow in test.

steps.name: Sprint 5+ — Advanced integrations and agents
steps.description: API and webhook integrations; building a lightweight AI agent for a decision-making task. The learner connects their no-code and RPA skills to AI layer, seeing how the components form a layered automation architecture.

steps.name: Capstone build and review
steps.description: The learner builds their full capstone automation addressing the real business problem selected in Sprint 1. The capstone is reviewed and graded — typically on automation correctness, measurable time saving, and documentation quality — and a certificate is issued on passing.

principles: Every sprint delivers a working project, not a lesson — if a sprint ends with only notes and no built tool, it has failed.
Quantify before you automate: time cost calculation in Sprint 1 prevents building automations that save minutes but cost hours to maintain.
Introduce concepts in build order, not textbook order — the learner encounters RPA when they hit a wall that no-code cannot solve, not as a scheduled topic.
The capstone must address a real problem the learner actually faces; a synthetic capstone produces a certificate that does not transfer to work.
patterns.threshold: The learner can independently scope a new automation problem, select the appropriate tool (no-code, RPA, or AI agent), build a working solution, and articulate where responsible-use or risk guardrails apply.
patterns.masterpiece: A deployed capstone automation that reduces a measured weekly time cost in a real workflow, documented with a prompt or blueprint suitable for the team's shared library, accompanied by a certified completion credential.

known_uses.note: 4-month, 7-sprint, part-time program. Covers prompt engineering → RPA → AI agents. Capstone: real-world business challenge.

known_uses.name: Maven AI Engineering Buildcamp: From RAG to Agents
known_uses.quote: Ship a Capstone AI Project — Start to Finish
known_uses.note: 6-week cohort; 8+ projects (FAQ assistant, coding agent, deep research agent); capstone + hackathon. Taught by Alexey Grigorev (DataTalks.Club, 100,000+ students).

known_uses.quote: What I loved about this course: hands-on experience building real AI systems (not just theory!), deep dives into RAG, vector databases, evaluation, and monitoring
known_uses.note: 10-week, free, open-source cohort; builds end-to-end RAG app; certification requires capstone + 3 peer reviews.

known_failure_modes.note: The anti-pattern of running a sprint that ends with only lecture notes and no built project. Common when facilitators underestimate build time and fill the sprint with too much concept. Learners cannot demonstrate sprint completion.

Assigning a capstone problem that is not from the learner's real work. The anti-pattern produces a certificate the learner cannot connect to their job; the skills atrophy within weeks because there is no real workflow to apply them to.

Teaching learners which buttons to press in one specific tool version rather than the underlying trigger-action-condition model. The anti-pattern means learners cannot transfer to a different tool or adapt when the tool interface changes.

patterns.example_scenario: A procurement analyst at a manufacturing firm enrols in a 4-month AI automation bootcamp. In Sprint 1 she maps her team's five most time-consuming tasks and identifies that supplier invoice matching costs the team roughly 12 hours per week across three people. She selects this as her capstone target. Sprints 2 and 3 build her skills on Make and a Claude-powered chatbot for internal FAQ. Sprint 4 confronts the challenge that their ERP system has no public API; she builds an RPA workflow using a browser automation tool to extract invoice data. Sprint 5 adds a decision agent that flags mismatches above a dollar threshold for human review. Her capstone automates the extraction-matching-flagging pipeline and reduces the weekly team effort from 12 hours to under 2. The grader confirms the tool is deployed and running; she earns her certificate. She presents the blueprint at the next team Build Clinic, becomes the department's internal automation lead, and runs two subsequent clinics herself.

patterns.name: Task Automation Reskilling Sprint
patterns.summary: A structured multi-week program that moves operations or business staff from manual repetitive work to AI-automated workflows. Focused on identify-automate-deploy cycles using RPA, AI agents, and no-code tools; no coding required.
patterns.intent: Equip operations and business staff to identify, build, and deploy AI-powered automations that replace their own repetitive manual work, without writing code.
patterns.when_to_apply: Use when an operations team or business function faces high manual workload on repetitive tasks and the organisation wants to address that through upskilling rather than hiring or outsourcing. The reskilling sprint is the right frame — rather than 'automation course' — because the goal is role transformation: the learner moves from doing the task manually to owning the automation that does it. Most effective when there is departmental sponsorship and a real automation problem waiting on the other side of the program.

inputs.name: Operations-role learner with manual task burden
inputs.description: A business analyst, coordinator, or operations staff member with 5+ hours per week spent on identifiable repetitive tasks — data entry, report generation, status updates, routine communications — and employer support to invest time in the program.

inputs.name: Sprint curriculum covering the automation stack
inputs.description: A sequenced program that progresses from task mapping and prompt engineering through no-code workflow tools, chatbot building, API integrations, RPA, and introductory AI agents. No coding requirement; tools are selected for non-technical accessibility.

inputs.name: Departmental automation problem
inputs.description: A real, documented repetitive process within the learner's own department that can serve as the capstone target. Manager sign-off on this problem before the program starts ties the credential to tangible business value.

outputs.name: A more capable learner
outputs.description: An operations professional who can independently audit their own workflow for automation opportunities, scope them for no-code or AI tools, build and deploy solutions, and apply responsible-use judgment about when human review is required.

outputs.name: Deployed departmental automation (Masterpiece)
outputs.description: A deployed automation that measurably reduces manual hours in a real departmental process, certified by the program and approved by the learner's manager as production-ready.

outputs.name: Internal automation champion credential
outputs.description: A completion certificate and demonstrated skills portfolio that qualify the learner to lead future Build Clinics or mentor peers through their first automations.

steps.name: Sprint 1 — Identify and map
steps.description: The learner audits their weekly workflow, lists repetitive tasks, and calculates hours spent per task per week. They select a capstone candidate — the task with the highest time cost and lowest exception rate — and document the current manual process step by step. Quantifying the problem before building is what makes the program's output measurable.

steps.name: Sprints 2–3 — Chatbots and workflows
steps.description: The learner builds AI-powered chatbots for FAQ and intake tasks and constructs multi-step workflows connecting apps they already use. Each sprint delivers one working tool. Responsible-use and risk checkpoints are integrated here — the learner practises identifying what should not be fully automated.

steps.name: Sprint 4 — RPA for legacy processes
steps.description: For processes that cannot be handled by no-code tools — legacy systems with no API, desktop applications — the learner uses RPA tools to automate data extraction and entry. Sprint ends with one RPA workflow tested against real data.

steps.name: Sprint 5+ — Advanced integrations and AI agents
steps.description: The learner connects APIs, webhooks, and introductory AI agents to handle decision-routing tasks. By this sprint they can assemble a layered automation: no-code workflow triggers an RPA step when needed, and an AI agent routes exceptions.

steps.name: Capstone and career transition
steps.description: The learner builds and deploys their capstone automation addressing the departmental problem identified in Sprint 1. The program includes portfolio support and optional career-transition preparation, positioning the learner as an internal automation resource rather than an employee whose role is at risk.

principles: Reskilling, not replacement — the program's frame is that the learner becomes the person who builds and owns the automations, not the person automated out of a job.
Quantify before building — the Sprint 1 task map anchors every subsequent build to a measurable time saving, making the program's value legible to management.

Responsible-use checkpoints belong in the build sprints, not an introductory module — learners understand the stakes of automating without human review when they encounter it while building, not in the abstract.
The capstone must reduce hours in a process the learner's manager recognises; a notional capstone produces a credential with no organisational credibility.
patterns.threshold: The learner can audit a new repetitive task, scope it for the appropriate automation tool, build a working solution, and deliver a summary to their manager that quantifies the time saving and documents where human review remains required.
patterns.masterpiece: A deployed automation that eliminates at least 3 hours per week of manual work in a documented departmental process, manager-approved, with a responsible-use risk note, deposited as a blueprint in the team's shared library.

known_uses.name: TripleTen AI Automation Bootcamp
known_uses.quote: Free people from dull work. Use AI to automate routine tasks.
known_uses.note: 7-sprint, 4-month; covers Zapier, HubSpot, RPA, AI agents; no mandatory meeting times; flexible remote schedule.

known_uses.name: 패스트캠퍼스 복붙으로 끝내는 AI 업무 자동화 with Claude
known_uses.quote: 클로드와 함께 복붙만으로 끝내는 초간단 업무 자동화
known_uses.note: Korean; copy-paste-driven workflow automation with Claude; covers data analysis, marketing, HR, customer service. lang: ko

known_failure_modes.note: The anti-pattern of running a reskilling program for optics rather than genuine role transformation. Visible when the capstone is a trivial automation that saves minutes per week while the learner's actual high-cost manual tasks remain untouched. Manager sign-off on the capstone problem before Sprint 1 is the direct counter-move.

The anti-pattern of completing the program without integrating responsible-use and risk checkpoints into the build sprints. Learners who have not explicitly practised identifying what should not be automated will eventually automate something that should have a human in the loop.

Completing the reskilling sprint without transitioning the learner into a Build Clinic facilitator or library contributor role. The anti-pattern leaves newly capable automators as isolated experts; connecting them to the team's recurring build events multiplies their impact.

patterns.example_scenario: A billing coordinator at an insurance back-office processes 80–100 manual claims-status update emails per day, spending roughly 4 hours on a task that is mostly copy-paste from a claims system into email templates. She enrols in a 4-month reskilling sprint. Sprint 1 maps her top five manual tasks: claims status emails (4 hrs/day), monthly reconciliation report (6 hrs/month), new-policy intake form routing (2 hrs/day), and two smaller tasks. She and her manager agree the claims email is the capstone. Sprint 3 delivers a Make workflow that queries the claims system via API, fills an email template, and queues the message for her approval before sending — she reviews a batch, not individual emails. Sprint 4 is not needed (the claims system has an API); instead she uses the slot to automate the reconciliation report. Sprint 5 adds a routing agent for intake forms. Her capstone deploys the full claims email workflow: it saves 3.5 hours daily and she reviews the AI-drafted emails in a 20-minute batch each morning. Her manager signs off. She earns her certificate and runs the next month's Build Clinic, walking three colleagues through their first Make scenarios.

patterns.name: Agent-Build Course
patterns.summary: A structured course that walks builders through the four agentic design patterns: reflection, tool use, planning, and multi-agent collaboration. Each pattern is paired with code labs. Graduates leave able to build and ship a working agent.
patterns.intent: Graduate a builder who can identify, implement, and combine the four foundational agentic design patterns in a working, deployed agent.
patterns.when_to_apply: Use this move when a builder has basic Python literacy and wants a systematic introduction to agent design patterns, frameworks, and real deployments. It is the right choice over a single-framework or single-vendor tutorial.

inputs.name: Builder with basic Python literacy
inputs.description: A learner who can read and run Python scripts and understands API calls; no prior agent-framework experience required.

inputs.name: Agentic design pattern curriculum
inputs.description: Structured materials covering the four foundational patterns — reflection, tool use, planning, multi-agent — each paired with a runnable code example in a real framework such as LangGraph, CrewAI, or smolagents.

inputs.name: Framework sandbox environment
inputs.description: A pre-configured coding environment (Jupyter, Colab, or local venv) where each lab can be run without dependency friction.

outputs.name: A more capable learner
outputs.description: A builder who can recognize, explain, and implement all four foundational agentic design patterns and compose them in a single agent system.

outputs.name: Masterpiece: a deployed working agent
outputs.description: A shipped agent that uses tool-call and at least one other design pattern against a real task — the concrete proof the move was completed.

outputs.name: Completion credential
outputs.description: A certificate of completion from the course provider (Hugging Face, DeepLearning.AI, IBM Coursera, Microsoft Learn) that signals builder-level competence to employers.

steps.name: Learn each design pattern in isolation
steps.description: Work through reflection, tool use, planning, and multi-agent as separate modules. Each module pairs a conceptual explanation with a runnable code example so the builder can see the pattern in action before combining it with others.

steps.name: Build a pattern-composed agent
steps.description: Combine at least two patterns — typically tool use plus reflection or tool use plus planning — into a single working agent aimed at a real task. This step forces the builder to understand pattern interaction, not just individual mechanics.

steps.name: Complete capstone and earn credential
steps.description: Ship the finished agent through an assessment gate: a graded assignment, benchmark leaderboard (such as GAIA in the Hugging Face track), or peer review. Receiving the credential marks the threshold.

principles: Patterns before frameworks — understanding what reflection or planning does is more durable than memorizing a single SDK's API.
Runnable beats readable — every concept must be paired with code the learner can run, break, and modify to understand it.
Capstone under real conditions — the final agent must run against a real task, not a toy dataset.
patterns.threshold: The builder ships one deployed agent that uses tool-call and at least one other agentic design pattern, and can explain why each pattern choice fits the task.
patterns.masterpiece: A deployed, working agent that integrates at least two of the four foundational agentic design patterns against a real task — submitted for assessment and earning the course completion credential.

known_uses.note: 9-course professional certificate; 3-month timeline; frameworks include LangGraph, CrewAI, AG2, BeeAI, MCP

known_failure_modes.note: The anti-pattern of teaching only one framework so deeply that builders cannot transfer skills when the framework changes. Courses that lead with SDK syntax rather than design patterns produce builders who are stuck when the API is updated.

The anti-pattern of completing all video lessons without shipping the capstone agent. Without the build step, the builder gains pattern vocabulary but not pattern skill — the credential means nothing and the move was not completed.

patterns.example_scenario: A backend engineer at a mid-size logistics company has been writing REST APIs for five years but has never touched an agent framework. She enrolls in the DeepLearning.AI Agentic AI course to understand what her team is being asked to build. In the reflection module she sees how an LLM can critique its own output and loop until quality passes a threshold. In the tool-use module she wires a web-search tool to a simple agent and watches it answer questions it could not answer from memory alone. The planning module shows her how a task is decomposed into sub-steps before execution — the pattern her team actually needs for their route-optimization use case. For the capstone she builds an agent that accepts a shipment manifest, queries a freight-rate API, scores routes using a reflection loop, and outputs a ranked recommendation. The agent combines tool use and reflection — two patterns working together — in 140 lines of Python. She submits it, it passes the graded assessment, and she earns the completion certificate. Her manager sees the credential and assigns her as the agent-architecture lead on the team's next project. The masterpiece is not the certificate; it is the deployed agent that handles a real logistics task.

patterns.name: Center-of-Excellence AI-Native Engineering
patterns.summary: An enterprise Center of Excellence dedicated to training internal builders to design, build, and run agents on the company's own stack. Vendor technical-enablement partners support the CoE. The goal is a standing agent-building capacity across the organisation, not one-off experiments.
patterns.intent: Build a standing internal capacity to design, deploy, and operate agents at enterprise scale by training cohorts of engineers inside a dedicated CoE with vendor or specialist technical-enablement support.
patterns.when_to_apply: Use this move when an organisation has passed the proof-of-concept stage and needs repeatable agent-building capability across multiple teams — not when the first prototype is still being built. It requires an executive mandate, a dedicated team of 5–20 engineers, and at least one vendor or specialist partner willing to provide deep technical enablement, not just account management.

inputs.name: Executive mandate and CoE team
inputs.description: Leadership commitment to fund a dedicated team of 5–20 engineers for a sustained period, with a mandate to build production agents — not to evaluate them.

inputs.name: Vendor or specialist technical-enablement partner
inputs.description: An AI provider (such as Anthropic, OpenAI, Microsoft, or Google) or specialist consultancy that provides architecture guidance, code review, and failure-mode reviews to the CoE team — not just sales support or generic documentation.

inputs.name: Real production problems from internal stakeholders
inputs.description: A backlog of real automation opportunities from business units across the organisation — the raw material that each CoE cohort builds against.

inputs.name: Internal platform: reference architectures, prompt libraries, evaluation harnesses
inputs.description: A growing internal knowledge base that the CoE builds and maintains — reusable components that successive cohorts extend rather than rebuild from scratch.

outputs.name: A more capable learner
outputs.description: Each CoE cohort graduate who has built and shipped a production agent — and who returns to their home team as a local agent-building expert able to mentor colleagues without CoE support.

outputs.name: Masterpiece: a portfolio of production agents with measurable outcomes
outputs.description: A growing portfolio of agents built by CoE graduates, each with a documented business outcome — tasks automated, hours freed, cost reduced — that constitutes the organisation's evidence base for continued investment.

outputs.name: Internal reference architecture
outputs.description: A documented, tested, organisation-specific agent architecture standard — covering stack choices, security model, data access patterns, and observability — adopted by teams outside the CoE.

outputs.name: Distributed agent-building experts
outputs.description: CoE-trained engineers embedded in business units across the organisation who can scope, build, and operate agents without returning to the CoE for every new project.

steps.name: Stand up the CoE with a mandate and partner
steps.description: Secure executive sponsorship with a clear charter: the CoE produces production agents, not evaluations or strategy documents. Engage the technical-enablement partner and agree on what support they will provide — architecture reviews, office hours, failure-mode audits. Staff the CoE with engineers who have or can rapidly acquire foundational agent-building skills.

steps.name: Run rolling cohort embeds
steps.description: New builders from across the organisation embed with the CoE for 4–12 weeks. Each cohort member builds a real agent against a problem from their home team under CoE mentorship. The CoE lead reviews architecture decisions; the technical-enablement partner reviews for security, scalability, and failure modes. Cohort members ship before they exit.

steps.name: Build and publish internal reference assets
steps.description: As each cohort runs, the CoE documents what it learns. This produces a reference architecture for the company's stack, a prompt library with versioned templates, and an evaluation harness that tests agents against a common quality standard. These assets accumulate over time and reduce the ramp-up cost for each successive cohort.

steps.name: Measure and report business outcomes
steps.description: Track the agents shipped by CoE graduates: what they automate, what they save, what quality improvements they produce. Report this to the executive sponsor. The CoE's continued mandate depends on this evidence; without it, the programme is indistinguishable from an expensive experiment.

principles: The CoE builds, not advises — a CoE that only produces strategy documents and internal presentations is not a builder programme.
Cohort graduates return as experts — the exit condition is not a certification but the ability to mentor a colleague in the home team without CoE support.
The vendor partner enables, not replaces — technical enablement from a vendor supplements CoE capability; the CoE retains architectural ownership.
Outcomes trump activity — the programme's evidence is production agents with measured business impact, not hours of training delivered or courses completed.
patterns.threshold: CoE graduates have each shipped a monitored production agent with a documented business outcome, and the internal reference architecture has been adopted by at least one team outside the CoE.
patterns.masterpiece: A portfolio of production agents built by CoE cohort graduates — each with a documented business outcome and monitored in production — plus an internal reference architecture standard adopted by teams outside the CoE, demonstrating that the organisation now has standing agent-building capacity rather than isolated expertise.

known_uses.name: NEC / Anthropic AI-Native Engineering CoE
known_uses.quote: NEC will establish an internal Center of Excellence (CoE) with the aim of developing highly skilled AI professionals, utilizing technical support and training provided by Anthropic.
known_uses.note: 30,000 NEC employees on Claude globally; CoE targets one of Japan's largest AI-native engineering teams; announced April 2026

known_uses.name: NTT DATA OpenAI Center of Excellence
known_uses.quote: NTT DATA Launches Smart AI Agent™ to Accelerate Generative AI Adoption
known_uses.note: CoE serves as knowledge hub for OpenAI-stack agent implementation; targets $2B agent revenue by 2027; tens of thousands of GenAI practitioners

known_failure_modes.note: The anti-pattern of a CoE that produces guidelines, evaluation reports, and governance frameworks but does not ship production agents. An advisory CoE builds institutional authority but not institutional capability; the builder programme threshold is not met.

The anti-pattern of a CoE whose entire architecture expertise resides in the vendor technical-enablement team rather than in the CoE engineers. When the vendor relationship changes, the CoE's capability evaporates. Technical enablement must transfer knowledge, not just solve problems.

The anti-pattern of cohort members who embed with the CoE but do not ship an agent before returning to their home team. Without a shipped agent as the exit condition, the embed produces exposure but not capability, and the distributed-expert goal is not reached.

patterns.example_scenario: A Japanese technology integrator with 40,000 engineers is running scattered AI experiments across 30 business units but cannot get any agent past the proof-of-concept stage. Each team that builds something hits the same architecture problems, solves them independently, and cannot share the solution because there is no common platform. The CTO secures a board mandate: stand up a CoE with a two-year runway and a goal of 50 production agents shipped by end of year one. The company engages Anthropic as the technical-enablement partner. Anthropic provides three things. First, architecture office hours with CoE engineers every two weeks. Second, a code review of every agent before it moves to staging. Third, a failure-mode review checklist tailored to the company's security and compliance constraints. The first 12-engineer CoE cohort runs for eight weeks. Each engineer brings a real problem from their home team. By week six, all 12 have agents in staging. By week eight, nine are in production — the other three are blocked by data-access approvals outside the CoE's control, not by engineering failure. The nine who ship document their architectures in a shared internal wiki. By month three, the wiki holds enough patterns for the CoE to publish a reference architecture. It covers stack choices: Claude API, LangGraph, internal auth, and Datadog for observability. The CoE also publishes a prompt template library for the most common internal tasks and an evaluation harness that runs 50 standard test cases against any new agent before deployment. Cohort two's ramp time is 40% shorter because they start from the reference architecture rather than blank files. By the end of year one, 47 agents are in production across 18 business units. The masterpiece is not any individual agent but the portfolio. It contains 47 agents, each with a documented outcome, owned by engineers in business units who built them without CoE dependency. The reference architecture is what made cohort two's ramp 40% shorter than cohort one's.

patterns.name: Agent-Builder Dojo
patterns.summary: A short, intensive, small-group practice den lasting days to weeks. Builders repeatedly build real agents against real business problems under close facilitation. Measurable output — typically time-to-deploy or hours-saved — is the exit condition.
patterns.intent: Ship at least one production-candidate agent per participant in a compressed, high-accountability build environment where the facilitator unblocks rather than lectures.
patterns.when_to_apply: Use this move when a team of builders already has foundational pattern knowledge and needs hands-on repetition against their own real problems — not when participants still need concept introduction. A dojo is the right follow-on to a build course; it is the wrong choice as a first step for complete beginners.

inputs.name: Cohort of 6–20 builders with shared technical level
inputs.description: Participants who have at least basic familiarity with agent concepts and Python; ideally they have completed a build course or equivalent self-study. The shared technical baseline prevents the facilitator from splitting attention across wildly different starting points.

inputs.name: Real business problems from participants' own organisations
inputs.description: Each participant brings a specific, scoped problem from their own work context — not a toy problem. Solving real problems is what produces measurable outcomes and prevents the dojo from becoming academic.

inputs.name: Facilitator with agent-architecture depth
inputs.description: A practitioner who can unblock on architecture decisions, prompt structure, tool plumbing, and framework selection. The facilitator's role is reactive unblocking, not proactive lecturing.

inputs.name: Build-deploy infrastructure
inputs.description: Cloud access, API keys, and deployment tooling so participants can push working agents by end of each cycle, not just run them locally in notebooks.

outputs.name: A more capable learner
outputs.description: A builder who has shipped an agent under real constraints and can describe the design decisions, failure modes encountered, and the next iteration needed.

outputs.name: Masterpiece: a production-candidate deployed agent
outputs.description: A running agent — built by the participant against their own real problem — with a measured outcome such as tasks automated, hours freed, or error rate reduced.

outputs.name: Aggregate outcome report
outputs.description: A post-cohort summary tracking time savings, task-automation counts, or other business-impact metrics across all participants — the evidence base for continuing investment in the dojo programme.

steps.name: Problem scoping session
steps.description: Each participant presents their real business problem to the group. The facilitator and peers help scope it to the minimum agent design that can be shipped within the dojo's time box. Problems that are too broad get narrowed; problems that are trivially solvable without agents are redirected.

steps.name: Build-deploy-review cycles
steps.description: Daily or weekly cycles in which each participant builds, deploys something runnable, and gets a short review from the facilitator and peers. The cycle rhythm creates accountability and surfaces blockers early. No lecture-only sessions are permitted; every session produces a runnable artefact.

steps.name: Facilitated unblocking
steps.description: When a participant is stuck — on architecture, prompts, tool integration, or deployment — the facilitator unblocks in real time. Unblocking sessions are public when the problem is likely shared; private when it is specific to one participant's stack.

steps.name: Capstone demo and measurement
steps.description: Each participant presents their shipped agent with a measured outcome. The demo is live — the agent runs during the presentation. Outcomes are recorded in aggregate. Participants who have not shipped are not considered to have completed the dojo.

principles: Real problems only — a dojo that allows toy problems produces toy skills.
Facilitation is unblocking, not teaching — any session that turns into a lecture has lost the dojo format.
Ship something runnable every cycle — an agent that only runs in a notebook has not been built.
Measure what changed — time saved, tasks automated, error rate reduced: the dojo's proof is a number, not a feeling.
patterns.threshold: Each participant has shipped at least one production-candidate agent with a documented, measured outcome by the final session.
patterns.masterpiece: A running, deployed agent built against a real business problem from the participant's own organisation, with a measured outcome — tasks automated, hours freed, or quality metric improved — demonstrated live at the capstone session.

known_failure_modes.note: The anti-pattern of participants scoping down to problems that are safe and easy rather than real and hard. When the dojo permits toy problems, participants ship agents that do not survive contact with production and the measured outcome is zero.

The anti-pattern of the facilitator filling dead time with concept explanations. Once a session turns into a lecture, the dojo has become a classroom and the hands-on accountability structure collapses.

The anti-pattern of calling a Jupyter notebook that runs locally a 'deployed agent'. If the agent cannot be invoked by someone other than the builder without help, it has not been shipped and the dojo threshold has not been met.

patterns.example_scenario: A financial services firm runs a 4-week internal Agent-Builder Dojo with 12 engineers who completed the company's agent build course six weeks earlier. Each engineer brings a real problem from their team. One engineer is targeting the manual process of reconciling daily trade confirmations — currently two hours of analyst time per day. In the scoping session, the facilitator helps her narrow the problem to a single agent that reads incoming confirmation PDFs, extracts key fields, and flags discrepancies against the internal ledger. By end of week one she has a local prototype. The facilitator unblocks a tool-plumbing issue where the PDF parser was returning inconsistent JSON — a 20-minute fix that would have cost her two days alone. By week two the agent is deployed in a test environment reading real PDFs. By week three it is in production on a 10-trade daily sample. At the capstone demo in week four she shows the agent running live against that day's confirmations: 47 trades reconciled in 4 minutes, two discrepancies flagged, zero analyst time. Measured outcome: 1.8 analyst hours saved per day. The masterpiece is the running reconciliation agent with a documented time saving — not a notebook, not a demo script, but a deployed system processing real trade data.

patterns.name: Framework Deep-Dive
patterns.summary: A focused course on a single agent-orchestration framework (LangGraph, CrewAI, smolagents, AutoGen/AG2) that takes a builder beyond hello-world into state management, memory, human-in-the-loop, streaming, and production deployment.
patterns.intent: Take a builder from hello-world familiarity with a framework to production-level competence — including state management, memory, human-in-the-loop patterns, streaming, and deployment.
patterns.when_to_apply: Use this move after a builder understands the foundational agentic design patterns and has chosen — or been assigned — a specific framework as their team's standard. It is the wrong starting point for beginners. The abstractions make most sense once a builder already knows what a planning loop or a tool call does and wants to see how this specific framework implements them.

inputs.name: Builder with foundational agent design pattern knowledge
inputs.description: A practitioner who has completed a build course or equivalent and understands what reflection, tool use, planning, and multi-agent mean — independent of any one framework's syntax.

inputs.name: Chosen framework
inputs.description: The specific orchestration framework the course targets: LangGraph (state graphs, nodes, edges), CrewAI (crews, agents, tasks, processes), smolagents (CodeAgent, ToolCallingAgent, multi-agent hierarchies), or AutoGen/AG2 (conversational agents, group chats).

inputs.name: Framework documentation and course materials
inputs.description: The official framework documentation plus a structured course — such as LangChain Academy's Introduction to LangGraph or Deep Agents with LangGraph — that progresses from core model to production deployment.

outputs.name: A more capable learner
outputs.description: A builder who can design and implement stateful, multi-turn agents with memory, streaming, human-in-the-loop checkpoints, and multi-agent orchestration within the chosen framework — and who knows the framework's production deployment options.

outputs.name: Masterpiece: a stateful production agent
outputs.description: A deployed, stateful, multi-turn agent with at least one tool integration and one human-in-the-loop checkpoint — demonstrating that the builder has moved past hello-world into production-ready use of the framework.

outputs.name: Reusable project code
outputs.description: A set of runnable projects — one per module — that the builder can extend and adapt for their own production use cases, serving as a personal reference implementation library.

steps.name: Module 1: Core graph or agent model
steps.description: Understand the framework's fundamental abstraction: LangGraph's state graph with nodes and edges; CrewAI's crew, agent, and task hierarchy; smolagents' CodeAgent and tool registry; AG2's conversational agent and group chat. Build a minimal runnable agent that exercises the core model without any advanced features.

steps.name: Module 2: Memory and persistence
steps.description: Add short-term memory (conversation history, scratchpad) and long-term memory (vector store or database-backed) to the agent. Understand the framework's checkpointing model — how agent state is saved and restored across sessions. Build a multi-turn agent that remembers context between invocations.

steps.name: Module 3: Human-in-the-loop and streaming
steps.description: Add human-in-the-loop hooks: approval gates before high-stakes actions, interrupt-and-revise patterns, and feedback injection. Add streaming so the agent's intermediate steps are visible to the user in real time. Build an agent that streams its reasoning and pauses for human approval before any write operation.

steps.name: Module 4: Multi-agent orchestration
steps.description: Add a second agent — a subagent, a specialist, or a critic — and wire the two together using the framework's orchestration primitives. Understand routing, handoff, and shared state between agents. Build a two-agent system where the orchestrator delegates to the specialist and synthesises the result.

steps.name: Module 5: Deployment and monitoring
steps.description: Deploy the agent to a production-ready environment using the framework's deployment tooling: LangGraph Platform, CrewAI Enterprise, or a cloud container. Add observability: traces, error logs, and a basic dashboard. The module ends with a live endpoint that can be called from outside the developer's machine.

principles: Understand the framework's core abstraction before touching advanced features — a builder who skips the state model will misuse memory and streaming.
Every module ends with a runnable project — reading documentation without building is preparation, not learning.
Deploy before the course ends — a framework skill that has never produced a live endpoint has not been validated.
patterns.threshold: The builder deploys a stateful, multi-turn agent with at least one tool integration and one human-in-the-loop checkpoint on the chosen framework's production deployment tooling.
patterns.masterpiece: A live, externally callable stateful agent — built in the chosen framework, deployed to a production environment, with memory across turns, at least one human-in-the-loop checkpoint, and at least one real tool integration — that any team member can invoke without the builder's help.

known_failure_modes.note: The anti-pattern of completing module one and moving on to a new framework rather than going deep. Builders who collect hello-world familiarity across four frameworks without mastering any one of them cannot deploy production agents in any of them.

The anti-pattern of completing modules one through four and never doing module five because 'deployment is a DevOps problem'. A framework skill that has never produced a live endpoint has not been validated under production conditions; the masterpiece has not been built.

patterns.example_scenario: A machine learning engineer at a media company has read about LangGraph, run a few notebook examples, and understands in abstract what a state graph is. Her team has been asked to build a research assistant agent that gathers sources, summarises them, and lets an editor review and approve each summary before publication. She decides to do the LangChain Academy Introduction to LangGraph course to get past hello-world. Module one gives her the state graph mental model: nodes as processing steps, edges as routing logic, state as the typed dict that flows through the graph. She builds a minimal three-node graph (search, summarise, output) that works end-to-end. Module two adds memory: the agent now stores previous search sessions and does not re-fetch sources it has already processed. Module three adds the human-in-the-loop checkpoint her editor workflow requires. After each summary the graph pauses; the editor reads it and approves or requests a revision. The graph then continues, or re-runs the summarise node with the revision note injected. She adds streaming so the editor sees the summary appearing word by word rather than waiting for the whole output. Module four introduces a critique subagent that scores each summary for factual accuracy before it reaches the editor — a quality gate that reduces editor revision requests. Module five deploys the graph to LangGraph Platform with a REST endpoint, a LangSmith tracing integration, and an error alert on graph exceptions. The editor can now invoke the agent from a simple web form without the engineer's help. The masterpiece is the live research-assistant agent: stateful, human-in-the-loop approved, streaming, two-agent, production-deployed — and callable by the editorial team without a Python environment.

patterns.name: Platform Agent Certification
patterns.summary: A vendor-issued credential (Salesforce, Microsoft, Google) that certifies a builder can design, configure, and deploy agents on that platform, providing a measurable threshold and a recognised signal for employers.
patterns.intent: Certify that a builder can design, configure, and deploy agents on a specific vendor platform, creating a credential that is meaningful to employers and clients who use that platform.
patterns.when_to_apply: Use this move when a builder works primarily within a single vendor ecosystem (Salesforce, Microsoft Azure, or Google Cloud) and needs a formal, employer-recognised signal of platform-level competence. It is the right choice when the organisation has standardised on a vendor platform and agent builders need a common, auditable skill baseline. It is the wrong choice as the sole training path — vendor certification certifies platform configuration, not agent design thinking.

inputs.name: Builder working in a vendor ecosystem
inputs.description: A practitioner who uses or is expected to use the vendor's agent tooling in their role — Salesforce Agentforce, Azure Foundry Agent Service, or Google ADK and Vertex AI Agent Builder.

inputs.name: Vendor learning path
inputs.description: The structured self-paced materials on the vendor's learning platform: Salesforce Trailhead Agentblazer path, Microsoft Learn AI-3026 modules, or Google Skills Agentic AI path.

inputs.name: Vendor agent-builder tooling access
inputs.description: A sandbox or developer account on the platform so the builder can complete hands-on labs rather than only watch demonstrations.

outputs.name: A more capable learner
outputs.description: A builder who can configure, deploy, and monitor agents within the specific vendor platform — including the platform's tool integration model, agent orchestration primitives, and deployment pipeline.

outputs.name: Masterpiece: a working vendor-platform agent
outputs.description: An agent deployed on the vendor platform that demonstrates the capabilities tested in the certification: tool calling, orchestration, multi-agent coordination (where applicable), and monitoring.

outputs.name: Vendor-issued digital credential
outputs.description: A named certification badge (Salesforce Certified Agentforce Specialist, Microsoft AI-3026 completion, Google ADK Technical Expert Badge) that can be shared on professional profiles and is recognised by employers using that platform.

steps.name: Complete the vendor learning path
steps.description: Work through the structured modules on the vendor learning platform. Each module covers a specific capability: agent configuration, tool definition, orchestration, memory, guardrails, and deployment. Complete all hands-on labs in the vendor sandbox — skip no labs, as the exam or capstone tests lab-level knowledge.

steps.name: Build a platform-native agent
steps.description: Design and deploy an agent using the vendor's native tooling — not a framework wrapper. For Salesforce this means Agentforce configuration in the Agent Builder UI and Flow. For Microsoft this means Foundry Agent Service with MCP tool definitions. For Google this means ADK agent code deployed on Vertex AI. The agent must use at least two platform-specific capabilities not available in framework-neutral code.

steps.name: Pass the assessment gate
steps.description: Complete the vendor's assessment: a proctored exam (Salesforce), a learning-path completion check (Microsoft), or a capstone project submission (Google Skills). Earn the digital badge. The badge is the threshold; the agent built in step two is the masterpiece.

principles: Platform depth complements pattern breadth — a vendor cert is most valuable when the builder already understands agent design patterns independent of the platform.
Labs before lectures — the exam tests what you can configure, not what you can describe; skip no hands-on exercises.
The credential is a signal to employers, not proof of agent design skill — treat it as a floor, not a ceiling.
patterns.threshold: The builder passes the vendor assessment gate and earns the named digital credential: Salesforce Certified Agentforce Specialist, Microsoft AI-3026 path completion, or Google ADK Technical Expert Badge.
patterns.masterpiece: A working agent deployed on the vendor platform that uses at least two platform-specific capabilities — demonstrated during the capstone or exam lab — earning the vendor-issued digital credential.

known_uses.name: Salesforce Certified Agentforce Specialist

known_uses.name: Develop AI Agents on Azure (AI-3026)
