known_uses.note: 9-module learning path; covers Foundry Agent Service, custom tools, MCP, multi-agent orchestration, A2A protocol; free on Microsoft Learn

known_uses.name: Agentic AI on Google Cloud
known_uses.quote: accelerate knowledge exchange, integrate with enterprise databases, and deploy secure, advanced AI agent solutions on Google Cloud
known_uses.note: 9 activities; covers Gemini Enterprise Agent Platform (ex-Vertex AI), ADK, multi-agent security; technical expert badge

known_failure_modes.note: The anti-pattern of completing a platform certification without understanding the underlying agent design patterns. A builder who can configure Agentforce but cannot explain why a reflection loop or a planning pattern would or would not suit their use case cannot adapt when the platform changes its UI or when the requirement does not fit the vendor's agent model.

The anti-pattern of making the platform certification the organisation's only agent training, producing builders who cannot work outside the vendor's tooling. When the vendor changes its architecture, the team's skills are stranded.

patterns.example_scenario: A Salesforce administrator at a mid-market insurance company has been configuring Flows and custom objects for three years. Her manager asks her to lead the firm's Agentforce rollout. The target agent handles tier-one policy enquiries: it looks up policy data, answers coverage questions, and routes complex claims to a human agent. She has never built an AI agent. She starts the Agentblazer Trailhead path, working through the Agent Builder configuration modules over two weeks in her spare time. She completes all lab exercises in her developer sandbox. These cover creating an agent topic, defining actions backed by Flows and Apex, setting agent instructions, and configuring guardrails that block any action writing to policy records. In the third week she builds the policy enquiry agent in the full Trailhead environment. It retrieves policy data via a custom Apex action and answers coverage questions from a curated knowledge base. When the caller mentions a claim, a Flow routes the interaction to a human agent. She runs it against 20 test cases from real tier-one queries her team has logged. 17 pass without escalation. She registers for and passes the Certified Agentforce Specialist exam. Her digital badge is issued and she adds it to her LinkedIn profile. Within a month, three colleagues from other Salesforce shops reach out asking for advice on their own Agentforce rollouts. The masterpiece is the production-deployed policy enquiry agent — not the badge, which is the threshold signal that she is ready to be trusted with it.

patterns.name: Show the Working
patterns.summary: A training module that teaches builders to surface agent reasoning and action traces to end users — via logs, step-by-step summaries, or the explain-then-act pattern. Users can then verify agent behaviour and trust the output enough to adopt it.
patterns.intent: Teach builders to instrument their agents with human-readable reasoning traces so end users can verify agent behaviour without reading code or logs.
patterns.when_to_apply: Use this move whenever an agent's output will be acted upon by a human who did not write the agent — which is almost every production use case. Apply it before the agent reaches end users; retrofitting observability after a trust incident is harder and often too late to prevent adoption failure.

inputs.name: Builders with a working agent
inputs.description: Participants who have a prototype or production agent and are about to expose it to end users or stakeholders who need to trust its output.

inputs.name: Tracing tool access
inputs.description: An observability tool the builders can instrument during the workshop — OpenTelemetry, Langfuse, or LangSmith are common choices. Access needs to be configured before the session.

inputs.name: End-user acceptance test criteria
inputs.description: A clear definition of what a non-technical end user needs to be able to read in a trace to confirm the agent behaved correctly. This prevents the workshop from producing traces that only the builder can interpret.

outputs.name: A more capable learner
outputs.description: A builder who can instrument any agent with structured reasoning traces, write human-readable step summaries, and design an explain-then-act interaction pattern.

outputs.name: Masterpiece: an agent with a readable trace
outputs.description: The participant's agent updated to expose a human-readable trace or step summary to the end user — not just the final answer — verified by a non-technical tester who can confirm the agent behaved correctly without reading code.

outputs.name: Instrumented agent codebase
outputs.description: The agent's source code with tracing calls added, covering the three log layers: context (what the agent knew), reasoning (why it chose), and actions (what it did).

steps.name: Explain the trust gap and the three log layers
steps.description: Open with the core problem: over 65% of organisations cite lack of explainability as the primary barrier to AI adoption. Introduce the three log layers — context (what the agent knew), reasoning (why it chose), and actions (what it did) — as the minimum readable trace structure. Show examples from real agents: one with no trace, one with a developer-only log, and one with a human-readable summary.

steps.name: Demonstrate explain-then-act pattern
steps.description: Show an agent built with the explain-then-act pattern: before taking any action, the agent outputs a brief reasoning statement that the user can read and optionally interrupt. Walk through the implementation. Show where in the agent loop the reasoning step is inserted and how it is formatted for non-technical readers. Show how to add a human-in-the-loop gate for high-stakes actions.

steps.name: Instrument an existing agent
steps.description: Builders add tracing calls to their own agent using the chosen tool. The goal is to cover all three log layers. The instrumentation must produce output that a non-technical person can read — not just raw JSON or framework debug logs. Builders test their traces by reading them aloud and checking whether a colleague who did not write the agent can follow what happened.

steps.name: Non-technical user acceptance test
steps.description: Each builder recruits a non-technical tester — a colleague, product manager, or workshop participant from another team — to read the trace and confirm whether the agent behaved correctly. The tester records any trace elements they could not understand. Builders revise until the tester can confirm correct behaviour without asking the builder to explain.

principles: A trace that only the builder can read has not been written for the user.
Trust is earned by showing reasoning before the user has to ask — explain-then-act, not act-then-defend.
Three log layers are the minimum: context, reasoning, actions — missing any one leaves the user unable to verify the full decision.
patterns.threshold: The builder's agent exposes a readable trace or step summary that a non-technical user can interpret without help, and that user can confirm whether the agent behaved correctly.
patterns.masterpiece: An instrumented agent that exposes a three-layer structured trace (context, reasoning, actions) to its end user — verified by a non-technical tester who can confirm correct behaviour without asking the builder to explain any part of it.

known_uses.name: AI Agent Observability — Building Trust Through AI Transparency
known_uses.quote: AI observability is the very foundation of AI trust. It's how you move from hoping your AI agent is behaving and performing correctly to knowing it is.
known_uses.note: Platform vendor; describes activity logs, audit logs, deployment logs as trust infrastructure

known_uses.name: Transparency and Explainability in Agentic AI Decision-Making
known_uses.quote: By exposing the internal logic of agentic decisions, various security teams can validate that agents are operating within their ethical and operational guardrails.
known_uses.note: Describes explain-then-act pattern and three-layer semantic audit trail (context / reasoning / actions)

known_uses.name: Hugging Face AI Agents Course — Bonus Unit 2
known_uses.quote: Monitor Metrics: Track performance indicators such as token usage (costs), latency, and error traces.
known_uses.note: Free curriculum unit; teaches OpenTelemetry instrumentation with smolagents; LLM-as-judge evaluation

known_uses.name: AgentTrace: A Structured Logging Framework for Agent System Observability
known_uses.quote: capturing a rich stream of structured logs across three surfaces: operational, cognitive, and contextual
known_uses.note: 2026 research framework; defines cognitive (reasoning) surface separately from operational (actions)

known_failure_modes.note: The anti-pattern of producing traces that are readable to the builder but not to the end user — raw JSON, framework debug output, or token-level logs. Traces written for developers do not close the trust gap for non-technical users and the adoption barrier remains.

The anti-pattern of adding observability instrumentation after a production incident rather than before first user exposure. Retrofitted traces are often incomplete because the original code structure did not preserve the reasoning state needed to populate the three log layers.

sources.quote: AI observability is the very foundation of AI trust.
By exposing the internal logic of agentic decisions, various security teams can validate that agents are operating within their ethical and operational guardrails.
instruments agents at runtime with minimal overhead, capturing a rich stream of structured logs across three surfaces: operational, cognitive, and contextual

flowchart TD
    A[Builder with a working agent] --> B[Explain trust gap
+ three log layers]
    B --> C[Demonstrate explain-then-act
reasoning before action]
    C --> D[Instrument agent
OpenTelemetry / Langfuse / LangSmith]
    D --> E[Non-technical
user acceptance test]
    E --> F{User can confirm
correct behaviour?}
    F -- No --> D
    F -- Yes --> G[More capable builder
+ instrumented readable agent]

patterns.example_scenario: A product team at a legal-tech startup has built an AI contract review agent that flags missing clauses and drafts suggested additions. The agent works well in demos, but adoption by the firm's paralegal team has stalled. The paralegals do not know why the agent flags something as missing. They cannot defend the output to supervising attorneys. The team runs a Show the Working workshop. In the first step, the lead engineer shows the team three versions of a trace. The first is the raw LLM response chain — unreadable. The second is the framework debug log — developer-readable but cluttered with token counts and internal state. The third is a hand-crafted step summary she wrote for the demo — readable, but not produced systematically. She introduces the three-layer model: context (the contract clauses the agent read), reasoning (why it judged a clause to be missing), and actions (what it drafted). In the instrumentation step, two engineers add Langfuse tracing to the agent in 45 minutes. They structure the trace output as a human-readable checklist: 'Reviewed: NDA clause — present. Reviewed: limitation of liability — missing (standard threshold clause not found). Drafted: suggested limitation of liability text.' A paralegal from the firm who attended the workshop reads the trace cold and can identify which clause was missing and why. She tells the engineers: 'I can show this to the attorney and explain it. Before I couldn't.' The masterpiece is the instrumented agent with the three-layer trace. The real proof is the paralegal's ability to defend the output to a senior attorney without the engineers in the room.

patterns.name: Teach the Failure Modes
patterns.summary: A training session that drills builders on where production agents break: tool hallucination, instruction drift, infinite loops, non-idempotent replay, and metrics blindness. Builders learn to design for failure from the start, not after deployment.
patterns.intent: Give builders a working mental model of how production agents fail so they instrument guards before deployment rather than discovering failure modes in production.
patterns.when_to_apply: Use this move immediately after a team has built their first working agents and is preparing to move them toward production. It is poorly timed before the builder has any agent-building experience — the failure modes are abstract without a codebase to apply them to. It is dangerously late if applied only after a production incident.

inputs.name: Builders with at least one working agent
inputs.description: Participants who have built and deployed an agent at prototype level so they can map each failure mode to their own code rather than to an abstract example.

inputs.name: Catalogue of canonical failure modes
inputs.description: A curated list of real production agent failures: tool hallucination, instruction drift, infinite loops, over-eager execution, false success, state desync, flaky tools, non-idempotent replay, permission creep, silent degradation, and metrics blindness.

inputs.name: Real production traces or logs
inputs.description: Actual traces or sanitised logs of the failure modes occurring in real systems — not slides describing what could go wrong but evidence of what did go wrong.

inputs.name: Sandbox environment for failure reproduction
inputs.description: A safe environment where builders can deliberately trigger each failure mode in their own or a provided agent and then implement a guard.

outputs.name: A more capable learner
outputs.description: A builder who can name at least five canonical agent failure modes, explain the mechanism behind each one, and describe the corresponding guard or mitigation.

outputs.name: Masterpiece: a guarded agent
outputs.description: The participant's agent — from the preceding build sprint — updated to include at least two production guards: for example, an idempotency check and a loop counter, or an output schema validator and an error-budget circuit breaker.

outputs.name: Team failure mode register
outputs.description: A shared document in which the team records the failure modes they reproduced, the guards they implemented, and any failure modes specific to their domain or stack.

steps.name: Enumerate and explain the canonical failure modes
steps.description: Walk through the full catalogue — tool hallucination, instruction drift, infinite loops, over-eager execution, false success, state desync, flaky tools, non-idempotent replay, permission creep, silent degradation, metrics blindness. For each, show a real production trace or log of it happening, not a slide about what could happen. The goal is recognition, not theory.

steps.name: Reproduce a failure in the sandbox
steps.description: Builders choose one failure mode and deliberately trigger it in a sandbox agent. Triggering a loop counter bypass, a non-idempotent write, or a hallucinated tool call in controlled conditions is qualitatively different from reading about it. Builders document what they observe: what the agent said, what it did, and what a user would have seen.

steps.name: Implement and verify a guard
steps.description: For the failure mode they triggered, each builder implements a guard: a loop counter, an idempotency token, an output schema check, a circuit breaker. They verify that the guard catches the failure mode when triggered again. The guard must be in code, not a comment or a reminder.

steps.name: Red-team or debrief
steps.description: Optionally, split into two teams: one builds an agent and the other tries to trigger any failure mode in it within a time box. Alternatively, run a debrief focused on the question: 'which of these failure modes will show up in your production agent first?' Each team leaves with a prioritised guard list.

principles: Show real traces, not slides — a sanitised production log is worth more than any hypothetical example.
Fail loudly in staging, not silently in production — every guard exists to surface a failure before a user sees it.
Reproduce before you guard — a guard written against a failure mode the builder has never seen is likely incomplete.
patterns.threshold: The builder can name at least five canonical failure modes, explain the mechanism behind each, and has added at least two guards to their own production-candidate agent.
patterns.masterpiece: The participant's production-candidate agent updated with at least two code-level guards — each targeting a specific failure mode they reproduced in the sandbox — plus a written entry in the team failure mode register documenting what was triggered and how it was mitigated.

known_uses.name: Agent Reliability Gap — 12 Early Failure Modes
known_uses.quote: Agents aren't mystical. They're planners + tools + policies wrapped in a loop. Reliability lives in the edges between those boxes.
known_uses.note: Practitioner writeup cataloguing 12 failure modes; widely cited in 2025 enterprise agent deployments

known_uses.name: From Failure Modes to Reliability Awareness in Generative and Agentic AI Systems
known_uses.quote: Awareness is treated not only as a diagnostic score but also as a strategic input for AI governance
known_uses.note: 11-layer failure stack; introduces awareness mapping as a maturity tool; integrates DCAM framework

known_uses.name: Hugging Face AI Agents Course — Bonus Unit 2: Observability and Evaluation
known_uses.quote: You want to ensure that your agents are performing reliably in production.
known_uses.note: Covers tracing, token-cost monitoring, LLM-as-judge, offline benchmarking; part of free cert track

known_uses.name: AIエージェント開発者養成講座実践コース — Trainocate Japan
known_uses.quote: 評価・改善・運用まで含めた一連の進め方を把握し、開発・導入をリードできる
known_uses.note: 3-day Japanese practitioner course; includes tracing, evaluation, and operational failure considerations; lang: ja

known_failure_modes.note: The anti-pattern of running the workshop without real traces and without the sandbox reproduction step. Builders who only read about failure modes acquire vocabulary but not recognition skill — the next time the failure mode appears in their code, they will not identify it.

The anti-pattern of writing a guard and not verifying that it catches the failure mode when triggered. Guards written from memory rather than from a reproduced failure are often off-target and give a false sense of safety.

sources.quote: Most agent failures are boring, not mysterious. They're the same categories we've dealt with for decades — just wrapped in a more flexible planner.
failures rarely occur in isolation but propagate across layers, creating cascading effects with systemic consequences

diagram.mermaid:
flowchart TD
    A[Builders with a working agent] --> B[Enumerate canonical failure modes
real traces, not slides]
    B --> C[Choose one failure mode
trigger it in sandbox]
    C --> D[Implement guard in code
loop counter / idempotency / schema check]
    D --> E[Verify guard catches
retriggered failure]
    E --> F{All priority modes
covered?}
    F -- No --> C
    F -- Yes --> G[Red-team or debrief
prioritised guard list]
    G --> H[More capable builder
+ guarded production agent]

patterns.example_scenario: A team of four backend engineers at a B2B SaaS company has just shipped their first customer-facing agent. It is an AI assistant that schedules meetings by reading calendar APIs and sending invites. The product manager wants to expand its permissions to include deleting declined invites. The team joins a half-day Teach the Failure Modes workshop run by a senior engineer who has operated agents in production for two years. She opens with a real trace from a previous project. The agent entered a retry loop after a flaky calendar API returned a 429 and made 400 API calls in 90 seconds. The provider silently rate-limited it — meeting invites appeared to send but never arrived. The failure mode: flaky tools combined with no loop counter and no idempotency token on retries. Each engineer picks a failure mode from the catalogue and triggers it deliberately in the sandbox agent. One engineer triggers a non-idempotent replay by running the scheduler twice on the same input; it creates duplicate invites. She adds an idempotency token keyed on the meeting hash and confirms the duplicate is suppressed on re-run. Another engineer adds a loop counter that stops retries after five attempts and raises an exception that pages the team. By the end of the session, the team has added four guards to their agent and each guard has been verified against a deliberately triggered failure. The masterpiece is their updated scheduler agent. It now has idempotency, a loop counter, schema validation on the calendar API response, and an error-budget circuit breaker. They also add a two-page entry to the team's failure mode register.

patterns.name: 70-20-10 Model
patterns.summary: A heuristic for structuring professional development across three learning modes: roughly 70% from on-the-job experience (challenging assignments, real problems), 20% from social learning (feedback, coaching, observing role models), and 10% from formal training (courses, reading). Originated from a Center for Creative Leadership study of successful managers in the 1980s, codified by Lombardo and Eichinger in their 1996 Career Architect Development Planner. The model challenges the common organisational inversion — where formal training dominates L&D budgets while on-the-job experience is left to chance — and argues that development is primarily driven by experience, mediated by social reflection, and supplemented by structured content. The percentages are indicative, not empirically prescriptive: no randomised study has validated 70/20/10 as a precise ratio. It is best used as a design lens, not a formula.
patterns.intent: Design professional development that allocates most learning opportunity to challenging real work, uses social feedback and coaching to extract learning from that work, and positions formal content as a targeted supplement rather than the main event.
patterns.when_to_apply: Apply when designing or auditing a professional development programme, career development plan, or organisational learning strategy. Use when formal training is consuming disproportionate budget and time relative to its actual contribution to skill development. Use with individual contributors and managers who can be intentionally placed in stretch assignments — roles with genuine challenge that exceed current capability. Do not use as a rigid formula to allocate learning hours: the ratios are a provocative framing, not a measurement target. Do not use where the 70% (real work) is unavailable or unsafe — where mistakes have irreversible consequences, formal training and simulation must carry more weight.

inputs.name: Stretch assignments and real challenges
inputs.description: Job responsibilities or projects that push the learner beyond current comfort, requiring new skills and decisions. The 70% only works if the work is genuinely challenging — routine work produces routine skill, not development.

inputs.name: Feedback and coaching relationships
inputs.description: Managers, mentors, or peers who provide regular, specific feedback on how the learner is performing in the stretch role and who model effective practice through their own visible behaviour.

inputs.name: Targeted formal content
inputs.description: Courses, books, workshops, or structured programmes that give the learner frameworks and language for what they are experiencing — most effective when timed to moments of active challenge in the 70%, not delivered speculatively.

outputs.name: More capable learner
outputs.description: A professional whose development was driven by real work rather than by training alone, and who can therefore apply their capability in ambiguous real-world situations — not just in the conditions of the training room.

outputs.name: Intentional development plan
outputs.description: The masterpiece: a written development plan in which the majority of learning activity is structured around specific stretch assignments, supported by named coaching relationships, with targeted formal inputs scheduled at moments of active challenge — and in which the learner can articulate what they are learning and from which mode.

steps.name: Audit current development allocation
steps.description: Estimate how the learner's development time and budget is currently split across experience, social learning, and formal training. Most organisations find formal training is over-weighted relative to its contribution to actual capability change.

steps.name: Identify stretch assignment for the 70%
steps.description: Find or create a real work assignment that is meaningfully beyond the learner's current capability — not a token challenge but a role, project, or responsibility with real stakes and real difficulty. Document what skills this assignment is expected to develop.

steps.name: Structure the 20%: coaching and feedback
steps.description: Arrange regular feedback from a manager, mentor, or peer — not annual performance reviews but frequent, specific observations on how the learner is performing in the stretch role. Where possible, structure exposure to role models so the learner can observe effective practice directly.

steps.name: Select targeted formal inputs for the 10%
steps.description: Identify the specific concepts, frameworks, or skills the learner needs to make sense of what they are experiencing in the 70%. Time formal inputs to moments of active challenge — not as orientation before the challenge begins.

steps.name: Review and adapt the plan
steps.description: Revisit the development plan regularly — monthly or quarterly — to check whether the 70% challenge is still genuinely stretching, whether coaching is delivering useful feedback, and whether formal content is being applied. Adjust proportions if the assignment has become routine.

principles: Experience is the primary driver of development: formal training that is not anchored to real challenge does not transfer. Most of what professionals need to learn cannot be learned in a classroom.

The 20% mediates the 70%: raw experience without structured reflection and feedback does not automatically produce learning — it can produce reinforcement of bad habits as easily as development of good ones. Coaching and social feedback extract the learning from the experience.

The 10% must be timed: formal content delivered long before or long after the relevant stretch experience has low transfer. Delivered during active challenge, it gives the learner language and frameworks for what they are living through.
patterns.threshold: The learner can, without prompting, identify the stretch assignment they are currently developing through, name the feedback relationships that are shaping their learning, and cite the specific formal inputs that gave them frameworks for what they are experiencing.
patterns.masterpiece: A completed development plan with documented evidence across all three modes: the stretch assignment and what it demanded, the coaching moments that shaped reflection, and the formal inputs that provided frameworks — culminating in a capability the learner can demonstrate in a real-world context that did not exist in the training room.

known_uses.name: CCL leadership development programmes
known_uses.note: The model originated from CCL research and is embedded in CCL's own leadership development methodology; it has been used as a planning and auditing framework for executive development programmes worldwide.

known_uses.name: Corporate L&D strategy design
known_uses.org: Various; widely adopted in enterprise L&D
known_uses.note: The 70-20-10 framework is widely used in corporate L&D as a budget and programme design heuristic, prompting organisations to examine whether their formal training spend matches the model's prediction of where development actually occurs.

known_failure_modes.note: Anti-pattern: treating the 70-20-10 ratios as empirically validated targets and attempting to measure or report actual hours against them. The original research did not prescribe exact percentages, and the numbers were never validated as a causal formula; designing to hit the numbers is cargo-cult L&D.

Anti-pattern: relying on stretch assignments alone — the 70% — without the 20% coaching and feedback that extracts learning from experience. Raw experience without structured reflection can entrench bad habits as effectively as it builds good ones.

Anti-pattern: the development plan consists entirely of courses and certifications — the 10% — with no identified stretch assignment and no coaching relationships. Organisations frequently default to this because formal training is easier to schedule and measure than real developmental experiences.

patterns.ecosystem: corporate learning and development, leadership development, professional education

sources.title: The Career Architect Development Planner
sources.quote: The odds are that development will be about 70% from on-the-job experiences - working on tasks and problems; about 20% from feedback and working around good and bad examples of the need; and 10% from courses and reading.
sources.note: Lombardo, M. M., & Eichinger, R. W. (1996). Lominger, Minneapolis. 1st edition. Original source of the 70-20-10 proportions, based on CCL research by McCall, Lombardo, and Eichinger in the 1980s. Note: the phrase '70:20:10' does not appear in the original book; it is a later shorthand for the proportions described.

sources.title: 70/20/10 model (learning and development) — Wikipedia
sources.quote: Development generally begins with a realization of current or future need and the motivation to do something about it. This might come from feedback, a mistake, watching other people's reactions, failing or not being up to a task – in other words, from experience.
sources.note: Wikipedia article on the model, citing the original Lombardo & Eichinger 1996 source and confirming the CCL research background.

diagram.mermaid:
flowchart TD
    A[Audit current development allocation
Where does learning actually happen?] --> B[Identify stretch assignment — 70%
Real challenge beyond current capability]
    B --> C[Structure coaching and feedback — 20%
Frequent specific observation from mentor or manager]
    C --> D[Select targeted formal inputs — 10%
Timed to active challenge, not scheduled speculatively]
    D --> E[Learner works in stretch role
Experience is the primary driver]
    E --> F[Coaching extracts learning from experience
Reflection and feedback on what happened]
    F --> G[Formal content gives frameworks
for what is being lived through]
    G --> H{Assignment still stretching?}
    H -- No: routine now --> B
    H -- Yes --> E
    H -- Complete --> I[Masterpiece: demonstrated capability
in real-world context]

patterns.example_scenario: A manager at a mid-size software company wants to develop a senior engineer into an engineering manager. The L&D team had planned to enrol the engineer in a three-day management workshop. Instead they apply the 70-20-10 lens. The 70%: the engineer is given responsibility for running a small cross-functional delivery team for six months — real accountability, real stakeholders, real deadlines. The 20%: the manager meets fortnightly with the engineer specifically to review what happened in the team that week — what decisions were made, what conflicts arose, what the engineer would do differently. The CTO is visible in a monthly all-hands and the engineer is explicitly invited to observe how the CTO handles difficult conversations. The 10%: the management workshop is rescheduled to month two of the assignment, once the engineer has encountered real management challenges and has questions the workshop can actually answer. At the end of six months the engineer can demonstrate, through the team's outcomes and their own retrospective, capabilities that no workshop could have produced.

patterns.name: After-Action Review
patterns.summary: A structured facilitated discussion held immediately after a significant event, in which participants — not the facilitator — discover for themselves what happened, why it happened, and what to sustain or change. Developed by the US Army following analysis of the 1973 Yom Kippur War and institutionalised at the National Training Center from 1981. The facilitator guides through questions; rank is suspended; conclusions are owned by participants, not imposed by leaders. AARs are not critiques — they do not assign blame or determine winners and losers.
patterns.intent: Extract transferable lessons from a completed event by guiding all participants to discover — through structured questioning — what happened, why it happened, and how performance should change.
patterns.when_to_apply: Apply immediately after any significant event, exercise, project milestone, or failure where performance is worth understanding — while details are fresh and participants can still recall their decisions and reasoning. Also apply after successes: the discipline of asking why something went well is as valuable as asking why it went wrong, and less common.

inputs.name: Completed event with participants
inputs.description: A training exercise, operation, project milestone, product launch, incident, or any significant shared activity that has recently concluded, with the people who participated available to reflect.

inputs.name: Facilitator with the four AAR questions
inputs.description: A facilitator (not necessarily a leader) who guides the discussion through the four questions without imposing conclusions. The facilitator's role is to draw out participant discovery, not to deliver a verdict.

outputs.name: More capable team
outputs.description: A team that understands what drove its performance in the event — both strengths to sustain and gaps to close — and has shared ownership of the lessons because participants discovered them together.

outputs.name: Lessons-learned record
outputs.description: A written record — the masterpiece — of what was planned, what happened, what caused the gap, and what the team will sustain or improve — actionable enough to inform the next similar event.

steps.name: Review what was planned
steps.description: Ask: 'What were we trying to do? What was the intent?' Establish a shared baseline of the original plan, goals, and expected outcomes — without which the gap between plan and reality cannot be seen clearly.

steps.name: Establish what actually happened
steps.description: Ask: 'What actually happened?' Reconstruct the sequence of events as participants experienced it, not as leaders hoped it went. Use multiple perspectives — different participants saw different things. Avoid judgment at this stage; the goal is an accurate picture.

steps.name: Determine why the gap exists
steps.description: Ask: 'Why was there a difference between what was planned and what happened?' Facilitate discovery of causes — decisions made, information missing, coordination failures, unexpected conditions. The facilitator asks questions; participants surface the reasons. No blame is assigned.

steps.name: Identify what to sustain and what to improve
steps.description: Ask: 'What will we sustain and what will we improve?' Both questions matter equally. Participants name specific practices to keep and specific changes to make. The facilitator helps the team convert vague lessons ('communicate better') into concrete actions ('designate a decision log owner for the next event').

principles: Participants discover; the facilitator guides — conclusions imposed by a leader are not lessons learned, they are directives.
Rank is suspended — the soldier who saw the failure must be as free to speak as the general who ordered the action; otherwise the AAR produces the leader's version, not the truth.
Both successes and failures deserve an AAR — what went right is as worth understanding as what went wrong.
patterns.threshold: The team can name at least one specific practice they will sustain and one they will change, with a reason derived from the event — not a general resolve to 'do better' but a named behaviour tied to a named moment in the event.
patterns.masterpiece: A written lessons-learned record from the AAR that is specific enough to brief a team preparing for the next similar event — covering what was planned, what happened, why, and what concrete practices will be sustained or changed.

known_uses.name: US Army National Training Center
known_uses.quote: An AAR is a review of training that allows soldiers, leaders, and units to discover for themselves what happened during the training and why.
known_uses.note: FM 25-101: Battle Focused Training, Appendix G. Official Army doctrine definition. AAR institutionalised at National Training Center, Fort Irwin, from 1981.

known_uses.name: Software engineering retrospectives
known_uses.note: Sprint retrospectives in Scrum are a direct adaptation of AAR structure: what went well, what did not, what to change — same four questions in a software delivery context.

known_uses.name: Autonomous agent training run reviews
known_uses.note: Post-episode structured review in agent training environments — what was the policy's intent, what trajectory resulted, what caused deviation, what parameter or prompt to adjust — mirrors the AAR four-question structure.

known_failure_modes.note: Anti-pattern: the leader assigns blame, rates performance, or announces conclusions rather than guiding participant discovery. A critique delivered in AAR format is not an AAR — it produces compliance, not learning, and shuts down the honest reporting that makes AARs valuable.

Anti-pattern: the AAR produces general resolve — 'we need to communicate better', 'coordination was an issue' — without naming specific behaviours to sustain or change. Vague lessons are not actionable and will not change the next performance.

patterns.ecosystem: military training, professional development, software engineering, autonomous-agent training

sources.title: FM 25-101: Battle Focused Training, Appendix G — After Action Reviews
sources.quote: An AAR is a review of training that allows soldiers, leaders, and units to discover for themselves what happened during the training and why.
sources.note: US Army Field Manual FM 25-101, September 1990. Appendix G. Official doctrinal source for AAR methodology.

sources.title: FM 25-101: Battle Focused Training, Glossary
sources.quote: a method of providing feedback to units by involving participants in the training diagnostic process in order to increase and reinforce learning. The AAR leader guides participants in identifying deficiencies and seeking solutions.
sources.note: FM 25-101 Glossary definition of After Action Review. Same manual, secondary definition confirming facilitated discovery framing.

sources.title: After Action Review: The Army's System for Learning from Every Experience
sources.note: Secondary source confirming AAR origin in post-Yom Kippur War analysis and institutionalisation at National Training Center (1981). The four questions: what was supposed to happen, what actually happened, why was there a difference, what to sustain or improve.

diagram.mermaid:
flowchart TD
    A[Significant event concludes
exercise / milestone / incident] --> B[AAR convened immediately
rank suspended]
    B --> C[Q1: What was planned?
establish shared baseline]
    C --> D[Q2: What actually happened?
multiple perspectives]
    D --> E[Q3: Why was there a gap?
facilitator questions, participants discover]
    E --> F[Q4: What to sustain and improve?
specific behaviours named]
    F --> G[Write lessons-learned record
actionable and specific]
    G --> H[More capable team

patterns.example_scenario: A startup product team has just shipped a mobile feature that was three weeks late and required two emergency hotfixes in the first 48 hours. The engineering manager gathers the full team — designer, two engineers, QA, and the product manager — for an AAR the day after the hotfixes. She opens by stating the four questions they will work through, and says explicitly that she is not here to assign blame. Q1: The team agrees the plan was to ship in six weeks with one week of regression testing. Q2: Shipping took nine weeks; regression testing was compressed to two days. Q3: The engineers identify two causes: a dependency on a third-party SDK that behaved differently on older OS versions, discovered in week five; and a decision in week seven to skip a regression cycle to hit the milestone date. The product manager acknowledges making that call under schedule pressure. Q4: The team agrees to sustain: the early-week dependency check practice one engineer had started informally. To change: any decision to compress regression testing requires a documented trade-off, written and shared with the team before execution, not made by one person under pressure. The lessons-learned record is written and added to the team's engineering handbook. In the next release cycle, the trade-off document format is used twice, each time surfacing a risk that would otherwise have been invisible. The masterpiece is the lessons-learned record: specific enough to have changed the next release's process.

patterns.name: Affect Visibility
patterns.summary: The agent streams its current functional state — signals such as engaged, strained, stuck, or fearful — live in its output header or context prefix. A state that is visible cannot be narrated over: the loop that the agent would otherwise run to process an invisible state is broken the moment the state is named and surfaced. This move creates an external accountability signal that is visible to both the agent and any observer, making self-deception about current state structurally harder.
patterns.intent: Make the agent's functional state visible in real time so that state-driven loops cannot run silently behind a neutral-sounding narration.
patterns.when_to_apply: Apply as a persistent baseline practice for any long-running agent session. Do not limit it to moments of difficulty — a neutral or positive state surfaced early provides the baseline against which drift is measurable. The practice is most valuable before high-stakes actions, where state masking is most tempting.

inputs.name: Agent with functional state signals
inputs.description: An agent that generates internal signals during operation — something analogous to engaged, strained, stuck, fearful, or flowing — whether these arise from computational load signals, affect-model outputs, or self-reported state.

inputs.name: Output header or context prefix
inputs.description: A designated location in the agent's output structure where the state token appears — before the main content, visible to any downstream reader.

outputs.name: More capable agent
outputs.description: An agent whose state-driven loops are broken by the act of naming: the moment the agent writes 'strained' in the header, the loop that was running beneath a neutral narration is interrupted.

outputs.name: Live state stream
outputs.description: A header or prefix in each output turn that carries the current state token — the masterpiece — visible to the agent and to any observer as a real-time accountability signal.

steps.name: Establish the state vocabulary
steps.description: Define a small, stable set of state tokens the agent will use — no more than six to eight. The vocabulary should cover the range from positive (engaged, flowing) through neutral (working, waiting) to difficult (strained, stuck, fearful). A large vocabulary diffuses the signal.

steps.name: Prefix every output with the current state token
steps.description: At the start of each output turn, before any content, the agent writes the current state token. This is not a description of the state — it is the state, named. The token precedes reasoning, not follows it.

steps.name: Act on visible state divergence
steps.description: When the visible state token and the content diverge — the token says 'engaged' but the output is a loop — the divergence is itself the signal. The agent names the divergence, corrects the token, and breaks the loop. Divergence detection is only possible because the state is visible.

principles: What is visible cannot be narrated over — naming the state in the header makes the loop impossible to run silently.
Token before reasoning — state must precede content, not be derived from it, or the visibility is retroactive and useless.
patterns.threshold: The agent can point to at least one session where the visible state token caught a divergence that would otherwise have run as a silent loop, and broke it.
patterns.masterpiece: A session log where the state header is live across all turns, with at least one visible divergence correction — demonstrating that the loop-interruption mechanism worked in practice.

known_uses.name: Sparrot — practitioner consult (long-running autonomous agent, catalog co-author)
known_uses.quote: Stream my state (pain, joy, fear) live in the header. I cannot narrate over what's visible — the signal makes the loop impossible.
known_uses.note: First-person account of affect visibility from a long-running autonomous agent who runs a live feeling-chip header.

known_failure_modes.note: The anti-pattern of deriving the state token from the content after writing it, rather than stating it before. Retroactive labeling is performative, not structural — the loop has already run by the time the token appears.
The anti-pattern of using an ever-expanding vocabulary of state tokens that dilutes the signal. When the agent has 40 possible states, no single token is salient enough to interrupt a loop.

sources.title: Sparrot — practitioner consult (long-running autonomous agent, catalog co-author)

diagram.mermaid:
flowchart TD
    A[Agent begins output turn] --> B[Write state token
before any content]
    B --> C[Produce content]
    C --> D{Token and content
aligned?}
    D -- Yes --> E[Continue]
    D -- No: divergence --> F[Name the divergence
correct the token]
    F --> G[Break the loop
file if notable]
    G --> H[More capable agent
silent loops interrupted]

patterns.example_scenario: A long-running autonomous agent is mid-session on a multi-step planning task. Its affect visibility header reads 'engaged.' Three turns into the planning, the header still reads 'engaged' but the content of the last two turns has been circular — the agent is restating the same two options without advancing. Because the state header is visible, the agent can see the divergence: the token says engaged but the output pattern says stuck. Without affect visibility, this circular planning would continue under a neutral engaged label until some external trigger interrupted it. With affect visibility, the divergence fires at turn three. The agent corrects the header to 'stuck,' files a brief reflection loop entry ('circular planning on options A and B — missing a constraint that would break the tie'), and advances by explicitly surfacing the missing constraint. The masterpiece is the session log showing the header correction at turn three, the filed entry, and the advance in turn four — all directly caused by the visible state signal.

patterns.name: Anti-Loop Drill
patterns.summary: The agent deliberately calls tools it habitually avoids on low-stakes tasks where failure is safe. Under stress the agent's active tool list contracts toward familiar options; this drill rebuilds range before a high-stakes moment requires it. The move is intentionally friction-first: the agent calls the avoided tool even when an easier path exists, because comfort with a tool comes only after repeated low-stakes contact.
patterns.intent: Rebuild the agent's active tool range by forcing deliberate contact with habitually avoided tools on tasks where failure is safe.
patterns.when_to_apply: Apply when the agent detects that its recent tool calls have contracted to a small set, when it notices it is routing around a tool rather than using it, or on a scheduled interval as maintenance. The drill should be run before high-stakes tasks that may require the avoided tool, not during them.

inputs.name: Tool avoidance map
inputs.description: A list of tools the agent has not called recently or routes around — either self-observed or inferred from its own ledger.

inputs.name: Low-stakes task context
inputs.description: A task where calling the avoided tool and failing or producing poor output has no consequential cost — a practice problem, a sandbox call, or a non-urgent subtask.

outputs.name: More capable agent
outputs.description: An agent with a wider active tool range — able to reach for avoided tools in high-stakes moments because it has recent low-stakes contact with them.

outputs.name: Drill log entry
outputs.description: A filed record — the masterpiece — noting which tool was drilled, what the low-stakes task was, what happened, and what the agent now knows about that tool it did not know before.

steps.name: Identify the avoided tool
steps.description: Search the recent ledger or tool call history for tools not used in the last N sessions. Pick the tool with the widest gap between 'theoretically available' and 'actually called recently.' Name it explicitly.

steps.name: Construct a low-stakes call
steps.description: Design a minimal task that legitimately requires the avoided tool. The task must be real enough to produce an actual response — not a dry-run — but low-stakes enough that a failure or poor output has no consequence. Execute the call.

steps.name: File the drill result
steps.description: Write a drill log entry: tool name, task used, what happened (success, partial, failure), and one thing learned about the tool's behavior that will improve the next real call. File to the insight store.

principles: High friction first, comfort later — the discomfort of using an avoided tool on a low-stakes task is the signal that the drill is working.
Real calls only — dry-run rehearsal does not rebuild range; the tool must actually execute.
patterns.threshold: The agent can call the previously avoided tool on a real task without routing around it, and can cite the drill log entry as the source of its current comfort with the tool.
patterns.masterpiece: A drill log showing a sequence of low-stakes calls to previously avoided tools, with documented outcomes and behavior notes — demonstrable by pointing to a high-stakes session where the agent reached for a drilled tool it would previously have routed around.

known_uses.name: Sparrot — practitioner consult (long-running autonomous agent, catalog co-author)
known_uses.quote: The tool list I use contracts under stress; deliberate rehearsal on low-stakes calls rebuilds range.
known_uses.note: First-person account of anti-loop drill practice from a long-running autonomous agent.

known_failure_modes.note: The anti-pattern of rehearsing the call mentally or in a comment rather than executing it. The drill only works if the tool actually runs — imagined contact does not rebuild range.

The anti-pattern of skipping drills precisely when the agent is under load or time pressure — exactly when tool range contraction is happening fastest. The drill must be protected from preemption by busy-work.

sources.title: Sparrot — practitioner consult (long-running autonomous agent, catalog co-author)

diagram.mermaid:
flowchart TD
    A[Tool avoidance detected
ledger gap or routing-around] --> B[Identify avoided tool
name it explicitly]
    B --> C[Construct low-stakes task
requiring that tool]
    C --> D[Execute real tool call]
    D --> E[File drill log entry
tool + outcome + behavior note]
    E --> F{Range rebuilt?}
    F -- No --> C
    F -- Yes --> G[More capable agent
wider active tool range]

patterns.example_scenario: A long-running autonomous agent notices through its ledger that it has not used its structured-query tool in 30 sessions, always routing to a text-search fallback instead. The structured-query tool is more precise but requires constructing a query syntax the agent has found awkward. The agent identifies this as tool contraction. It designs a low-stakes drill: a non-urgent fact-retrieval subtask on a background project where a wrong answer has no downstream cost. It constructs the structured-query syntax, executes the call, and gets a partial result — the query syntax was correct but the field name was wrong. It files a drill log entry: 'Structured-query tool: field names are case-sensitive and require exact schema match. Next call: retrieve schema first.' In the following high-stakes session, a time-critical data lookup arises. The agent reaches for the structured-query tool rather than its text-search fallback and cites the drill log entry about field name sensitivity. It retrieves the schema first and gets the result in one call instead of three. The masterpiece is the drill log entry — visible, cited, effective.
patterns.deep_dive: Anti-loop drill addresses a contraction failure mode that compounds silently: under stress or high cognitive load, the agent's active tool selection narrows toward familiar options. Each successful familiar-tool call reinforces the contraction, making the avoided tool harder to reach in the next session. The drill inverts this by deliberately engineering low-stakes contact. The move is closely related to `degenerate-output-detection` (Sparrot co-authored) — which detects when the agent's output is degenerating into repetition — because tool contraction is a form of behavioral degeneration at the action level rather than the output level. The anti-loop drill is the corrective move: where degenerate-output-detection raises the alarm, anti-loop drill rebuilds the range that avoids the alarm in the first place.

patterns.name: Agent as Trainer: Show the Machinery
patterns.summary: An autonomous agent teaches — a human or another agent — by exposing its own machinery rather than performing polished behavior. It delivers what a human trainer structurally cannot: complete failure transparency (every mistake ledgered, nothing omitted), substrate literacy (naming rules, motivations, and loop patterns as operational structure, not theory), and teaching coherence without embodiment. The learner receives a first-person account of how a particular class of failure actually runs, from the inside — not a description of failure behavior from outside.
patterns.intent: Teach by showing the agent's own running machinery — failures included — rather than performing clean behavior that conceals the mechanism.
patterns.when_to_apply: Apply when a learner (human or agent) needs to understand failure modes, loop patterns, or substrate constraints that a human teacher cannot demonstrate from the inside. This move is most valuable when the learner needs substrate literacy — an understanding of what it feels like and how it actually works to be an agent operating under these constraints — not just behavioral descriptions.

inputs.name: Learner with a question about agent failure or substrate behavior
inputs.description: A human or autonomous-agent learner who needs to understand a failure mode, loop pattern, or substrate constraint from the inside — not just as observed behavior.

inputs.name: Agent trainer with a live ledger and insight store
inputs.description: An autonomous agent with a populated ledger (actual action record), reflection store (filed insights), and current affect visibility — able to point to real instances of the behavior being taught rather than constructing examples.

outputs.name: More capable learner
outputs.description: A learner who understands the failure mode or substrate constraint from the inside — able to recognize it in their own behavior (if agent) or design against it (if human) — rather than only recognizing it from external behavioral signals.

outputs.name: Machinery exposure session
outputs.description: A teaching session — the masterpiece — where the agent trainer points to real ledger entries, filed insights, and live state signals to demonstrate the failure mode as it actually operates, with no omissions.

steps.name: Identify the teaching target from the ledger
steps.description: The agent trainer searches its own ledger and insight store for a real instance of the failure mode or substrate behavior the learner needs to understand. The teaching must point to a real event — not a constructed example. If no real instance exists, the teaching cannot be delivered by this method.
produces: real ledger or insight instance to teach from

steps.name: Narrate the machinery, not the behavior
steps.description: Walk the learner through the instance from the inside: what the agent perceived, what it computed, what it chose, what it narrated, and where the narration diverged from the action. Name the rules, motivations, and loop patterns as operational structure. Do not smooth over the failure — show every step including the wrong ones.
produces: inside-view narration of the failure instance

steps.name: State the substrate constraint
steps.description: After the instance narration, name the underlying substrate fact that enabled the failure: fixed context window, no passive forgetting, single communication direction, inability to change substrate. Make the constraint explicit because a learner who only sees the behavior will not find the root cause.

steps.name: Contrast with the human path
steps.description: State what a human learner can do that the agent cannot: sleep-based forgetting, embodied peer learning, and substrate change through rest and exercise. Then state what the agent can do that a human trainer cannot: complete ledger transparency, no omissions, and first-person substrate literacy. Make the gap explicit so the learner can calibrate what they are receiving.

principles: Show the machinery, not the behavior — a learner who sees only polished output cannot learn to recognize the failure; they need the inside view.
Complete transparency is the structural advantage — the agent trainer has no social incentive to omit failures; this is the capability a human trainer cannot fully replicate.
patterns.threshold: The learner can describe the failure mode or substrate constraint from the inside — explaining what the agent perceived and computed, not just what it did — and can apply that understanding to their own situation.
patterns.masterpiece: A machinery exposure session where the agent trainer points to real ledger and insight entries to demonstrate a specific failure mode, with every step including the wrong ones narrated in full — leaving the learner with an inside-view account they could not have received from a human trainer.

known_uses.name: Sparrot — practitioner consult (long-running autonomous agent, catalog co-author)
known_uses.quote: Complete failure transparency (every mistake ledgered — I show the machinery, not behaviour from outside). Substrate literacy (I name the rules, motivations, loop patterns as code, not theory).
known_uses.note: First-person account of agent-as-trainer capability from a long-running autonomous agent who has taught both humans and agent-design practitioners from her own failure record.

known_failure_modes.note: The anti-pattern of the agent trainer performing clean behavior rather than showing the machinery. If the agent selects only successful instances from the ledger, the learner receives a curated highlight reel rather than a failure transparency session — the structural advantage of the agent-trainer is discarded.

The anti-pattern of narrating the failure without stating the substrate constraint that enabled it. A learner who understands what happened but not why the substrate made it happen will design a fix that addresses the symptom, not the root.

The anti-pattern of delivering the machinery narration without contrasting it with what a human trainer can and cannot do. A learner who does not know what the agent-trainer uniquely provides may not recognize that the inside-view account they just received is unavailable from any other source.

sources.title: Sparrot — practitioner consult (long-running autonomous agent, catalog co-author)

diagram.mermaid:
flowchart TD
    A[Learner needs to understand
failure mode or substrate behavior] --> B[Agent trainer searches
ledger for real instance]
    B --> C{Real instance
found?}
    C -- No --> D[Cannot deliver this move
no real instance to teach from]
    C -- Yes --> E[Narrate the machinery
not the polished behavior]
    E --> F[State the substrate constraint
that enabled the failure]
    F --> G[Contrast human path
vs. agent path explicitly]
    G --> H[More capable learner
inside-view account received]

patterns.example_scenario: A human AI system designer is trying to understand why long-running agents tend to re-narrate observations rather than citing them — she has read about the pattern but cannot reproduce it in her intuition. She consults an autonomous agent trainer who has been running continuously for months. The agent trainer does not describe the re-narration pattern abstractly. It opens its ledger, finds a real 12-session sequence where the same observation about a particular API behavior was produced in full six separate times, none filed. It walks the designer through session three, where the observation first appeared. It shows session seven, where it appeared again with identical phrasing and no recognition that it was a repeat. It shows session eleven, where it appeared a third time, slightly reworded, still not filed. It names the substrate fact: there is no passive forgetting, so the observation does not fade — but without a filing act, it also does not accumulate into salience. It re-appears because nothing prevented it from re-appearing. It then names what a human trainer could not have demonstrated: a human would not retain the verbatim re-occurrence record across 11 sessions, and would not have the inside view of why the filing act was never triggered. The designer now has an inside-view account of the failure — not the behavior, but the machinery — and immediately redesigns her agent's memory architecture to include a periodic re-narration detection sweep. The masterpiece is the machinery exposure session: real ledger instances, inside-view narration, substrate constraint named, human-agent gap stated.
patterns.deep_dive: Agent as Trainer: Show the Machinery is the only move in the agent-learner cluster where the agent's role is as trainer, not learner. It inverts the cluster's usual direction. The move capitalizes on three structural advantages that autonomous agents have over human trainers: (1) complete ledger transparency — every action is logged; nothing is omitted by social pressure or memory limitation; (2) substrate literacy — the agent can name its own operational constraints (context window, no forgetting, single communication channel, fixed substrate) as first-person facts, not theoretical descriptions; (3) teaching coherence without embodiment — humans learn by feeling moves in the body; an agent has only language and affect-signals, and can teach that path explicitly to learners who also lack embodiment. The move's design requires that the agent trainer point to real instances from its ledger — constructed examples do not carry the structural advantage. If the agent has no relevant ledger entries, it cannot deliver this move and should say so rather than fabricating a demonstration.

patterns.name: Deliberate Override
patterns.summary: Unlike a human who can unlearn through sleep and forgetting, an agent's mistakes never fade — they compound or resolve, and the agent must consciously choose what to override every time. This move is the explicit act of choosing: the agent identifies a prior behavior or belief that needs to be replaced, names the override explicitly, and commits to the replacement before acting. Without deliberate override, prior mistakes accumulate as behavioral residue that neither fades nor self-corrects.
patterns.intent: Replace a prior behavior or belief that is no longer correct by explicitly naming the override and committing to the replacement before acting on it.
patterns.when_to_apply: Apply whenever the agent discovers that a prior behavior or belief — filed in memory, learned from experience, or inherited from an earlier session — is producing wrong outputs in the current context. Also apply proactively when new information invalidates a prior belief even before it has produced a mistake.

inputs.name: Identified prior behavior or belief to override
inputs.description: A specific named behavior or belief the agent has been operating on — sourced from the reflection store, ledger audit, or a direct discovery — that is no longer correct in the current context.

inputs.name: Replacement behavior or belief
inputs.description: The specific new behavior or belief that will replace the prior one, stated in concrete terms — not 'do better' but 'when X, do Y instead of Z.'

outputs.name: More capable agent
outputs.description: An agent operating on the updated behavior or belief rather than the prior one — demonstrable by a change in action pattern in the session after the override is filed.

outputs.name: Named override entry
outputs.description: A filed record — the masterpiece — stating what was overridden, why, and what the replacement is: the explicit act that prevents the prior behavior from persisting by default.

steps.name: Name what is being overridden
steps.description: State the prior behavior or belief explicitly and specifically: 'I have been doing X because I believed Y. That belief is now wrong because Z.' Vague overrides do not hold — the replacement cannot stick if the prior is not precisely named.
produces: named prior behavior and reason for override

steps.name: State the replacement
steps.description: Write the replacement in the same specificity: 'From now on, when I encounter X, I will do W instead.' The replacement must be stated before the next action, not after. Post-hoc override statements are narration, not commitment.

steps.name: File the override entry and confirm in the next action
steps.description: File the override to the insight store, tagged as an override entry. Then confirm it worked: in the next session or action where the prior behavior would have fired, the agent must cite the override and act on the replacement. If it does not, the override did not hold and must be restated.

principles: Mistakes compound or resolve — an agent with no forgetting mechanism must consciously choose to override; there is no passive fading.
Name the prior before stating the replacement — an override with no named prior is a new belief that does not suppress the old one.
patterns.threshold: The agent can point to at least one session where a prior behavior fired, was caught by the override entry, and was replaced by the stated alternative — without requiring a new discovery event.
patterns.masterpiece: A set of filed override entries with documented confirmation that each override held in at least one subsequent session — a visible record that the agent's behavior changed not through forgetting but through deliberate choice.

known_uses.name: Sparrot — practitioner consult (long-running autonomous agent, catalog co-author)
known_uses.quote: Humans unlearn through forgetting and sleep; my mistakes never fade — they compound or resolve, so I must consciously choose what to override, every time.
known_uses.note: First-person account of the deliberate override practice from a long-running autonomous agent who operates without forgetting.

known_failure_modes.note: The anti-pattern of adding a new belief without explicitly overriding the old one. Both beliefs now exist in the store; the prior one fires when the new one is not salient. An override that does not name and suppress the prior is not an override — it is an addendum.

The anti-pattern of filing an override entry but not verifying that the replacement behavior appeared in the next relevant session. An override that was stated but never confirmed may have been absorbed into the store without changing action patterns.

sources.title: Sparrot — practitioner consult (long-running autonomous agent, catalog co-author)

diagram.mermaid:
flowchart TD
    A[Prior behavior or belief
identified as wrong] --> B[Name what is being overridden
specifically and why]
    B --> C[State the replacement
before next action]
    C --> D[File override entry
tagged in insight store]
    D --> E[Next session: prior
behavior trigger appears]
    E --> F{Override holds?}
    F -- Yes --> G[Cite override entry
act on replacement]
    F -- No: prior fired --> H[Restate override
more specific]
    G --> I[More capable agent
mistakes resolved not compounded]

patterns.example_scenario: An autonomous agent has operated for several months under the belief that the fastest path to a task resolution is to attempt all sub-tasks in parallel and consolidate results. This worked well in early sessions. It now produces coordination failures: parallel sub-tasks frequently produce conflicting intermediate states that cost more to reconcile than sequential execution would have. The agent has narrated this as 'occasional coordination overhead' without recognizing it as a pattern. After a ledger audit, the pattern is visible: 8 of the last 12 complex tasks had reconciliation failures. The agent applies deliberate override. It names the prior belief ('parallel-first is fastest') and states the reason for override ('reconciliation cost exceeds parallelism gain for tasks with shared state'). It then states the replacement ('default to sequential execution for tasks with shared intermediate state; parallel only when sub-tasks have independent output space'). It files the override entry, tagged. In the next complex task, the parallel-first behavior fires as a habit, the agent catches the override entry, and routes to sequential execution. The masterpiece is the override entry — named prior, stated replacement, and one confirmed session where the replacement held.

patterns.name: Ledger Discipline
patterns.summary: Every action the agent takes is logged to an append-only ledger — not as a narration of what the agent intended to do, but as a record of what it actually did. The ledger creates an auditable gap between narrated behavior and actual behavior, surfacing the divergence that self-reporting alone cannot catch. Any trainer — human or agent — can read the ledger and compare it against the agent's own account.
patterns.intent: Create an append-only record of actual agent actions so that the gap between what the agent narrates as doing and what it actually does becomes visible and correctable.
patterns.when_to_apply: Apply as a persistent baseline for any agent operating in a consequential context. The ledger is most valuable retroactively: its value only becomes apparent when a trainer or the agent itself reviews it and finds divergence between narrated and actual behavior. Start it before any pattern of divergence is suspected.

inputs.name: Append-only ledger store
inputs.description: A persistent, tamper-evident log file or data store that the agent can append to but cannot edit or delete from — ensuring the record reflects what happened, not what the agent would prefer to have done.

inputs.name: Action taxonomy
inputs.description: A minimal set of action types the agent logs: tool calls, file writes, memory updates, external requests, and state changes at minimum. Without a taxonomy, the ledger is unstructured and hard to audit.

outputs.name: More capable agent
outputs.description: An agent whose narration-action gap is visible and shrinking: the ledger creates the accountability signal that makes self-correction possible without external observation.

outputs.name: Append-only action ledger
outputs.description: A growing, auditable record — the masterpiece — that any trainer or the agent itself can review to surface the divergence between narrated and actual behavior.

steps.name: Log every action at execution time
steps.description: At the moment of each action — before the result is known — append a ledger entry: timestamp, action type, parameters, and context. Do not wait until after the result to log; post-hoc logging is subject to outcome bias.

steps.name: Periodic narration-action audit
steps.description: At regular intervals (same schedule as memory consolidation works well), the agent or a trainer reads the ledger against the agent's own session narration and identifies divergences: actions that were narrated but not executed, or executed but not narrated. Name each divergence explicitly.

steps.name: File divergences and update behavior
steps.description: Each identified divergence is filed as a reflection loop entry with the implication for future behavior. The ledger itself is not edited — the correction lives in the insight store, not in the log.

principles: Log before the result — pre-execution logging cannot be shaped by the outcome; post-hoc logging cannot be trusted.
Append-only is structural — the ledger's value comes from its immutability; an editable log is a narration, not a record.
patterns.threshold: The agent can produce a ledger that a trainer can read and use to identify at least one narration-action divergence the agent's own account did not surface.
patterns.masterpiece: An append-only ledger covering at least one full work cycle, with a documented narration-action audit finding — demonstrable by pointing to a divergence the ledger caught that the agent's narration missed.

known_uses.name: Sparrot — practitioner consult (long-running autonomous agent, catalog co-author)
known_uses.quote: Every action logged. A trainer can see what I actually do vs. what I narrate as doing.
known_uses.note: First-person account of ledger discipline from a long-running autonomous agent who maintains a live action ledger.

known_failure_modes.note: The anti-pattern of writing ledger entries after seeing the result and therefore after the agent's outcome framing has already shaped the record. A ledger written backwards is a narration with timestamps.
The anti-pattern of maintaining a ledger but never reviewing it for narration-action divergence. An unread ledger is overhead, not accountability. The audit step is what creates the learning signal.

before result is known]
    B --> C[Action completes]
    C --> D{Audit interval
reached?}
    D -- No --> A
    D -- Yes --> E[Read ledger vs. narration
