steps.description: The newcomer takes on progressively more central tasks as competence develops, observing more experienced members in the process. The learning is embedded in the doing, not separated from it as a training phase.

steps.name: Develop the shared repertoire
steps.description: The community collectively develops and maintains its shared repertoire — the vocabulary, tools, approaches, and stories that encode the community's practical knowledge. Newcomers acquire this repertoire through participation, not instruction. They start by using the vocabulary before they fully understand it and develop understanding through use.

steps.name: Become a more central participant over time
steps.description: As a learner's competence grows, they move from periphery to core — eventually becoming one of the practitioners who models and narrates good work for the next generation of newcomers. The community reproduces its competence through this continuous cycle, not through a fixed curriculum.

principles: Learning is becoming, not receiving — competence is measured by growing participation in the community, not by absorbing content. The community is the curriculum.
Peripheral participation must be legitimate — newcomers must do real work, not practice problems. The 'peripheral' refers to the task's centrality, not its authenticity.
patterns.threshold: A newcomer can complete a real community task and have the output recognized as competent by more experienced community members — without requiring remediation or special accommodation.
patterns.masterpiece: A community whose shared repertoire — vocabulary, tools, approaches, canonical stories — is actively used and evolved by its members, and through which newcomers demonstrably develop competence faster than they would in isolation or formal training alone.

known_uses.name: Lave and Wenger — Situated Learning (1991)
known_uses.org: University of California, Berkeley; University of California, Irvine
known_uses.quote: Learning viewed as situated activity has as its central defining characteristic a process that we call legitimate peripheral participation.
known_uses.note: Lave and Wenger observed apprenticeship learning in multiple craft communities (Vai and Gola tailors, naval quartermasters, meat cutters, midwives, AA members) and found that learning was embedded in participation rather than in formal instruction.

known_uses.name: Wenger — Communities of Practice (1998)
known_uses.quote: Communities of practice are groups of people who share a concern or a passion for something they do and learn how to do it better as they interact regularly.
known_uses.note: Wenger's 1998 book Communities of Practice: Learning, Meaning, and Identity formalized the three-element model (domain, community, practice) and extended the concept from craft apprenticeship to organizational and professional settings.

known_failure_modes.note: The anti-pattern of forming a group with organizational mandate but no genuine shared domain — members attend meetings without caring about advancing a common practice. Without real domain stakes, the shared repertoire never develops and newcomers have nothing to participate toward.

The anti-pattern of assigning newcomers practice problems rather than real peripheral tasks. Practice problems do not produce the same learning as real work because they carry no community stakes — the newcomer is not yet participating, they are preparing to participate, which is a structurally different experience.

The anti-pattern of a community that is excellent at producing work but has no legible path from periphery to core — newcomers cannot see how to take on more central tasks and either stay peripheral indefinitely or leave. The community's competence does not reproduce.

sources.title: Situated Learning: Legitimate Peripheral Participation (Lave and Wenger, 1991)
sources.quote: Learning viewed as situated activity has as its central defining characteristic a process that we call legitimate peripheral participation.

sources.title: Communities of Practice: Learning, Meaning, and Identity (Wenger, 1998)
sources.quote: Communities of practice are groups of people who share a concern or a passion for something they do and learn how to do it better as they interact regularly.

diagram.mermaid:
flowchart TD
    A[Newcomer enters community
at the periphery] --> B[Legitimate peripheral participation
real but low-stakes tasks]
    B --> C[Observes more experienced
practitioners on central work]
    C --> D[Takes on more central tasks
as competence grows]
    D --> E[Acquires shared repertoire
vocabulary, tools, stories]
    E --> F{Full participant?}
    F -- Not yet --> D
    F -- Yes --> G[Becomes practitioner
models for next newcomer]
    G --> H[Community reproduces
competence through participation]

patterns.example_scenario: A junior data scientist joins an open-source machine learning library. Her first contribution is a documentation fix — legitimate, real, merged into the main branch, but peripheral to the library's core algorithmic work. She watches pull request discussions between senior maintainers, absorbing their vocabulary: 'numerically stable,' 'memory layout matters here,' 'this is a footgun for new users.' After three months she submits a small bug fix in a utility module, with a test. A senior maintainer reviews it and pushes back on the test coverage — a specific feedback pattern the junior had never received in her day job. She revises. Six months in, she takes on a feature proposal, has it debated in a GitHub issue thread, and ships it after two rounds of revision. Two years in, she is reviewing other people's pull requests, using the same vocabulary she first encountered in discussion threads, catching the same classes of issues the senior maintainers caught in her early work. She did not receive formal instruction in any of this — she learned by participating in increasingly central work in a community that cared about getting it right.

patterns.name: Deliberate Practice
patterns.summary: Deliberate practice is a structured, effortful activity specifically designed to improve performance in a particular domain, always with immediate feedback and focused attention on correcting weaknesses. It differs from mere experience or repetition: you push just beyond your current comfort zone with a clear improvement target in mind, guided by expert feedback. This pattern produces measurable skill gains that naive repetition cannot, by targeting the exact gap between current and desired performance.
patterns.intent: Build expert-level skill in a specific domain by repeatedly working at the edge of current ability with immediate, specific feedback.
patterns.when_to_apply: Apply when a learner has cleared beginner level and needs to break through performance plateaus. Use when you can identify a specific, improvable sub-skill, a coach or feedback mechanism is available, and the learner can sustain high mental effort for focused sessions. Do not use for initial orientation or for skills where errors have immediate serious real-world consequences with no safe practice environment.

inputs.name: Specific skill target
inputs.description: A well-defined sub-skill or performance gap that can be isolated and practiced in a controlled setting, distinct from general experience.

inputs.name: Feedback mechanism
inputs.description: A coach, mentor, automated evaluator, or clear performance metric that gives immediate, accurate feedback on each practice attempt.

inputs.name: Motivated learner
inputs.description: A learner who understands that deliberate practice is effortful and not inherently enjoyable, and who is committed to improvement rather than mere performance.

inputs.name: Practice time allocation
inputs.description: Protected, recurring blocks of time dedicated solely to practice — distinct from productive work. Ericsson's research indicates elite performers sustain roughly four hours of deliberate practice per day.

outputs.name: More capable learner
outputs.description: A practitioner who has measurably improved the targeted sub-skill and can perform it closer to expert level than before the session.

outputs.name: Identified next weakness
outputs.description: A clear picture of the next performance gap to target — the output of each session is both improved skill and a refined practice agenda.

outputs.name: Performance record
outputs.description: A log of practice sessions, attempts, errors, and corrections that documents the improvement trajectory and informs coaching adjustments.

steps.name: Define the improvement target
steps.description: Identify one specific sub-skill to improve this session — not 'get better at coding' but 'reduce latency in spotting off-by-one bugs in loop conditions'. The target must be narrow enough to be measurable within a single session.

steps.name: Practice at the edge of ability
steps.description: Perform the targeted activity at a difficulty level just beyond current comfort. If every attempt succeeds easily, the task is not stretching the learner. If every attempt fails without insight, the stretch is too large. Aim for roughly 60–70% success rate — enough failure to generate signal, enough success to maintain motivation.

steps.name: Receive and process immediate feedback
steps.description: After each attempt or small cluster of attempts, receive feedback that precisely names what went wrong and why. The learner must actively process this — not just hear it, but adjust their mental model before the next attempt.

steps.name: Repeat with correction
steps.description: Re-attempt the same or a closely related task incorporating the correction. The loop of attempt–feedback–correction–reattempt is the core engine; a single round is not deliberate practice.

steps.name: Review and set next target
steps.description: At the end of the session, assess which sub-skill moved and which remains the binding constraint. Record the finding and define the improvement target for the next session. This closes the practice loop and prevents drift back into comfortable repetition.

principles: Practice must be effortful: activities that feel comfortable are maintenance, not improvement.
Feedback must be immediate and specific: delayed or vague feedback cannot correct the mental model in time to matter.
The improvement target must be narrow: broad intentions diffuse effort and make gains unmeasurable.
Volume of mere repetition does not substitute for quality of deliberate effort: ten hours of naive practice rarely beats one hour of deliberate practice.
patterns.threshold: The learner can name the specific sub-skill they improved, describe the errors they corrected, and identify the next weakness to address — without prompting from a coach.
patterns.masterpiece: A documented portfolio of practice sessions showing a measurable performance arc: baseline, targeted weaknesses, corrections applied, and current performance level. The portfolio demonstrates not just what was practiced but how the practitioner's self-knowledge of their own gaps evolved.

known_uses.name: Berlin Academy of Music violin study (Ericsson, 1993)
known_uses.quote: there is complete correspondence between the skill level of the groups and their average accumulation of practice time alone with the violin
known_uses.note: The founding empirical study; distinguished expert, good, and music-teacher groups entirely by accumulated deliberate practice, not natural talent.

known_uses.name: Chess grandmaster training programs
known_uses.note: Structured game analysis with coaches targeting specific positional weaknesses is the dominant model for developing titled players.

known_failure_modes.note: Learners mistake logging hours for deliberate practice; without targeted weakness selection and immediate feedback the activity produces familiarity, not skill gain.
Once performance reaches an acceptable level, the learner stops stretching and practice becomes performance maintenance rather than improvement.
Practicing without a feedback mechanism produces confident incompetence — the learner reinforces their own errors rather than correcting them.

sources.title: The Role of Deliberate Practice in the Acquisition of Expert Performance
sources.quote: the teacher designs practice activities that the individual can engage in between meetings with the teacher. We call these practice activities deliberate practice

sources.title: The Role of Deliberate Practice in the Acquisition of Expert Performance — key finding
sources.quote: individual differences, even among elite performers, are closely related to assessed amounts of deliberate practice

sources.title: Peak: Secrets from the New Science of Expertise (Ericsson & Pool, 2016)
sources.quote: Deliberate practice nearly always involves building or modifying previously acquired skills by focusing on particular aspects of those skills and working to improve them specifically

diagram.mermaid:
flowchart TD
  A[Define narrow improvement target] --> B[Practice at edge of ability]
  B --> C[Receive immediate specific feedback]
  C --> D{Corrected?}
  D -- No --> B
  D -- Yes --> E[Reattempt with correction]
  E --> F{Session goal met?}
  F -- No --> B
  F -- Yes --> G[Review: set next target]
  G --> H[More capable learner]

patterns.example_scenario: A junior software engineer can write working code but repeatedly introduces race conditions when reasoning about concurrent access. Her tech lead acts as coach. In session one, they define the target: spot race conditions in shared-mutable-state examples. The engineer is given five code snippets — she catches two, misses three. The tech lead gives immediate feedback on each miss: 'You looked at the variable declaration but not at who else writes it.' The engineer reattempts the three failures with this lens, catches two of three. They log: 'Thread-of-ownership tracing is the gap.' Session two targets that sub-skill specifically with eight new examples. After six sessions the engineer catches 90% of planted race conditions and can explain the ownership chain in each case. The portfolio shows progression from 40% to 90% detection, with a clear log of which mental models were corrected and when.

patterns.name: Design Sprint
patterns.summary: A structured five-day process for answering critical business questions through design, prototyping, and testing ideas with real users — compressing months of iterative development into a single focused week. Jake Knapp created it at Google starting in 2010 and refined it at GV (Google Ventures) with Braden Kowitz, Michael Margolis, John Zeratsky, and Daniel Burka. It was published in Knapp's 2016 book Sprint. The GV website defines it as 'a five-day process for answering critical business questions through design, prototyping, and testing ideas with customers.' The design sprint's educational value is distinct from its product value. Participants learn to move from an ambiguous problem to a testable hypothesis to real user data in five days. They practise the cognitive moves of product reasoning, rapid prototyping, and hypothesis-driven decision-making under time pressure.
patterns.intent: Move a team from a critical, ambiguous question to real user validation data in five focused days — replacing months of assumption-driven iteration with one week of structured learning.
patterns.when_to_apply: Apply when a team faces a critical, ambiguous question that would otherwise generate weeks of inconclusive debate or months of build-then-discover cycles. Use a design sprint when the question is genuinely open — not 'how do we implement this?' but 'should we build this at all, and in what form?' A realistic prototype must be buildable in one day. Real target users must be recruitable for Friday testing. A decision-maker who can commit to next steps must be present throughout the week. Do not use a design sprint when the question has already been decided. Running a sprint to create the appearance of process is the most common misuse.

inputs.name: Sprint team (4–7 people)
inputs.description: A cross-functional team including a decision-maker (who must be present and empowered to commit), a designer, an engineer, a domain expert, and a customer research lead. The decision-maker's authority and presence is non-negotiable — without it, the sprint produces recommendations, not decisions.

inputs.name: Critical business question
inputs.description: A specific, answerable question about a product, service, or strategy where the cost of being wrong is high and the current path is unclear. The sprint is for questions where rapid learning is more valuable than additional planning.

inputs.name: Five uninterrupted working days
inputs.description: The full team's calendars cleared for Monday through Friday with no competing meetings or obligations. A team that is also doing their regular jobs during sprint week does not run a sprint — they run a meeting series.

inputs.name: Five real target users for Friday
inputs.description: Actual members of the target user population, recruited before the sprint begins, available for one-hour individual interviews on Friday. Without real users, the sprint produces a prototype but no validation data.

outputs.name: A more capable learner
outputs.description: A team member who has practised the full product reasoning cycle — from ambiguous question to testable hypothesis to prototype to real user data — in five days, making those cognitive moves faster and more reliable in future work.

outputs.name: Validated or invalidated prototype with user data
outputs.description: The masterpiece: a realistic prototype tested with five real users, accompanied by documented observations about what worked and what did not — answering the sprint question with real evidence rather than internal debate.

steps.name: Monday — map and set the target
steps.description: The team establishes the long-term goal and maps the problem space end-to-end — from customer first contact to resolution. They consult internal experts through structured 'How Might We' interviews. Then they select one specific moment in the map to target for the sprint. The day ends with one focused target chosen by the decision-maker.

steps.name: Tuesday — sketch competing solutions
steps.description: Each team member sketches their own solution to the target using a structured four-step method: notes, ideas, crazy-8s (eight sketches in eight minutes), and a solution sketch. Solutions are individual and diverse — the goal is divergence before convergence.

steps.name: Wednesday — decide and storyboard
steps.description: The team critiques Tuesday's sketches using silent voting and structured discussion, selects the most promising approach (the decision-maker has final authority), and builds a step-by-step storyboard — the blueprint for Thursday's prototype.

steps.name: Thursday — build a realistic prototype
steps.description: The team builds only the customer-facing surface of the prototype — realistic enough that users will react to it genuinely, using the fastest tools available. Engineers focus on the façade; the prototype does not need to work under the hood. The goal is something testable by Friday morning.

steps.name: Friday — test with real users
steps.description: One team member interviews five users one-on-one, following a prepared script but reacting to what users actually do. The rest of the team watches a live stream and takes structured notes. By end of day, the team has real user data answering the sprint question — and a clear decision about next steps.

principles: Real user data beats internal debate — any internal discussion of what users will think or do is worth zero compared to one hour of watching a real user interact with a prototype.

The prototype is not the product — building only the façade needed for testing is a skill, not a shortcut. The decision to not build the backend is the design sprint's most important scope discipline.
patterns.threshold: The team can move from an ambiguous question to a tested prototype in five days, and can articulate what they learned from real user feedback that they could not have learned from internal discussion.
patterns.masterpiece: A tested prototype and a documented set of user observations that directly answers the sprint question — with clear evidence for what to do next, based on data from real users rather than internal assumptions.

known_uses.name: GV (Google Ventures) — 100+ companies
known_uses.quote: The sprint is a five-day process for answering critical business questions through design, prototyping, and testing ideas with customers.
known_uses.note: The authoritative source for the design sprint methodology. GV ran sprints with over 100 portfolio companies before the 2016 book publication, refining the process across startups and established product teams.

known_uses.name: Google Cloud AI Design Sprint
known_uses.note: Google Cloud offers structured AI-specific design sprints to enterprise customers for scoping, validating, and prototyping AI-powered features before committing to full engineering investment.

known_uses.name: AI product design sprints — used at Adobe, PayPal, Boeing, Salesforce, Nike
known_uses.note: Google Design introduced Problem Framing (a design sprint variant for AI problem scoping) to product managers and designers from Adobe, PayPal, Boeing, Salesforce, and Nike — demonstrating the sprint format's adoption in AI feature development across industries.

known_failure_modes.note: The anti-pattern of running a sprint without the actual decision-maker present and empowered throughout the week. Without someone who can commit to next steps based on Friday's data, the sprint produces a prototype and a recommendation that must then go through the real decision process — eliminating the format's primary value.

The anti-pattern of using a design sprint when the question has already been decided and the sprint is being run to create the appearance of user-centred process. Participants detect this within the first day. The sprint format requires a genuinely open question; a closed question produces a Potemkin prototype and no real learning.

The anti-pattern of testing the Friday prototype with colleagues, friends, or other employees rather than actual target users. Internal 'users' already know the context, the product language, and the team's intent. Real users reveal the gaps internal testing cannot surface.

sources.title: The Design Sprint — GV
sources.quote: The sprint is a five-day process for answering critical business questions through design, prototyping, and testing ideas with customers.

sources.title: Knapp, J., Zeratsky, J., & Kowitz, B. (2016). Sprint: How to Solve Big Problems and Test New Ideas in Just Five Days. Simon & Schuster.

diagram.mermaid:
flowchart TD
    A[Critical business question
ambiguous, high-stakes] --> B[Monday: Map and target
long-term goal + focus selection]
    B --> C[Tuesday: Sketch solutions
individual divergent sketches]
    C --> D[Wednesday: Decide and storyboard
silent vote + decision-maker commits]
    D --> E[Thursday: Build prototype
customer-facing facade only]
    E --> F[Friday: Test with 5 real users
one-on-one structured interviews]
    F --> G[Data synthesis
patterns across 5 sessions]
    G --> H[More capable learner
+ validated prototype masterpiece]

patterns.example_scenario: An AI product team at a mid-size company is debating whether to add an AI-powered 'smart reply' feature to their B2B customer support tool. The discussion has been running for six weeks with no decision. A design sprint is called. Monday: the team maps the customer support journey and selects the target moment — the point where a support agent first reads an incoming ticket and must decide how to respond. The long-term goal: agents respond to 80% of tickets in under two minutes without reducing response quality. Tuesday: each of the six team members sketches their own version of the smart reply interface. Wednesday: the team votes silently on the five most promising elements, and the CPO (present and empowered) makes the call: a panel that suggests three ranked responses the agent can edit or ignore. Thursday: the designer and a frontend engineer build a realistic-looking interface — no backend, just a prototype app with hard-coded responses. Friday: five current customer support agents from two existing customers interview with the researcher while the team watches a live stream. Three agents immediately try to edit the suggested responses. Two ignore them entirely. The data is clear: agents want suggestions, but they do not trust them without the ability to see the reasoning. The sprint question is answered. The decision about what to build next is made on real evidence, not internal debate.

patterns.name: Earn Your Marks
patterns.summary: Layer points, badges, streaks, and tiered status onto AI learning to sustain motivation across months. Works when badge progression maps to real skill milestones — and fails when it is a thin trophy layer on top of unchanged content. Salesforce targets 1 million Agentblazers through three tiers (Champion, Innovator, Legend) each tied to a concrete capability, not just activity. Research distinguishes this design from the shallow-gamification anti-pattern: badges awarded purely for activity without testing application crowd out intrinsic motivation via the overjustification effect.
patterns.intent: Sustain learner motivation across a multi-month AI upskilling programme by making skill progression visible, social, and rewarded — without letting the reward mechanism displace the skill itself.
patterns.when_to_apply: Apply when learners must sustain engagement over more than four weeks with no external deadline forcing completion — the self-paced async context where motivation naturally decays. Also apply when the org wants a shared vocabulary for skill levels across a large population (e.g. 'she is a Champion, he is an Innovator'). Use when you can map each tier to a concrete capability that can be verified, not just content consumed. Do not apply as a substitute for a real skill-assessment mechanism — badges without verified capability are the anti-pattern. Do not use streaks as the primary retention mechanism for skills that require depth rather than daily habit.

inputs.name: Skill tier map
inputs.description: A designed progression of three or more tiers where each tier is defined by a specific AI capability the learner can demonstrate — not by hours of content consumed. Each tier must have a verifiable challenge or assessment.

inputs.name: Public learner profile
inputs.description: A visible profile (on a platform, in a Slack status, or on a company directory) where earned status is displayed so social recognition reinforces continuation.

inputs.name: Assessment mechanism per tier
inputs.description: A verified challenge — a quiz with realistic edge cases, a hands-on project, a peer-reviewed artefact — that a learner must pass to earn each badge. Without this, the system produces the shallow-gamification anti-pattern.

outputs.name: More capable learner
outputs.description: A learner who has progressed through verified skill tiers, each backed by a demonstrated capability — not just a count of videos watched.

outputs.name: Visible skill tier record
outputs.description: The masterpiece: a public or shareable profile showing earned tiers, the dates they were achieved, and the capabilities each tier represents. Usable as a portable credential within or beyond the org.

steps.name: Design the tier ladder
steps.description: Define three or more tiers. Each tier must be named, must map to a concrete AI capability, and must have a clear assessment gate. For example: Champion = can write a working prompt that completes a defined task. Innovator = can build and deploy a simple agent. Legend = can design an org-wide agent strategy and present it to peers.

steps.name: Build the assessment gates
steps.description: For each tier, create a verified challenge that cannot be passed by activity alone. Use scenario quizzes with realistic edge cases, hands-on build tasks, or peer-reviewed artefacts. The gate is what separates a meaningful badge from a trophy.

steps.name: Surface status publicly
steps.description: Display earned tiers on a learner profile visible to peers and managers. Social recognition — being seen to have advanced — is a primary driver of continued progression. Leaderboards are optional; profile visibility is not.

steps.name: Use streaks selectively
steps.description: Apply streaks only for skills that benefit from daily-habit formation (e.g. reviewing one AI-generated draft per day). Do not use streaks as a proxy for skill depth — a 30-day streak of clicking does not equal capability.

steps.name: Renew annually
steps.description: Require annual re-verification of top-tier status so the credential stays current as AI capabilities change. Salesforce's Agentblazer status renews each year; prior years remain visible but the current year must be re-earned.

principles: A badge earns its meaning from the assessment behind it — design the assessment first, then the badge.
Social visibility multiplies the motivating effect of status; a badge no one can see is a private reward with private reach.
Streaks build habits; tiers build skills — use each for what it does, not interchangeably.
patterns.threshold: Every learner who holds a given tier has passed the verified assessment for that tier — there is no tier holder who earned their status through activity alone.
patterns.masterpiece: A learner profile showing at least two tiers earned through verified assessment, with dates and the capability each tier represents — portable inside and outside the org.

known_uses.name: Salesforce Agentblazer Status — Champion, Innovator, Legend
known_uses.note: Three-tier progression tied to specific Agentforce capabilities. Status renews annually. 1 million Agentblazers targeted in 2025. Free Agentforce Specialist Certification Exam offered to drive adoption.

known_uses.name: Salesforce Ben — Agentblazer Status Guide
known_uses.note: Agentblazer Legend requires designing complex strategies to manage and customise the Agentforce lifecycle — a capability gate, not an activity gate.

known_failure_modes.note: Anti-pattern: awarding badges purely for activity (views, clicks, hours) without testing application. Research (Journals of Gamification, 2024) calls this the BPL anti-pattern — overreliance on badges, points, and leaderboards without transforming the core experience. This crowds out intrinsic motivation via the overjustification effect and produces a population of badge-holders with no verified capability.

Anti-pattern: replacing skill assessment with streak maintenance. A learner who completes a 60-day streak of 5-minute micro-lessons has formed a habit but may not have formed a skill. Streaks and assessments must both be present.

diagram.mermaid:
flowchart TD
    A[Design tier ladder
Capability per tier, not content hours] --> B[Build verified assessment gate
per tier]
    B --> C[Learner completes content
and attempts assessment]
    C --> D{Assessment passed?}
    D -- No --> C
    D -- Yes --> E[Badge awarded
Profile updated publicly]
    E --> F{More tiers available?}
    F -- Yes --> G[Learner sees next tier capability]
    G --> C
    F -- No --> H[Masterpiece: verified tier profile
portable credential]

patterns.example_scenario: A global professional services firm wants to upskill 8,000 consultants in AI-assisted research and analysis over 12 months. A self-paced course platform alone has a 6% completion rate. The L&D team redesigns the programme around three tiers: Practitioner (can use AI tools to accelerate research), Specialist (can build a prompt library for a client engagement), and Expert (can design and present an AI-augmented delivery approach to a client). Each tier has a verified gate: a scored scenario quiz, a reviewed prompt library, and a peer-assessed presentation respectively. Tier status appears on the internal directory and the learner's email signature. Within six months, 71% of the cohort has reached Practitioner; 34% has reached Specialist. The Expert tier — which requires a real client artefact — is held by 11% but those 11% are now running internal AI enablement sessions for their practice areas, multiplying the programme's reach beyond the original cohort.

patterns.name: Experiential Learning Cycle
patterns.summary: Learning advances through a four-stage cycle: concrete experience, reflective observation, abstract conceptualization, and active experimentation. Knowledge is created not by absorbing information but by transforming lived experience through systematic reflection and deliberate testing. Skipping any stage leaves the cycle incomplete. Acting without reflecting, or theorising without testing, makes learning shallow.
patterns.intent: Deepen learning by cycling continuously through doing, reflecting, concluding, and experimenting rather than treating any single stage as sufficient.
patterns.when_to_apply: Apply whenever a learner has direct access to a real task or situation and enough time for structured reflection. Use it at the start of a skill-building initiative to set the repeating rhythm. Return to it whenever learning stalls because the learner is stuck in one stage. That stall might be over-theorising without acting, or acting without reflecting.

inputs.name: Concrete experience
inputs.description: A real activity, task, simulation, or encounter that the learner performs — not a description read about, but something directly lived or done.

inputs.name: Reflective capacity and time
inputs.description: Structured opportunity and prompts for the learner to observe and review what happened, without rushing immediately to conclusions or next actions.

outputs.name: More capable learner
outputs.description: A learner who has moved through at least one full cycle and can name what they experienced, what they observed, what principle they formed, and what they will test next.

outputs.name: Tested abstraction
outputs.description: A generalised principle, model, or rule that the learner formed from reflection and has then applied experimentally — the masterpiece of one cycle, and the input to the next.

steps.name: Concrete experience
steps.description: The learner carries out a real task, experiment, or encounter. The experience must be genuine — not hypothetical — because the learning derives from what actually happened, including surprises and failures.

steps.name: Reflective observation
steps.description: The learner pauses and reviews the experience from multiple angles: what happened, what was noticed, what felt unexpected, what others might have seen differently. Reflection is structured, not casual — journal prompts, guided questions, or peer discussion create the discipline.

steps.name: Abstract conceptualisation
steps.description: The learner draws conclusions, forms a principle, or builds a mental model that makes sense of the observations. This is the generalisation step: from 'what happened here' to 'what this means in general.'

steps.name: Active experimentation
steps.description: The learner applies the new principle to a fresh situation — a new task, a variation, or a real problem. The experiment generates a new concrete experience, restarting the cycle at a higher level of understanding.

principles: Knowledge is created through transformation of experience, not through transmission of information.
All four stages are necessary — a cycle that stops at reflection produces insight without skill; one that stops at action produces habit without understanding.
Each completed cycle raises the entry point of the next — learning is genuinely cumulative.
patterns.threshold: The learner can walk through a completed cycle from memory — naming the concrete experience, the observation, the principle formed, and the experiment conducted — and can identify where in a current cycle they are stuck.
patterns.masterpiece: A portfolio of completed cycles in which each active-experimentation stage generated a new concrete experience that became the input to the following cycle — showing genuine cumulative growth rather than repeated entry-level encounters.

known_uses.name: Medical and nursing education
known_uses.note: Kolb's cycle used as the structural framework for clinical rotation design, simulation debriefs, and reflective journals in health professions education.

known_uses.name: Reinforcement-learning agent training
known_uses.note: The four-stage structure maps onto RL agent loops: environment interaction (CE), reward analysis (RO), policy update (AC), next-episode execution (AE).

known_failure_modes.note: Anti-pattern: jumping from concrete experience directly to the next action without reflective observation or conceptualisation. The cycle completes fast but no generalisation forms — the learner repeats the same experience repeatedly without advancing.

Anti-pattern: stopping after abstract conceptualisation and never running the active-experimentation step. The learner accumulates untested models that feel certain but have not been stress-tested against reality.

sources.title: Experiential Learning: Experience as the Source of Learning and Development
sources.quote: Learning is the process whereby knowledge is created through the transformation of experience.
sources.note: Kolb, D.A. (1984), p. 38. Quote confirmed by multiple secondary academic sources citing the original.

sources.title: Kolb's Learning Styles and Experiential Learning Cycle — Simply Psychology
sources.quote: Effective learning is seen when a person progresses through a cycle of four stages: of (1) having a concrete experience followed by (2) observation of and reflection on that experience which leads to (3) the formation of abstract concepts (analysis) and generalizations (conclusions) which are then (4) used to test a hypothesis in future situations, resulting in new experiences.

diagram.mermaid:
flowchart TD
    A[Concrete Experience
Do the real thing] --> B[Reflective Observation
Review what happened]
    B --> C[Abstract Conceptualisation
Form a principle or model]
    C --> D[Active Experimentation
Apply the principle to a new situation]
    D --> E[New Concrete Experience
higher-level entry point]
    E --> B

patterns.example_scenario: A software engineer joins a new codebase and is asked to fix a bug she has never seen before. She attempts the fix (concrete experience) — it partially works but introduces a regression. She pauses and reviews her approach: she had changed a shared utility without tracing all its callers (reflective observation). She forms a principle: shared utilities must be traced before any modification (abstract conceptualisation). On the next bug fix she writes a caller-trace script first, runs it, and only then edits the utility (active experimentation). The script surfaces two callers she would have missed. The new experience is running the trace, seeing the callers, and making the safe edit. It becomes the entry to a new cycle. In that cycle she reflects on when caller-tracing adds most value. After three cycles, she has a reliable, tested practice rather than a one-off workaround.

patterns.name: Experimental Exploration with Checkpoints
patterns.summary: When a problem is too uncertain to plan directly, the learner runs a time-boxed experiment — a spike — aimed at a specific question rather than a deliverable. A fixed time limit and a clearly stated question keep the exploration productive without allowing it to become unbounded research. Checkpoints at the midpoint and end of the box force a decision: is the question answered, should the box be extended, or should the approach change? The output is knowledge, not code or a product — and that knowledge is sufficient to unlock the next planning decision.
patterns.intent: Resolve a specific uncertainty through a strictly time-boxed exploration so that the next planning or learning decision can be made on evidence rather than assumption.
patterns.when_to_apply: Apply when a decision or next step is blocked by a specific unknown — a technical question, a feasibility concern, a conceptual gap — that can be investigated in a bounded time without full commitment to an implementation. Do not apply to vague uncertainty; the exploration requires a concrete, answerable question. Do not extend the box without a conscious checkpoint decision.

inputs.name: Specific answerable question
inputs.description: A precise question whose answer will unblock a decision — not 'how does this technology work?' but 'can this library handle 10,000 concurrent connections within our latency budget?'

inputs.name: Fixed time box
inputs.description: A hard upper bound on exploration time, agreed before the spike begins — typically one to three days. The box is the guardrail that prevents learning from becoming indefinite research.

outputs.name: More capable learner or team
outputs.description: A learner or team that has resolved a concrete uncertainty and can now make the next decision based on evidence rather than assumption.

outputs.name: Spike report
outputs.description: A short written record — the masterpiece — stating the question explored, the approach taken, what was found, and the decision it now enables. The spike report is what the learning produced; any code or prototype is disposable unless explicitly retained.

steps.name: State the question and the time box
steps.description: Before beginning, write the specific question the spike must answer and the hard end time of the box. Share both with anyone affected by the next decision. A spike without a written question tends to drift into general research.

steps.name: Run the exploration
steps.description: Investigate the question using the fastest reliable method — prototype, literature scan, expert interview, experiment, or calculation. Explore only what serves the question; resist the pull of interesting adjacent territory.

steps.name: Midpoint checkpoint
steps.description: At the midpoint of the time box, assess: is the question going to be answered by the end? If yes, continue. If no, decide: narrow the question further, extend the box with a specific reason, or stop and report what was learned so far. The checkpoint is mandatory — an unchecked spike will always expand.

steps.name: Write the spike report
steps.description: At the end of the box, write the spike report: question asked, approach taken, what was found, and what decision is now enabled. If the question was not fully answered, say so and state what further investigation would be needed. The report is the output — not the prototype.

principles: The output is knowledge, not a deliverable — any artefact produced during the spike is a means of learning, not the goal.
The time box is not a suggestion — it is the mechanism that distinguishes a spike from open-ended research; a soft box is no box.
A concrete question before entry, a written report at exit — these are the two non-negotiable boundaries of the move.
patterns.threshold: The learner can identify the specific decision that the spike unblocked and explain, with reference to the spike report, what evidence changed the situation from uncertain to decided.
patterns.masterpiece: A spike report that states a precise question, a bounded investigation, a clear finding, and the decision it enabled — demonstrable by showing that the next planning step was made using the spike's evidence rather than assumption.

known_uses.name: Extreme Programming spike solutions
known_uses.quote: Spike is an Extreme Programming term meaning experiment. We use the word because we think of a spike as a quick, almost brute-force experiment aimed at learning just one thing.
known_uses.note: Ron Jeffries, XP co-originator, defining spikes as learning experiments. Quote sourced from johnclapham.wordpress.com citing Jeffries directly.

known_uses.name: Agile team uncertainty management
known_uses.quote: a spike refers to a time-boxed research activity that helps teams make better decisions and deliver better products
known_uses.note: Mountain Goat Software definition of spike in agile practice.

known_uses.name: Autonomous agent exploration tasks
known_uses.note: Agent design patterns that include bounded exploration sub-tasks with a hard step limit and a written finding before proceeding — spike structure applied to agent planning loops.

known_uses.name: Spikes (Scaled Agile Framework)
known_uses.quote: When faced with a question, risk, or uncertainty, Agile Teams conduct small experiments before moving to implementation rather than speculate about the outcome.
known_uses.note: SAFe codifies the spike at enterprise scale, framing the deliverable explicitly as risk reduction — gaining the knowledge needed to reduce the risk of a technical approach or improve estimate reliability.

known_failure_modes.note: Anti-pattern: starting an exploration with a vague question and no hard time box, calling it a spike. Without a concrete question and a fixed end time, the activity is general research — useful, but not the move. Learning is diffuse and no decision is unblocked.

Anti-pattern: retaining and building on the prototype, script, or code produced during the spike as if it were production-quality. Spike artefacts are disposable learning tools; treating them as deliverables introduces technical debt built on exploratory, unreviewed work.

patterns.ecosystem: software engineering, professional development, autonomous-agent training

sources.title: Why is a Spike called a Spike? — John Clapham (2016)
sources.quote: Spike is an Extreme Programming term meaning experiment. We use the word because we think of a spike as a quick, almost brute-force experiment aimed at learning just one thing.
sources.note: Ron Jeffries (XP co-originator) quoted directly. Confirms XP origin and 'learning one thing' framing.

sources.title: What Are Agile Spikes? — Mountain Goat Software
sources.quote: a spike refers to a time-boxed research activity that helps teams make better decisions and deliver better products
sources.note: Mike Cohn / Mountain Goat Software secondary definition confirming time-boxed and decision-enabling framing.

sources.title: Spikes — Scaled Agile Framework
sources.quote: Spikes represent activities such as exploration, architecture, infrastructure, research, design, and prototyping.

diagram.mermaid:
flowchart TD
    A[Specific answerable question
+ hard time box agreed] --> B[Run exploration
fastest reliable method]
    B --> C[Midpoint checkpoint
will question be answered?]
    C -- Yes --> D[Continue exploration]
    C -- No: too broad --> E[Narrow question
or extend box deliberately]
    E --> B
    D --> F[Time box ends]
    F --> G[Write spike report
question / approach / finding / decision enabled]
    G --> H[More capable learner
+ decision unblocked by evidence]

patterns.example_scenario: A machine learning team is deciding whether to switch their inference pipeline from batch processing to real-time streaming. The architecture change is large; committing to it without evidence would be expensive if the latency targets cannot be met. The team proposes a spike: the question is 'Can our current model serve predictions at under 200ms p99 latency with 500 concurrent users on our candidate streaming framework?' The time box is two days. One engineer runs the investigation: deploys a minimal version of the model behind the streaming framework, generates load at 500 concurrent users, and measures latency. At the midpoint checkpoint the question is on track. At the end of the box she writes the spike report: p99 latency was 147ms under load, within budget, but memory overhead was 40% higher than batch. Decision enabled: proceed with streaming architecture, but include a memory monitoring alert in the implementation plan. The report is shared; the team makes the architecture decision in the next planning session based on evidence. No code from the spike is retained — it was exploratory scaffolding. The masterpiece is the spike report: a two-page document that replaced weeks of assumption-based planning.

patterns.name: Flipped Classroom
patterns.summary: Instruction is moved out of the classroom into asynchronous pre-work — typically video — and the time formerly spent on instruction is used for active application, problem-solving, and teacher-guided interaction. 'That which is traditionally done in class is now done at home, and that which is traditionally done as homework is now completed in class' (Bergmann & Sams, 2012, p. 13). The logic is that passive reception of new material is the part of learning that least requires a trained instructor present; active struggle with application is precisely where expert guidance is most valuable. Popularised by Colorado chemistry teachers Jonathan Bergmann and Aaron Sams from 2007, who began recording video lessons so absent students could catch up — and found the results were better for everyone.
patterns.intent: Reallocate classroom time from passive information delivery to active, instructor-supported application, so that expert guidance is available at the moment of greatest cognitive need.
patterns.when_to_apply: Apply when learners can reliably access pre-work outside class and when the instructor's most distinctive contribution is not delivering information but responding to learner difficulty in real time. Use when class time is scarce and is currently dominated by lecture — any reallocation toward active work with expert feedback will improve learning. Do not use when learners lack access to video or internet outside class, when pre-work compliance is very low, or when the subject matter has no clear application component that benefits from peer and instructor interaction. Also avoid when the instructor's live explanation is genuinely irreplaceable and hard to pre-record effectively.

inputs.name: Asynchronous instructional content
inputs.description: Video lectures, annotated readings, or other self-paced material that delivers the foundational concepts learners need before attending the active session. Must be accessible outside class and navigable at the learner's own pace.

inputs.name: Accountability mechanism for pre-work
inputs.description: A short quiz, question prompt, or entry ticket that confirms learners engaged with the pre-work before the active session — without which a large proportion will arrive unprepared and active time collapses.

inputs.name: Active in-class tasks
inputs.description: Problem sets, projects, case studies, or peer-teaching activities that require and develop understanding of the pre-work material and cannot be completed without it — the tasks that make the reclaimed class time worthwhile.

outputs.name: More capable learner
outputs.description: A learner who has encountered concepts before class, applied them with instructor and peer support during class, and received targeted feedback at the moment of greatest confusion — rather than discovering confusion alone at home during homework.

outputs.name: Instructor time spent on high-value interaction
outputs.description: The masterpiece at the instructor level: class time in which the instructor spends no time on information delivery and all available time on individual and small-group diagnosis, feedback, and targeted reteaching.

steps.name: Audit current class time
steps.description: Map what actually happens during class: how many minutes go to lecture, how many to active work, how many to Q&A. Identify which parts of the lecture can be pre-recorded without loss and which genuinely benefit from live presence.

steps.name: Create or curate pre-work content
steps.description: Record short, focused videos (10–15 minutes is the effective ceiling for a single concept) or curate existing materials. Annotate key moments. Structure so the learner can pause, rewind, and revisit at their own pace.

steps.name: Design a pre-work accountability check
steps.description: Create a brief quiz or question prompt that learners complete before arriving, confirming engagement with the content. Make it low-stakes but required — it is a readiness gate, not a performance measure.

steps.name: Design active in-class tasks
steps.description: Replace lecture time with tasks that require application of the pre-work: problems, case analyses, peer instruction, design challenges. Ensure tasks are genuinely difficult enough to need instructor support so learners experience the value of the reclaimed time.

steps.name: Use class time for guided active work
steps.description: Circulate continuously during active sessions. Spend time with struggling learners; extend successful ones. The instructor's role shifts from presenter to expert coach — available at the moment of greatest difficulty.

principles: Class time is the scarcest resource and the only time expert feedback is reliably available: spend it on the tasks that most need that feedback, not on content delivery the learner can receive alone.

Pre-work only works if it is done: accountability mechanisms are not optional add-ons but structural components of the flip. Without them, the active class session degenerates into re-teaching the pre-work to the unprepared majority.
patterns.threshold: The learner arrives at the active session with sufficient foundational understanding to engage productively with the application tasks without needing the instructor to re-deliver the pre-work material.
patterns.masterpiece: A session in which the instructor does not deliver a single minute of whole-class lecture, spends the entire period responding to individual and small-group difficulty, and learners complete application tasks at a depth they could not have reached working alone.

known_uses.name: Bergmann and Sams — Woodland Park High School chemistry
known_uses.note: Bergmann and Sams began recording video lessons in 2007 to support absent students; they found in-class time reclaimed for active work improved outcomes for all students and popularised the approach through their 2012 book.

known_uses.name: Khan Academy and classroom partnerships
known_uses.note: Khan Academy's video library was explicitly adopted by teachers as the pre-work component of flipped classrooms, with class time reserved for working through problems — a large-scale instantiation of the Bergmann-Sams model.

known_failure_modes.note: Anti-pattern: learners do not engage with pre-work before the session, so the active class time is spent re-delivering the foundational content and the flip produces no gain over a traditional lecture. Occurs when pre-work accountability mechanisms are absent or non-enforced.

Anti-pattern: the instructor reclaims class time from lecture but fills it with more passive activity — watching additional videos in class, whole-class Q&A without individual work — rather than genuinely active application. The flip moves the location of passive learning without changing its nature.

Anti-pattern: the model is applied in a context where a subset of learners lacks reliable access to video or internet outside class, so the flip systematically disadvantages those learners who most need support. Equity must be assessed before adopting the model.

patterns.ecosystem: education, corporate training, online and blended learning

sources.title: Flip Your Classroom: Reach Every Student in Every Class Every Day
sources.quote: that which is traditionally done in class is now done at home, and that which is traditionally done as homework is now completed in class
sources.note: Bergmann, J., & Sams, A. (2012). ISTE / ASCD. p. 13. The defining statement of the model by its originators.

sources.title: The Flipped Classroom — Education Next
sources.quote: I now have time to work individually with students. I talk to every student in every classroom every day
sources.note: Bergmann quoted in Education Next on the core pedagogical gain of the flipped model: instructor time freed for individual interaction.

diagram.mermaid:
flowchart TD
    A[Learner watches pre-work video at home
Self-paced; pause and rewind] --> B[Pre-work accountability check
Quiz or entry ticket before class]
    B --> C{Pre-work done?}
    C -- No --> D[Targeted catch-up or pre-work reassignment]
    D --> C
    C -- Yes --> E[Active in-class work
Problems, cases, projects]
    E --> F[Instructor circulates
Coaches struggling learners; extends strong ones]
    F --> G[Formative checkpoint
Is understanding solid enough to continue?]
    G -- No --> F
    G -- Yes --> H[Masterpiece: application task completed
with expert feedback at point of difficulty]

patterns.example_scenario: A corporate data science team lead runs a weekly Friday workshop. Previously each session started with 30 minutes of lecture on a new technique, then 20 minutes of exercise, then 10 minutes of questions. She flips the model: on Thursday she releases a 12-minute narrated walkthrough video of the week's technique with a five-question quiz due by Friday 8am. Friday's 60 minutes are now entirely active: the first 10 minutes review the quiz results as a group, the remaining 50 minutes are spent on a case study problem the team can only solve by applying the technique. She circulates and targets the two engineers whose quiz answers show conceptual gaps. By the end of the session every engineer has worked through the full case with real data and received at least one targeted comment from the team lead. The following week's quiz shows retention substantially higher than in the old lecture format.

patterns.name: Formative Assessment Checkpoints
patterns.summary: Formative assessment is a continuous, embedded practice of gathering evidence about learner understanding and adjusting instruction — or the learner's own effort — in response to that evidence. Unlike summative assessment, which judges what was learned after the fact, formative assessment intervenes while learning is still in progress. Black and Wiliam's landmark 1998 review found strong evidence that improving formative assessment raises achievement standards, and that the feedback loop must close in both directions: the teacher adjusting instruction and the learner adjusting their own approach through self-assessment. Checkpoints make the practice visible and regular rather than implicit and occasional.
patterns.intent: Keep learning on track by regularly surfacing the gap between current understanding and the target, then using that gap information to adjust instruction or learner effort before it becomes a terminal failure.
patterns.when_to_apply: Apply throughout any learning sequence — not just at the end. Insert checkpoints wherever the learning sequence branches or depends on a prior concept being solid: if the prior concept is shaky, subsequent instruction builds on sand. Use brief, low-stakes checks — exit tickets, targeted questions, short tasks — rather than full tests, so the checkpoint itself does not consume disproportionate time. Apply especially when the instructor suspects misconceptions have formed, when learner engagement has dropped, or when the pace of instruction has been faster than normal. Train learners in self-assessment as early as possible so they can initiate their own checkpoints without waiting for external prompting.

inputs.name: Learning objective in checkable form
inputs.description: A specific, observable outcome that can be assessed in a short task or question — not 'understands recursion' but 'can write a recursive function that correctly handles the base case.'

inputs.name: Low-stakes checkpoint mechanism
inputs.description: A quick probe of understanding — question, brief task, exit ticket, peer explanation, or diagnostic quiz — that generates evidence without the stress and time cost of formal evaluation.

inputs.name: Commitment to act on the evidence
inputs.description: A willingness from the instructor or learner to actually change what happens next based on checkpoint results, rather than treating the checkpoint as a box-ticking exercise.

outputs.name: More capable learner
outputs.description: A learner whose misconceptions were caught and corrected while the material was still current, rather than discovered retrospectively in a final assessment when remediation is harder and more costly.

outputs.name: Adjusted instructional path
outputs.description: The masterpiece of a checkpoint cycle: a concrete change to what happens next — reteaching a concept, slowing pace, giving targeted feedback, redirecting a learner's self-study — that demonstrably closes the gap identified.

steps.name: Specify the checkpoint objective
steps.description: Before the checkpoint, articulate exactly what evidence of understanding you need: which concept, which operation, which misconception you are probing. Vague objectives produce checkpoints that generate noise rather than signal.

steps.name: Design a minimal, targeted probe
steps.description: Create the smallest checkpoint that generates reliable evidence of the specific objective. A single well-chosen question often outperforms a ten-item quiz in revealing whether a concept is understood or misunderstood.

steps.name: Administer and collect evidence
steps.description: Run the checkpoint in a low-stakes mode — no grades, no judgment — so learners respond honestly. Anonymous or group responses (show-of-hands, shared whiteboards, polling tools) reduce social pressure that masks real understanding.

steps.name: Interpret the evidence and decide
steps.description: Categorise responses: who understands, who has a specific misconception, who is guessing. Make a concrete decision about what changes: reteach, pair stronger with weaker, send one group ahead, slow down. The checkpoint is useless without this decision.

steps.name: Act and close the loop with the learner
steps.description: Implement the change. Crucially, give learners feedback on their checkpoint response — not a grade but a specific note on what was correct, what was wrong, and what to do next. Black and Wiliam are explicit: feedback must tell the learner how to improve, not just whether they succeeded.

principles: Formative assessment works only when the loop closes: evidence gathered but not acted upon is surveillance, not assessment. The loop must close for both instructor (adjusting teaching) and learner (adjusting effort and strategy).

Feedback should be task-referenced, not person-referenced: telling a learner their answer was wrong is far less useful than telling them exactly which step went wrong and what to do instead. Grade-based or comparative feedback provokes ego responses that block learning.

Learner self-assessment is not a nice-to-have: training learners to assess their own understanding is what makes formative assessment scalable and persistent. A learner who can identify their own gap does not need an instructor at every checkpoint.
patterns.threshold: The learner can accurately self-assess their own understanding of the current topic — identifying what they know confidently, what they know partially, and what they do not know — and can initiate a request for targeted feedback on the specific gap.
patterns.masterpiece: A learning sequence in which every checkpoint generated a concrete instructional adjustment, the learner's self-assessments were consistently accurate against external evidence, and the final summative assessment showed substantially higher performance than a comparable sequence without checkpoints.

known_uses.name: Black Box reform in UK schools
known_uses.org: King's College London / UK school reform
known_uses.note: Black and Wiliam's 1998 review directly prompted large-scale formative assessment reforms in UK schools, influencing national assessment policy and teacher professional development for over a decade.

known_uses.name: Automated progress checks in e-learning platforms
known_uses.note: Adaptive learning platforms embed formative checkpoints after every concept module — quiz questions, exercise sets, mastery gates — and adjust the learner's path based on performance, operationalising Black and Wiliam's loop at scale.

known_failure_modes.note: Anti-pattern: checkpoints are run on schedule but instruction does not change based on results. The checkpoint becomes a ritual with no feedback loop — learners sense the disconnect and stop engaging honestly with the probe.

Anti-pattern: the only feedback returned to the learner is a score or grade. Black and Wiliam's research is explicit that grades without task-specific guidance on improvement do not improve learning and can actively harm motivation.

Anti-pattern: so many checkpoints are inserted that assessment time crowds out learning time, and learners experience checkpoint fatigue. Formative checkpoints must be minimal — their purpose is to generate a decision signal, not to be a comprehensive measure.

patterns.ecosystem: education, professional development, e-learning, autonomous-agent training

sources.title: Inside the Black Box: Raising Standards Through Classroom Assessment
sources.quote: There is a body of firm evidence that formative assessment is an essential component of classroom work and that its development can raise standards of achievement. We know of no other way of raising standards for which such a strong prima facie case can be made.
sources.note: Black, P., & Wiliam, D. (1998). Phi Delta Kappan, 80(2), 139–144. The landmark review of 250+ studies on formative assessment. Established the evidence base and defined the core feedback-loop model.

sources.title: Inside the Black Box: Classic Education Gold from Wiliam and Black — Teacher Head
sources.quote: Feedback to any pupil should be about the particular qualities of his or her work, with advice on what he or she can do to improve, and should avoid comparisons with other pupils.
sources.note: Quotes from Black & Wiliam 1998 reproduced in a practitioner review, confirming the task-specific, non-comparative feedback principle.

sources.title: Revisiting Inside the Black Box 20 Years On — Corwin Connect
sources.quote: For formative assessment to be productive, pupils should be trained in self-assessment so that they can understand the main purposes of their learning and thereby grasp what they need to do to achieve.
sources.note: Black & Wiliam 1998 quote on learner self-assessment, reproduced in the 20th anniversary retrospective. Confirms the self-assessment principle as original to the 1998 paper.

diagram.mermaid:
flowchart TD
    A[Specify checkpoint objective
What evidence of understanding do I need?] --> B[Design minimal targeted probe
Smallest check that generates reliable signal]
    B --> C[Administer low-stakes
Anonymous or group mode; no grades]
    C --> D[Interpret evidence
Who understands? Who has which misconception?]
    D --> E{Gap found?}
    E -- Yes --> F[Act: reteach / target feedback / slow down]
    F --> G[Close loop with learner
Task-specific guidance on what to do next]
    G --> A
    E -- No --> H[Advance to next objective]
    H --> A

patterns.example_scenario: A software engineering instructor finishes explaining database indexing and suspects learners have conflated 'an index speeds every query' with 'an index speeds queries on the indexed column'. Rather than waiting for the end-of-week assignment, she runs a two-minute checkpoint: learners vote anonymously on a poll — 'Which of these three queries will the index help?' — with one correct answer and two plausible distractors. Results show 60% chose a distractor — a query on a non-indexed column. Instead of moving to the next topic, she reteaches the single concept of column-specificity, works one counterexample, then re-polls. Results shift to 85% correct. She also returns written feedback to the 15% still wrong, telling them their answer assumed the index covers all columns and asking them to check which column the WHERE clause uses. Three learners who self-assessed as confident before the checkpoint now flag the concept as shaky. The checkpoint trained their self-assessment accuracy alongside the content.

patterns.name: Guided Discovery Learning
patterns.summary: The instructor designs an environment where learners explore and find patterns themselves, rather than being told conclusions. Scaffolding keeps exploration productive without removing the discovery. The teacher asks guiding questions, provides structured materials, breaks goals into smaller steps, and highlights key points. But the learner does the inferring. Jerome Bruner argued that knowledge discovered through one's own exploration is retained better and transferred more readily than knowledge received by instruction. The instructor is essential; this is not free play.
patterns.intent: Build durable, transferable knowledge by letting learners discover structure themselves within a carefully designed and scaffolded environment.
patterns.when_to_apply: Apply when the target knowledge has underlying structure the learner can detect through examples. Apply when the goal is transfer to novel situations rather than recall. Apply when the learner's motivation benefits from agency. Do not apply when precise procedural compliance is required immediately. Discovery learning is slower and requires tolerance for productive error.

inputs.name: Designed exploratory environment
inputs.description: Materials, problems, cases, or data arranged so that the target structure is discoverable — the learner can find it by exploring, but it is not handed to them explicitly.

inputs.name: Scaffolding plan
inputs.description: A set of guiding questions, step-down hints, examples, and encouragement the instructor will deploy as the learner encounters difficulty — enough support to keep exploration productive, not so much that the discovery is made for the learner.

outputs.name: More capable learner
outputs.description: A learner who has discovered a principle, pattern, or structure themselves and can therefore apply it to unfamiliar variations — not just recall it in the form it was first encountered.

outputs.name: Self-discovered principle or structure
outputs.description: A generalisation the learner articulated themselves through exploration — the masterpiece — which they own because they found it, making it both more memorable and more transferable.

steps.name: Design the discovery environment
steps.description: Select or create materials, problems, or cases in which the target structure is present and detectable. Sequence them so that simpler cases make the pattern visible before more complex cases test it. The instructor's design work is the hardest part of this move.

steps.name: Launch exploration with a clear but open question
steps.description: Give the learner a genuine question to explore — specific enough to focus inquiry, open enough that the answer must be found rather than looked up. The question should make the learner curious, not anxious.

steps.name: Scaffold without revealing
steps.description: As the learner explores, provide guidance through questions and hints rather than answers. If the learner is stuck, ask what they notice, what is similar to something they already know, or what would happen if they tried a variation. Model thinking-out-loud, not conclusions.

steps.name: Surface and consolidate the discovery
steps.description: When the learner names a pattern or principle themselves, help them articulate it precisely and test it against additional cases. The consolidation step prevents the discovery from remaining a vague feeling — it becomes a named, testable claim the learner owns.

principles: The instructor is essential — guided discovery is not free exploration; the design of the environment and the scaffolding are active teaching acts.
Discovery that the learner makes themselves transfers better than knowledge the instructor states — the act of finding it is part of what makes it stick.
Productive struggle is the mechanism — the learner must be allowed to be confused before the pattern becomes clear; removing confusion too early removes the learning.
patterns.threshold: The learner can apply the discovered principle to a new problem type not covered in the original exploration, without scaffolding — demonstrating transfer, not just recall.
patterns.masterpiece: A self-articulated principle or model, stated in the learner's own words, that the learner tested against at least two novel cases they had not encountered during the original exploration — evidence of genuine discovery rather than surface pattern-matching.

known_uses.name: LOGO programming for children
known_uses.note: Papert's LOGO environment applied Bruner-style guided discovery: children explored turtle geometry and discovered mathematical principles through programming, not through instruction.

known_uses.name: Autonomous agent curriculum design
known_uses.note: Structured task progressions in agent training where harder sub-goals are only accessible after the agent has discovered prerequisite behaviours — scaffolded exploration at the curriculum level.

known_failure_modes.note: Anti-pattern: removing all instructor guidance and letting the learner explore without structure. Pure unguided discovery is less effective than direct instruction for most learners — the 'guide' in guided discovery is load-bearing.

Anti-pattern: the instructor's scaffolding escalates to directly revealing the answer when the learner struggles, replacing the discovery with instruction. The principle is discovered for the learner rather than by the learner, removing the transfer benefit.

sources.title: The Act of Discovery — Jerome S. Bruner (1961)
sources.note: Bruner, J.S. (1961). The Act of Discovery. Harvard Educational Review, 31, 21–32. Foundational paper establishing the value of learner-driven discovery over direct instruction.

sources.title: Toward a Theory of Instruction — Jerome S. Bruner (1966)
sources.quote: students should discover for themselves the structure of the subject they are studying
sources.note: Bruner, J.S. (1966). Toward a Theory of Instruction. Cambridge, MA: Harvard University Press. Paraphrase of core claim confirmed by multiple secondary sources; direct quote from simplypsychology.org secondary synthesis.

sources.title: Jerome Bruner Theory of Cognitive Development — Simply Psychology
sources.quote: Going beyond the information given
sources.note: Bruner's phrase for the goal of discovery learning: learners should be able to extrapolate beyond what was explicitly presented. Confirmed as direct Bruner phrasing by Simply Psychology synthesis.

diagram.mermaid:
flowchart TD
    A[Instructor designs
exploration environment] --> B[Open question posed
to learner]
    B --> C[Learner explores
cases and materials]
    C --> D{Pattern noticed?}
    D -- No --> E[Instructor scaffolds
question / hint / variation]
    E --> C
    D -- Yes --> F[Learner articulates principle
in own words]
    F --> G[Test against novel cases
not in original set]
    G --> H[More capable learner
+ self-discovered principle]

patterns.example_scenario: A data science instructor wants a group of junior analysts to understand why log-transforming skewed data before regression improves model fit. Rather than explaining this, she prepares three datasets: one normally distributed, one moderately skewed, one severely skewed, all with the same underlying linear relationship to the target. She asks the group to fit a linear model to each and report their R-squared values. The models on the skewed datasets perform poorly. She asks: 'What is different about those datasets? Try transforming the skewed variable — you choose how — and refit.' The group tries square root and log transforms. Log transformation on the severe skew dataset jumps R-squared from 0.31 to 0.79. The instructor asks them what they changed and why they think it helped. The group articulates the principle themselves: when a variable spans several orders of magnitude, regression on the raw scale is dominated by extreme values; compressing the scale makes the linear relationship visible. They now own this principle through discovery. On a new dataset two weeks later they apply log transformation without being prompted.

patterns.name: Hackathon
patterns.summary: An intensive, time-boxed event — typically 24 to 72 hours — in which individuals or small teams build and ship a functional artefact from scratch against a shared theme or constraint. The word is a portmanteau of 'hack' (exploratory programming) and 'marathon.' The format was first documented in 1999. OpenBSD held a cryptographic development hackathon in Calgary on June 4, 1999, and Sun Microsystems ran a Java coding event at JavaOne in the same month. The defining educational property of a hackathon is compression: it forces learners to make real decisions under real time pressure, demonstrating whether they can actually ship — not just discuss, plan, or prototype. Research shows hackathons produce strong gains in collaborative skill and tool fluency; in one study, 63% of developers reported learning new tools faster through hackathons than through formal instruction.
patterns.intent: Demonstrate and develop the ability to ship a functional artefact under real time pressure and constraint — replacing theoretical competence with demonstrated delivery capability.
patterns.when_to_apply: Apply when the learning goal is demonstrated delivery capability, not conceptual understanding. Use a hackathon when learners have enough foundational skill to be productive — a hackathon is not a tutorial. The goal must be to surface gaps in integration and execution, not in basic concepts. The time pressure and public demo must be genuine, not theatrical. In AI upskilling, hackathons are most valuable at the transition from 'I understand this' to 'I can ship this.' No amount of additional reading or tutorial completion can substitute for that transition.

inputs.name: Shared theme or constraint
inputs.description: A problem space, dataset, technology stack, or challenge brief that all participants work within — narrow enough to enable comparison across teams, broad enough to allow diverse approaches.

inputs.name: Time box (24–72 hours)
inputs.description: A fixed, non-negotiable deadline. The time pressure is not punitive — it is the mechanism that forces learners to make scope decisions, accept imperfection, and ship rather than polish indefinitely.

inputs.name: Team of 2–5 participants
inputs.description: Small enough for every member to be actively building; large enough for genuine skill complementarity. Solo hackathon entry is possible but loses the collaborative learning dimension.

inputs.name: Public demo or submission
inputs.description: A required end-point where teams present or submit what they built — functional or not. The public accountability of the demo is what makes the hackathon a delivery event rather than a sandbox.

outputs.name: A more capable learner
outputs.description: A learner who has experienced the full cycle of scoping, deciding, building under pressure, and shipping — and who now knows specifically where their execution gaps are, not just their conceptual gaps.

outputs.name: Functional shipped artefact
outputs.description: The masterpiece — a working (or demonstrably attempted) product produced within the time box. Even a partially functional artefact is evidence of real decisions made under real constraints, which is the learning target.

steps.name: Kick off with the challenge brief
