flowchart TD
    A[Challenge brief
theme, constraint, evaluation criteria] --> B[Form teams
2-5 members]
    B --> C[Scope to minimum shippable slice
first 10% of time box]
    C --> D[Build cycle
1-2 hour increments]
    D --> E{Demoable slice?}
    E -- No --> F[Cut scope
not add features]
    F --> D
    E -- Yes --> G[Extend if time allows
or stabilise current slice]
    G --> H[Public demo
live functional artefact]
    H --> I[Debrief within 24h
scope decisions + transferable lessons]
    I --> J[More capable learner
+ shipped artefact masterpiece]

patterns.example_scenario: A 48-hour AI hackathon is held at the end of a six-week ML upskilling cohort. The challenge brief is given at 9am Friday: build a tool that helps non-technical staff in a hospital administration team classify incoming patient complaints by urgency without reading each one in full. Teams of three have access to a labelled dataset of 2,000 anonymised complaints, an LLM API with a spending cap of $20, and a shared deployment environment. By Friday noon, most teams have over-scoped: they plan custom fine-tuning, a comparison dashboard, and a feedback loop. The instructor does a scope check: 'What is the minimum artefact you need to demo by Sunday noon?' Two teams cut to a simple prompt-classification pipeline with a confidence threshold and a manual review queue for low-confidence cases. One team keeps the fine-tuning plan and ships nothing by Sunday. The two teams that cut scope present live demos to a panel that includes the hospital administration team manager. Her response — 'This is exactly the problem, and I can see this working' — is the learning that no slide deck about classification could produce. The Sunday debrief extracts the lesson: scope to the minimum slice that demonstrates the core value proposition; everything else is optional.

patterns.name: Immersive Drill
patterns.summary: Place learners inside a simulated environment — VR headset, realistic scenario, high-stakes stress — so they build muscle memory before touching live systems. Works especially well for roles where a mistake is costly or embarrassing. The simulation is scored in real time, the debrief surfaces what surprised learners, and the learner repeats until they hit the pass threshold. Walmart cut Pickup Tower training from 8 hours to 15 minutes without sacrificing proficiency using this pattern at scale across 4,700 locations.
patterns.intent: Build reliable behavioural skill for high-stakes AI-assisted moments by placing learners inside a scored simulation of the real situation — so muscle memory forms before the real event, not during it.
patterns.when_to_apply: Apply when the target skill involves a moment that is high-stakes, time-pressured, or embarrassing to fail in live conditions — handling an AI-generated escalation, operating a new AI tool under deadline, reviewing an AI draft in front of a client. Also apply when travel or headcount makes traditional instructor-led training prohibitively expensive at scale. Use when the org has at least 3-5 recurring scenario archetypes that represent the majority of real job moments. Do not apply for purely conceptual learning where there is no behavioural component to practise, or when the learner has zero prior conceptual grounding and needs orientation first.

inputs.name: Scenario archetypes
inputs.description: Three to five recurring real-job situations that capture the most common or highest-stakes AI-assisted moments the learner will face. Each scenario must have a clear decision point, a correct action path, and realistic failure paths.

inputs.name: Simulation environment
inputs.description: A VR headset setup, browser-based simulation, or high-fidelity role-play scaffold that replicates the sensory and social conditions of the real moment closely enough to trigger genuine stress or uncertainty.

inputs.name: Scoring rubric
inputs.description: Clear criteria for what counts as a pass at each decision point in the scenario, so the system can score in real time and the debrief is grounded in objective evidence rather than facilitator impression.

outputs.name: More capable learner
outputs.description: A learner who has encountered the target high-stakes moment, made decisions under realistic pressure, received immediate scored feedback, and repeated until they hit the pass threshold — arriving at live conditions with formed muscle memory rather than untested intent.

outputs.name: Pass/fail completion record
outputs.description: A scored record of the learner's simulation runs — the masterpiece evidence that the threshold was met and the learner is cleared to operate in the live environment without supervision for the practised scenario types.

steps.name: Design scenario archetypes
steps.description: Define 3-5 scenario types drawn from real incident data, job-task analysis, or subject-matter expert review. Each archetype must represent a moment the learner will actually face, not a synthetic example. Include at least one scenario where the AI tool produces a wrong or misleading output that the learner must catch.

steps.name: Deploy the simulation
steps.description: Provision the simulation environment to every learner site — VR headsets shipped, browser sim linked, or role-play guides distributed. The learner completes the scenario alone or in a small cohort of 2-4 people. The system scores choices at each decision point without interrupting the experience.

steps.name: Run the scored experience
steps.description: The learner works through the scenario under realistic time pressure and sensory conditions. They receive no coaching mid-run. The simulation records every decision and the time taken to make it.

steps.name: Debrief immediately after
steps.description: Within 15 minutes of completing the run, review the scored decisions together. Surface what surprised the learner, where they hesitated, and what they would do differently. The debrief is the primary learning mechanism — the simulation is the trigger that generates the experience the debrief processes.

steps.name: Repeat until threshold is met
steps.description: Track pass/fail rate across the cohort. Any learner below threshold repeats the scenario — on the same or a variant scenario — until they reach 90% pass. Rotation across scenario variants prevents memorising the path rather than internalising the skill.

principles: Muscle memory forms through repetition under realistic pressure, not through instruction about what to do under pressure.
The debrief is the learning event; the simulation is the raw material for the debrief.
Scale is not an obstacle — once the scenario is built, shipping a headset costs less than flying a trainer.
patterns.threshold: 90% of learners in the cohort pass the target scenario on a variant run (not the memorised path) without coaching intervention.
patterns.masterpiece: A scored pass record on a variant scenario — evidence that the learner can handle the real moment, not just the practised one. For AI-specific simulations, this means correctly identifying and recovering from at least one AI error scenario they have not seen before.

known_uses.name: Walmart VR Training — Strivr Platform
known_uses.note: 2.2 million associates across 4,700+ locations; 60+ immersive experiences. Training time cut from 8 hours to 15 minutes. Associates trained in VR outperformed non-VR peers 70% of the time with 10-15% higher scores.

known_uses.name: ArborXR Walmart Blueprint
known_uses.note: Secondary reporting on the scale and headset rollout strategy: 17,000+ headsets across 4,600 stores.

known_failure_modes.note: Anti-pattern: running the same scenario path every time so learners memorise the correct sequence rather than internalising the skill. Always rotate to a variant scenario for the threshold assessment.

Anti-pattern: treating the simulation as the learning and skipping the debrief to save time. Without the debrief, the experience produces habit but not understanding — learners pass the drill but cannot adapt when the real situation differs from the scenario.

diagram.mermaid:
flowchart TD
    A[Design 3-5 scenario archetypes
from real job moments] --> B[Deploy simulation environment
VR headset or browser sim]
    B --> C[Learner runs scored scenario
no coaching mid-run]
    C --> D[Immediate debrief
15 min post-run]
    D --> E{Threshold met?
90% pass}
    E -- No --> F[Repeat on variant scenario]
    F --> C
    E -- Yes --> G[Masterpiece: scored pass record
cleared for live operation]

patterns.example_scenario: A retail bank is rolling out an AI tool that flags suspicious transactions for human review. Tellers must learn when to override the AI flag and when to escalate. Flying trainers to 200 branches would cost six figures and take three months. Instead, the L&D team builds four scenario archetypes. These are a false positive on a regular customer, a genuine fraud the AI missed, an ambiguous case that needs a customer question, and a high-pressure moment with a long queue. Each teller gets a 20-minute browser-based simulation on their branch terminal. The simulation scores their decisions in real time. Immediately after, a 15-minute group debrief surfaces the two archetypes where the cohort hesitated most. Any teller below 80% on the scored run repeats a variant scenario the following day. After two weeks, 94% of tellers have hit threshold — with no trainer travel and no disruption to branch hours.

patterns.name: Jigsaw Classroom
patterns.summary: Students are divided into home groups, with each member assigned a different piece of the overall topic. Each goes to an expert group — everyone assigned the same piece — to study and master it, then returns to the home group to teach their piece to the others. Like a jigsaw puzzle, no single student has the full picture, so every piece — and every student who holds it — is essential for the group to understand the whole. Invented by Elliot Aronson and colleagues in Austin, Texas in 1971 as an urgent response to racial tension in newly desegregated schools. The structural feature that made it work was the elimination of competition: each student had information the others needed, so cooperation was the rational strategy rather than a moral exhortation.
patterns.intent: Distribute knowledge across learners such that every person's expertise is genuinely necessary for the group's understanding, making cooperation the rational strategy and peer teaching the primary learning mechanism.
patterns.when_to_apply: Apply when a topic can be genuinely partitioned into pieces of comparable depth that together form the whole, and when reducing intra-group competition is a design goal alongside knowledge coverage. Use when the learner population includes members who tend to be passive in traditional group work — the jigsaw structure makes passivity structurally costly to the group, not just socially uncomfortable. Less effective for topics that cannot be partitioned without each piece being meaningless in isolation, or when integration across pieces is so tight that teaching the parts separately produces false understanding.

inputs.name: Topic partitionable into N comparable pieces
inputs.description: A subject area that can be divided into sections of roughly equal depth and importance — each piece substantial enough to require real study, and each essential to understanding the whole. A topic that partitions into one hard piece and several thin ones produces expert groups of unequal value.

inputs.name: Groups sized to match the partition
inputs.description: Home groups of N members, one per topic piece, and expert groups of the same size (one member from each home group assigned the same piece). The structure breaks down if group sizes are mismatched to the partition.

outputs.name: More capable learner
outputs.description: A learner who has mastered one piece of the topic deeply (through expert group study) and has learned the remaining pieces through peer teaching — more engaged with each piece than passive reading would produce, because they both taught one and were genuinely dependent on others for the rest.

outputs.name: Whole-topic account assembled from peer teaching
outputs.description: The home group's combined understanding of the full topic — the masterpiece — assembled from each member's expert-group mastery and teaching, which is demonstrably richer than what any individual could have produced from reading the whole topic alone.

steps.name: Partition the topic and assign expert pieces
steps.description: Divide the topic into N pieces of comparable depth. Form home groups of N members. Assign each member one piece — the piece they will become the expert on for their home group. Every member of the home group holds a different piece; no two members share the same assignment.

steps.name: Expert group study
steps.description: All members assigned the same piece meet as an expert group to study it together — not just individually. The expert group's job is to understand their piece well enough to teach it to people who have not seen it. Expert groups should discuss, not just read in parallel.

steps.name: Return to home group and teach
steps.description: Each member returns to their home group and teaches their piece to the others. Each member's piece is the only source of that information available to the home group, so the teaching is not a courtesy — it is genuinely necessary. The listener asks questions; the teacher must answer them without the expert group to fall back on.

steps.name: Assess whole-topic understanding
steps.description: Follow up with individual assessment on the whole topic — not just the piece each student taught. This step confirms that the peer teaching produced genuine learning, and it creates accountability for the listening as well as the teaching step.

principles: Structural necessity, not moral exhortation — the jigsaw works because each student's piece is genuinely needed by the others, not because the instructor asks students to cooperate. Design the partition so that cooperation is the rational strategy.

Expert group quality determines home group outcomes — the teaching step in the home group is only as good as the understanding developed in the expert group. The expert group meeting is not optional preparation, it is the learning that enables the teaching.
patterns.threshold: Each home group member can answer questions about pieces they did not study directly — the peer teaching transferred genuine understanding, not just bullet points — demonstrated by individual whole-topic assessment scores.
patterns.masterpiece: Individual assessment results showing that all home group members understand all pieces of the topic — including the pieces they only learned through peer teaching — confirming that the cooperative interdependence produced real learning transfer.

known_uses.name: Elliot Aronson — Austin, Texas, 1971
known_uses.quote: My graduate students and I had invented the jigsaw strategy that year, as a matter of absolute necessity to help defuse an explosive situation.
known_uses.note: Aronson invented jigsaw in 1971 in Austin during school desegregation. The formal publication is Aronson, E., Blaney, N., Stephan, C., Sikes, J., and Snapp, M. (1978). The Jigsaw Classroom. Sage.

known_failure_modes.note: The anti-pattern of expert groups that spend their time reading individually rather than discussing and building shared understanding. Expert group members who have not truly mastered their piece produce home group teaching sessions that transmit bullet points rather than understanding — the peer teaching mechanism transfers only as deep as the expert group's preparation.

The anti-pattern of partitioning the topic into pieces of unequal depth, so that some expert groups have substantive material to master and teach while others have thin coverage. Unequal pieces undermine the structural necessity that makes jigsaw work: if one piece is trivially easy, its holder is not genuinely essential to the home group's understanding.

The anti-pattern of omitting the individual whole-topic assessment, removing accountability for the listening step. Without individual assessment on the whole topic, learners can free-ride on the teaching step — attending without genuinely learning what is being taught — because there is no cost to passive listening.

sources.title: The Jigsaw Classroom — official site (Elliot Aronson)
sources.quote: My graduate students and I had invented the jigsaw strategy that year, as a matter of absolute necessity to help defuse an explosive situation.

sources.title: The Jigsaw Classroom (Aronson, Blaney, Stephan, Sikes, Snapp, 1978)
sources.quote: Jigsaw is a cooperative learning technique in which students teach part of the regular curriculum to a small group of their peers.

diagram.mermaid:
flowchart TD
    A[Topic partitioned into N pieces
home groups formed] --> B[Each member assigned
one piece as expert topic]
    B --> C[Expert groups meet
all holders of same piece study together]
    C --> D[Expert group mastery
deep understanding of their piece]
    D --> E[Return to home group
each member teaches their piece]
    E --> F[Home group assembles
whole-topic understanding]
    F --> G[Individual whole-topic assessment
confirms peer teaching transferred]
    G --> H[More capable learner
deep on own piece, solid on all others]

patterns.example_scenario: A high school history class is studying the causes of the First World War. The teacher divides the topic into four pieces: nationalism, the alliance system, imperial rivalry, and the assassination at Sarajevo. Home groups of four are formed. Each member is assigned one cause to become expert on. The four students assigned 'the alliance system' meet as an expert group. They read, discuss, and work through why the alliance structure meant a small conflict could become a continental war. They test each other until they can explain it clearly. They return to their home groups. The student who studied alliances teaches it; the others ask questions; she must answer without the expert group. The student who studied nationalism teaches next. After four rounds, each home group has assembled an account of all four causes. Individual essays show that students understand all four causes — including the ones they did not study directly — more deeply than they would have from reading all four sections alone. The teaching-and-questioning cycle forced genuine understanding at each step.

patterns.name: Learn in the Flow
aliases: learning in the flow of work
patterns.summary: Deliver short AI skill nudges — under 10 minutes — directly inside the tools people already use: Slack, Teams, a CRM, an LMS overlay. Learning happens at the moment of need rather than during time set aside for training. The global microlearning market reached $1.55 billion in 2024 and is growing at 13.5% per year; 91% of L&D professionals say continuous learning is more important than ever. Spaced repetition built into the delivery — resurfacing the same concept at 3, 7, and 21 days — turns a single nudge into a lasting change in behaviour.
patterns.intent: Build AI skills without pulling people away from their work by embedding short, relevant learning nudges directly into the tools and moments where the skill is needed.
patterns.when_to_apply: Apply when learners cannot or will not carve out dedicated training time — the most common condition in operational roles. Apply when the target skill is contextual and benefits from being practised at the moment of need (writing a prompt, reviewing an AI draft, catching a hallucination). Apply after a cohort course or self-paced programme to sustain and reinforce learning through spaced repetition. Do not apply as the sole learning mechanism for complex conceptual skills that require sustained attention — microlearning reinforces; it rarely initiates. Do not apply when the nudge channel (Slack, Teams) is already overloaded and additional messages will be ignored or muted.

inputs.name: High-frequency work moments
inputs.description: Three to five specific points in the learner's daily work where an AI skill nudge would be immediately useful — identified through task analysis or job shadowing, not guessed from a job description.

inputs.name: Micro-lesson library
inputs.description: A library of 2-5 minute lessons, each covering one concept, one example, and one practice prompt. Each lesson must be completable without leaving the tool that triggered it.

inputs.name: Delivery integration
inputs.description: A technical integration that places the nudge inside the tool at the relevant moment — a Teams bot, a Slack channel, a Copilot sidebar tip, or an LMS overlay triggered by job activity.

outputs.name: More capable learner
outputs.description: A learner who has encountered the target concept at the moment it was relevant, practised it immediately in context, and had it reinforced at spaced intervals — producing retention that a one-time session cannot match.

outputs.name: Spaced reinforcement record
outputs.description: A record of nudge delivery, completion, and click-through depth — showing which concepts have been reinforced and which are due for the next spaced repetition interval.

steps.name: Identify the high-frequency moments
steps.description: Map the learner's daily work to find 3-5 moments where an AI skill would be immediately useful. Shadow a representative sample of learners or run a 10-minute job-task interview. Moments that are recurring, consequential, and currently handled without AI are the best targets.

steps.name: Build micro-lessons for each moment
steps.description: Create a 2-5 minute lesson for each target moment: one concept, one worked example from the learner's own job context, one practice prompt they can try immediately. Keep lessons completable without leaving the tool. Avoid multi-concept lessons — one concept per nudge, always.

steps.name: Integrate delivery into the tool
steps.description: Wire the nudge to the moment: a Teams bot that triggers when a meeting summary is opened, a Slack message that fires when someone posts in the #ai-tools channel, a Copilot sidebar tip that appears when a draft is generated. The delivery must feel like part of the tool, not an interruption from outside it.

steps.name: Apply spaced repetition
steps.description: Resurface the same concept at day 3, day 7, and day 21 after first delivery. Each resurfacing is slightly different — a new example, a harder variant of the practice prompt — but covers the same underlying concept. Spaced repetition is the mechanism that converts a single nudge into a durable behaviour change.

steps.name: Track and act on completion data
steps.description: Monitor completion rate and click-through depth per nudge. Nudges with low completion are either hitting the wrong moment or covering a concept the learner does not yet have the context to apply. Fix the trigger or the sequencing, not the content length.

principles: The best moment to learn a skill is the moment you need it — design delivery around work moments, not around a training calendar.
One concept per nudge, always — a 5-minute lesson with three concepts is three lessons that have not been separated yet.
Spaced repetition is the multiplier — a nudge without follow-up repetition produces a 60% forgetting rate within a week.
patterns.threshold: The target concept has been delivered at the relevant work moment, completed by the learner, and reinforced through at least the day-7 spaced repetition interval — with completion rate above 60% across the cohort.
patterns.masterpiece: A behaviour change observable in the work itself — the learner now writes prompts differently, catches AI errors faster, or handles the target work moment without reverting to the pre-training approach. Measured by before/after performance data on the specific work moment, not by nudge completion rate alone.

known_uses.name: 5Mins.ai Microlearning Platform
known_uses.note: AI-personalised delivery from a library of 20,000+ micro-lessons by 200+ industry experts. Integrates with productivity tools for in-flow delivery.

known_uses.name: MaxLearn: Learning in the Flow of Work
known_uses.note: Describes the architectural pattern for embedding learning triggers into CRM, collaboration tools, and performance dashboards.

known_failure_modes.note: Anti-pattern: sending too many nudges to too many channels simultaneously, causing learners to mute or ignore the delivery mechanism. The nudge must be rare enough to feel relevant, not so frequent it becomes noise.
Anti-pattern: building micro-lessons that cover multiple concepts in a single delivery. Each additional concept halves retention. Split ruthlessly until each nudge covers exactly one actionable idea.

diagram.mermaid:
flowchart TD
    A[Identify 3-5 high-frequency
work moments] --> B[Build micro-lesson
per moment
1 concept, 1 example, 1 practice]
    B --> C[Integrate nudge into tool
at relevant moment]
    C --> D[Learner receives nudge
in context]
    D --> E[Learner completes
2-5 min lesson]
    E --> F[Day 3: spaced repetition
new example, same concept]
    F --> G[Day 7: second repetition]
    G --> H[Day 21: third repetition]
    H --> I[Behaviour change
observable in work itself]

patterns.example_scenario: A financial services firm rolls out Microsoft Copilot to 1,400 analysts. A two-day classroom training produces a 40% adoption rate after six weeks — the other 60% opened Copilot once, felt uncertain, and stopped using it. The L&D team switches approach: they shadow 12 analysts for a day each and identify four recurring moments where Copilot would help — summarising long client emails, drafting first-pass reports, searching regulatory documents, and cleaning data tables in Excel. For each moment they build a 3-minute nudge: one concept ('here is how Copilot summarises email threads'), one worked example from a real anonymised client email, one practice prompt the analyst can copy and try immediately. The nudges fire via a Teams bot when each moment is detected — Copilot opened in Outlook triggers the email-summary nudge; Excel opened with a large table triggers the data-cleaning nudge. Each concept is resurfaced at day 3, 7, and 21 with a harder variant. After 8 weeks, Copilot active usage is at 73% — and the analysts who completed all four nudge sequences are the highest users.

patterns.name: Learning by Doing
patterns.summary: Learning happens through purposeful action on real problems, not through passive reception of information. The learner is given something to do — not something to learn — and the doing demands thinking. Education connects to students' genuine interests so that success in the activity depends on developing the skill, making learning intrinsically motivated rather than externally rewarded. Associated with John Dewey, grounded in his pragmatist philosophy of education.
patterns.intent: Produce genuine learning by immersing the learner in purposeful activity on a real problem where thinking is required and success is visible.
patterns.when_to_apply: Apply when a learner needs a skill that can only be internalised through practice — not when knowledge transfer alone is sufficient. Also apply when motivation is low and abstract instruction has stalled: connecting the skill to a real problem the learner cares about restarts engagement. Most powerful when the learner can see a direct link between their action and the outcome.

inputs.name: Real task or problem
inputs.description: A genuine activity that requires the target skill to succeed — not a drill designed around the skill in isolation, but a meaningful task where the skill serves a purpose the learner can see.

inputs.name: Learner with a stake in the outcome
inputs.description: A learner who has some interest in solving the problem — however small — so that success in the activity creates an intrinsic reason to develop the skill.

outputs.name: More capable learner
outputs.description: A learner who has developed the skill through doing rather than hearing about it — with tactile understanding of where the skill is difficult and where it is effective.

outputs.name: Completed work product
outputs.description: A real artefact produced by the learner through the activity — the masterpiece — which demonstrates the skill and anchors the learning to a concrete achievement rather than an abstract certificate.

steps.name: Select a real task that requires the skill
steps.description: Choose or design a genuine problem where the learner must use the target skill to make progress. Avoid synthetic exercises that simulate the skill in isolation — the task should have real stakes, even small ones, so that thinking is genuinely required.

steps.name: Place the learner in the task
steps.description: Give the learner primary responsibility for doing the work. The instructor's role shifts from information source to environment designer and supportive observer. Resist the urge to tell the learner what to do before they have tried.

steps.name: Let difficulty surface and provide targeted support
steps.description: Allow the learner to encounter obstacles in the task. Intervene with just enough support to keep them productive — not so much that you remove the difficulty, which is the source of learning. The difficulty is not a problem to eliminate; it is the mechanism.

steps.name: Connect the action to the principle
steps.description: After the learner has done enough to form an experience, surface the underlying principle together. 'You succeeded here because you traced the data through each stage — that is what the skill of data lineage tracing means in practice.' The principle arrives after the doing, not before.

principles: Give learners something to do, not something to learn — the doing is what generates the learning.
Thinking is demanded by meaningful activity, not by instruction — choose tasks where success depends on genuine reasoning.
The principle is anchored by the experience — explain it after the learner has encountered it, not before.
patterns.threshold: The learner can use the skill on a new problem of similar type without instruction, and can explain why the skill works by reference to their own experience of using it.
patterns.masterpiece: A real completed work product — built, written, debugged, or designed — that the learner produced by doing, which they can point to as evidence of the skill and from which they can reconstruct the principles they used.

known_uses.name: Software coding bootcamps
known_uses.org: Various (Recurse Center, App Academy, Turing School)
known_uses.note: Project-based curriculum in which learners build real applications from week one, developing skills through direct encounter with real problems rather than through lecture sequences.

known_uses.name: Autonomous agent capability acquisition
known_uses.note: Agents trained by executing real tasks in live environments rather than by imitation of recorded behaviour — performance on real tasks, not supervised copying, drives capability growth.

known_failure_modes.note: Anti-pattern: activity without any structured pause for review or sense-making. The learner accumulates task experience but forms no transferable understanding — they can repeat what they did but cannot apply the skill to a novel situation.

Anti-pattern: dropping the learner into a task that exceeds their current capacity with no support, expecting struggle alone to produce learning. Productive struggle requires enough scaffolding that the learner can make progress; pure overwhelm produces discouragement, not growth.

sources.title: Democracy and Education — John Dewey (1916)
sources.quote: They give the pupils something to do, not something to learn; and the doing is of such a nature as to demand thinking, or the intentional noting of connections; learning naturally results.
sources.note: Dewey, J. (1916). Democracy and Education. New York: Macmillan. Core statement of the learn-by-doing principle.

sources.title: Experience and Education — John Dewey (1938)
sources.quote: Every experience enacted and undergone modifies the one who acts and undergoes, while this modification affects...the quality of subsequent experiences.
sources.note: Dewey, J. (1938). Experience and Education. New York: Macmillan. p. 35. Confirms the transformative role of action in learning.

diagram.mermaid:
flowchart TD
    A[Real task requiring the skill
with genuine stakes] --> B[Learner takes primary responsibility
instructor observes]
    B --> C[Difficulty surfaces
obstacles encountered]
    C --> D[Targeted support
just enough to stay productive]
    D --> E[Learner acts through the difficulty
skill applied in context]
    E --> F[Connect action to principle
after the doing, not before]
    F --> G[More capable learner
+ completed work product]

patterns.example_scenario: A data analyst has been told in several workshops about the importance of checking data distributions before fitting a regression model. She understands the advice but does not feel it as urgent. Her manager gives her a real business question: predict next quarter's sales using last year's transaction data. She builds the model without checking distributions and presents results that are off by 40%. She investigates and finds the transaction data is heavily right-skewed by a single outlier quarter. She transforms the data, rebuilds the model, and the predictions become usable. The principle — check distributions before modelling — is now attached to a real failure she can name. In every subsequent project she checks distributions first, without being reminded, because the doing made the principle tangible in a way no lecture had. The masterpiece is the rebuilt model and the documented investigation: a real artefact that she can point to as the origin of the practice.

patterns.name: Learning by Teaching
patterns.summary: The learner teaches the material to someone else — a peer, a student, or even an imagined audience — and in doing so discovers the gaps in their own understanding that passive study concealed. The pedagogical insight is ancient: Seneca wrote 'while we teach, we learn' (docendo discimus) in the 1st century CE. Jean-Pol Martin formalized it as a systematic classroom method (Lernen durch Lehren, LdL) in German schools in the 1980s, and Richard Feynman popularized it as a personal study technique. The protege effect — the finding that preparing to teach material produces stronger retention and understanding than preparing for a personal test on the same material — is well-supported empirically. The mechanism: teaching requires the learner to build an explanation that works for a specific other person. This forces them to surface and resolve their own incomplete understanding rather than paper over it with recognition memory.
patterns.intent: Surface and resolve gaps in a learner's understanding by requiring them to teach the material to another person, because the act of constructing an explanation reveals what the learner does not yet know.
patterns.when_to_apply: Apply after an initial learning phase — when the learner believes they understand the material — as a test and deepener of that understanding. Use when passive review or re-reading would produce false confidence: the learner can recognize correct answers without being able to generate correct explanations. Especially effective for conceptual material where understanding is built out of connected ideas rather than isolated facts, because the gaps in connected understanding surface only when the learner must produce a coherent explanation rather than retrieve a stored answer. Also applicable to autonomous agents: an agent prompted to explain a concept to a simulated novice, then receive a follow-up question, surfaces reasoning gaps faster than one that only verifies correct answers.

inputs.name: A learner who has studied the material once
inputs.description: Someone who believes they understand the topic — the false confidence that learning-by-teaching is designed to test and break.

inputs.name: A real or simulated audience
inputs.description: A peer who genuinely does not understand the material, a student in a structured setting, or — in the Feynman technique — an imagined child audience. The audience must be specific enough that the learner calibrates their explanation to a real person rather than producing a generic monologue.

outputs.name: More capable learner
outputs.description: A learner whose understanding has been deepened by the act of teaching — who has resolved the gaps that passive study concealed and can now generate explanations rather than only recognize correct ones.

outputs.name: Explanation that reveals mastery
outputs.description: The teacher-learner's explanation of the concept — the masterpiece — which demonstrates the quality of their understanding more accurately than any test score. A clear, gap-free explanation of a complex topic to a naive audience is the strongest evidence of genuine mastery.

steps.name: Study the material to the point of believing you understand it
steps.description: Complete initial learning — reading, lecture, practice problems. The important step is reaching the point of apparent understanding. Learning-by-teaching only works as a deepener and gap-finder; it is not a substitute for initial engagement with the material.

steps.name: Attempt to teach it without notes
steps.description: Explain the concept to the real or imagined audience using only what you can retrieve and generate — not what you can recognize when prompted. The constraint of no notes is what forces the gaps to surface: a learner who 'understands' a concept but cannot explain it without referring back to the source does not yet understand it.

steps.name: Locate and name the gaps
steps.description: When the explanation stalls, becomes circular, or cannot answer a follow-up question from the audience, that is a gap. Name it explicitly: 'I cannot explain why this is the case, only that it is.' Named gaps are learnable; vague discomfort is not.

steps.name: Return to the source to fill the gap and re-teach
steps.description: Go back to the material to resolve the named gap. Then re-attempt the explanation — not from the beginning but from the gap. Repeat until the explanation is gap-free. Each iteration of gap-finding and filling deepens the understanding layer that passive study leaves thin.

principles: Teaching exposes what recognition conceals — a learner who can recognize a correct answer has lower-quality understanding than one who can generate a correct explanation. Teaching forces the test that recognition hides.

Gaps must be named to be filled — a learner who notices their explanation is weak but does not name specifically where it breaks down will re-read the material and re-arrive at the same false confidence.
patterns.threshold: The learner can explain the concept to a naive audience without stalling, without circular reasoning, and without referring to notes — and can answer follow-up questions that probe the concept's edge cases.
patterns.masterpiece: A clear, gap-free explanation of the target concept delivered to a real or simulated naive audience — demonstrable by the audience's ability to apply the concept correctly in a new situation based solely on the teacher-learner's explanation.

known_uses.name: Jean-Pol Martin — Lernen durch Lehren (LdL), German schools, 1982–
known_uses.quote: In the early 1980s, Jean-Pol Martin systematically developed the concept of having students teach other in the context of learning French as a foreign language.
known_uses.note: Martin's 1985 dissertation formalized LdL and in 1987 he founded a network of over a thousand teachers implementing the method across subjects.

known_uses.name: Richard Feynman — Feynman Technique (personal study method)
known_uses.quote: The Feynman Technique asks you to explain a concept in simple language as if teaching it to someone with no background.
known_uses.note: The Feynman Technique operationalizes learning-by-teaching as a four-step individual study method: choose a concept, explain it plainly, identify gaps, simplify further.

known_failure_modes.note: The anti-pattern of producing a fluent-sounding explanation that skips over the hard parts — the learner narrates the parts they understand and elides the parts they do not, producing an apparently complete explanation with unexamined holes. The mechanism requires actively hunting for gaps, not performing competence.

The anti-pattern of finding a gap but returning to passive re-reading rather than targeted resolution of the specific named gap. A learner who re-reads the whole chapter after finding one gap is doing avoidance learning — they will re-emerge with the same false confidence about the parts they already understood and the same gap about the part they did not.

sources.title: Learning by Teaching — Wikipedia (Jean-Pol Martin, LdL, Feynman Technique)
sources.quote: In the early 1980s, Jean-Pol Martin systematically developed the concept of having students teach other in the context of learning French as a foreign language.

sources.title: The Feynman Technique: How to Learn Anything by Teaching It (Glasp)
sources.quote: The Feynman Technique asks you to explain a concept in simple language as if teaching it to someone with no background.

diagram.mermaid:
flowchart TD
    A[Learner studies material
reaches apparent understanding] --> B[Attempts to teach
without notes to real or imagined audience]
    B --> C{Explanation
stalls or becomes circular?}
    C -- No --> D[Audience asks follow-up
edge case question]
    D --> E{Answer available?}
    E -- Yes --> F[Explanation complete
masterpiece: gap-free account]
    E -- No: gap found --> G[Name the gap explicitly]
    C -- Yes: gap found --> G
    G --> H[Return to source
target only the named gap]
    H --> B
    F --> I[More capable learner
genuine understanding replaces recognition]

patterns.example_scenario: A data analyst has read three articles about causal inference and believes she understands it. She attempts to explain it to a colleague who works in marketing with no statistics background. She gets through correlation-is-not-causation without trouble. She starts explaining instrumental variables and stalls: she can say 'an instrument is a variable correlated with the treatment but not the outcome' but when her colleague asks 'why does that help us establish causation?' she finds she cannot answer. She notices the gap, names it — 'I understand the definition but not the mechanism' — and goes back to one paper to find the specific answer. She returns, explains it again, this time getting through the mechanism. Her colleague asks: 'So when would you actually use this in practice?' She stalls again. Another named gap. After two iterations, she can explain instrumental variables clearly enough that her colleague can correctly identify whether a proposed analysis in a meeting would require one. The masterpiece is not her understanding — it is the colleague's ability to apply the concept, which would not exist if the analyst had not been forced to produce a gap-free explanation.

patterns.name: Mastery Learning
patterns.summary: Mastery learning is an instructional strategy in which learners must demonstrate a pre-defined level of mastery of each unit before advancing to the next. Unlike time-based instruction, mastery learning treats aptitude as rate of learning rather than a ceiling. Given sufficient time and quality instruction, most learners can reach a high standard. The key mechanism is a cycle of instruction, formative assessment, corrective re-instruction, and re-assessment until mastery is achieved.
patterns.intent: Ensure most learners reach a high standard on each prerequisite unit before advancing, by treating time-to-mastery as the variable rather than the performance ceiling.
patterns.when_to_apply: Apply when the subject matter is hierarchically structured so that each unit genuinely depends on prior mastery. Also use when learners have heterogeneous entry points and learning rates, and the program can flex the time allowed per unit. Avoid pushing all learners through on a fixed schedule. Less suitable for exploratory or creative domains without clear mastery criteria.

inputs.name: Learning objectives with mastery criteria
inputs.description: Each unit must have a clear, measurable mastery threshold — typically 80–90% on a formative assessment — that defines what 'mastered' means before the learner advances.

inputs.name: Formative assessment instrument
inputs.description: A quiz, checklist, or demonstration that quickly reveals whether the learner has reached the mastery threshold, distinct from a final summative exam.

inputs.name: Corrective instruction materials
inputs.description: Alternative explanations, worked examples, or practice problems for learners who do not pass the formative assessment on the first attempt. The correction must differ in approach from the initial instruction — not simply repeating the same content.

inputs.name: Flexible time allocation
inputs.description: A program design that allows faster learners to advance quickly while slower learners take longer on a unit — the critical structural departure from traditional instruction.

outputs.name: More capable learner
outputs.description: A learner who has genuinely mastered each prerequisite unit before moving on, making advanced content accessible rather than confusing.

outputs.name: Mastery record
outputs.description: A per-learner record of which units are mastered, allowing instructors to see exactly where each individual is in the curriculum and what corrective instruction they received.

outputs.name: Reduced achievement gap
outputs.description: Across a cohort, mastery learning compresses the distribution of outcomes by pulling up the lower tail — learners who would traditionally fall behind instead reach mastery with more time and corrective instruction.

steps.name: Define mastery criteria for each unit
steps.description: Before instruction begins, define what mastery means for each unit: which skills or knowledge items must be demonstrated, at what accuracy level, and what the formative assessment will look like. The threshold is typically 80–90% on a criterion-referenced test.

steps.name: Deliver initial instruction
steps.description: Teach the unit using the primary method — lecture, worked examples, reading, interactive exercises. The instruction is designed for the typical learner; it is not yet individualized.

steps.name: Administer formative assessment
steps.description: After the instructional period, assess all learners against the mastery criterion. The assessment must be low-stakes and diagnostic — its purpose is to identify gaps, not assign grades. Learners who meet the threshold advance to the next unit.

steps.name: Provide corrective instruction for non-masters
steps.description: Learners who did not reach the mastery threshold receive alternative instruction targeting their specific gaps. This is not repetition of the original instruction. It uses different examples, media, or explanatory approaches to give the concept another angle.

steps.name: Re-assess and advance
steps.description: After corrective instruction, learners take a parallel formative assessment. Those who now meet the threshold advance. The cycle of correction and re-assessment continues until mastery is achieved. Learners who reached mastery earlier may work on enrichment or peer-teaching activities.

principles: Aptitude predicts rate of learning, not ceiling of achievement: most learners can reach a high standard given enough time and quality instruction.
Corrective instruction must differ from initial instruction: repeating the same explanation to a learner who did not understand it the first time does not help.
Formative assessment is a diagnostic tool, not a judgment: its purpose is to identify the gap so instruction can be adjusted, not to rank learners.
patterns.threshold: The learner achieves the pre-defined mastery criterion (typically 80–90%) on the formative assessment for every unit in the curriculum before receiving a completion credential.
patterns.masterpiece: A completed learning portfolio showing mastery records across all units, including which units required corrective instruction and how many assessment cycles were needed — evidence that the learner did not advance on partial understanding.

known_uses.name: Bloom's original mastery learning trials in Chicago schools (1968–1970s)
known_uses.quote: most students, perhaps over 90 percent, can master what teachers have to teach them
known_uses.note: Bloom's seminal trials showed that mastery learning lifted the lower performers substantially while not disadvantaging faster learners.

known_uses.name: Khan Academy — mastery-based progression
known_uses.note: Khan Academy's skill tree requires demonstrated mastery of each node before unlocking the next, directly implementing Bloom's model at scale.

known_failure_modes.note: Applying mastery criteria without flexing the time allowed defeats the model — learners who need more time are pushed forward unmastered or held back arbitrarily.
Corrective instruction that simply re-presents the original material does not address why the learner did not understand it; the cycle stalls without real correction.
Setting the mastery bar too low (60% or below) produces a false sense of completion and undermines the prerequisite logic that makes subsequent units accessible.

sources.title: Learning for Mastery — Benjamin S. Bloom (1968)
sources.quote: Most students (perhaps over 90 percent) can master what teachers have to teach them, and it is the task of instruction to find the means which will enable students to master the subject under consideration.

sources.title: Closing Achievement Gaps: Revisiting Benjamin S. Bloom's 'Learning for Mastery'
sources.quote: majority of students (more than 90 percent) would achieve successful and rewarding learning

diagram.mermaid:
flowchart TD
  A[Define mastery criteria per unit] --> B[Deliver initial instruction]
  B --> C[Formative assessment]
  C --> D{Mastery threshold met?}
  D -- Yes --> E[Advance to next unit]
  D -- No --> F[Corrective instruction - different approach]
  F --> G[Re-assessment]
  G --> D
  E --> H{All units complete?}
  H -- No --> B
  H -- Yes --> I[More capable learner]

patterns.example_scenario: A company runs a six-unit AI fluency course for non-technical managers. Each unit ends with a ten-question formative quiz; the mastery threshold is 80%. In unit two (prompt engineering basics), eight of thirty learners score below 80%. Those eight receive an alternative set of worked examples using their own domain's vocabulary. The initial instruction used generic examples that did not land. Five pass the re-assessment immediately; three need a second corrective round with a coached practice exercise. All thirty advance to unit three having genuinely mastered the prerequisite. The facilitator records that unit two's generic examples are the binding failure mode to fix for the next cohort.

patterns.name: Mentorship and Coaching
patterns.summary: A more experienced practitioner (mentor or coach) engages one-to-one with a less experienced learner in a sustained relationship aimed at developing the learner's capability and judgment. Mentoring and coaching are related but distinct. Mentors primarily share their own experience and wisdom, building the relationship over months or years to develop the whole practitioner. Coaches primarily use questioning and reflection to draw out the learner's own capability, focusing on specific performance areas over a shorter horizon. Both patterns are older than formal education. Mentoring takes its name from Mentor, the figure entrusted with Odysseus's son in Homer's Odyssey. Both were formalized as distinct pedagogical approaches in the organizational literature of the 1980s and 1990s (Clutterbuck, Parsloe, Whitmore). The pattern also applies to autonomous agent training: an agent that receives sustained, individualized feedback from a human or agent trainer develops faster than one that receives only batch correction.
patterns.intent: Accelerate a learner's development of judgment, not just skill, through a sustained one-to-one relationship where the more experienced party provides individualized guidance unavailable in group or self-directed formats.
patterns.when_to_apply: Apply when the learner needs to develop judgment — knowing when and how to apply skill in novel situations — rather than just acquiring procedures. Mentoring is most effective for long-arc career development and navigating domain-specific wisdom that is not codified anywhere. Coaching is most effective for specific performance gaps where the learner has latent capability that targeted reflection can unlock. Use when no group format can provide the individualization the learner needs. Also appropriate for autonomous agents being developed by human trainers who track the agent's specific failure modes over time rather than providing generic feedback.

inputs.name: Committed mentor or coach with relevant experience
inputs.description: A practitioner who has navigated the domain or challenge the learner faces, is willing to invest sustained time in one-to-one engagement, and can tailor their approach to the specific learner's profile rather than delivering a generic curriculum.

inputs.name: Learner with identified development goals
inputs.description: A learner who can name what they are trying to develop — specific enough to make progress visible, broad enough to require more than a tutorial. Undirected mentorship diffuses into general conversation without development.

inputs.name: Sustained engagement over time
inputs.description: A commitment to multiple sessions over weeks or months — not a one-time consultation. The relationship develops the mentor's or coach's model of the specific learner, which is what makes individualized guidance possible.

outputs.name: More capable learner
outputs.description: A practitioner who has developed judgment calibrated to their specific context — not just acquired a skill list, but built the decision-making model that determines when and how to apply skills in situations the curriculum did not cover.

outputs.name: Individualized development trajectory
outputs.description: A documented or tacit account — the masterpiece — of the learner's specific growth arc: where they started, what their particular obstacles were, what specific interventions moved them, and where they are now. This individualized account is what the mentor or coach uniquely provides.

steps.name: Establish the development goal and baseline
steps.description: At the start of the relationship, the mentor or coach and the learner name what the learner is trying to develop and what the current baseline looks like. Vague goals ('become a better leader') produce vague development. Specific ones ('learn to run difficult stakeholder conversations without losing the room') produce legible progress.

steps.name: Provide individualized guidance on real situations
steps.description: The core of the relationship: the mentor or coach responds to real situations the learner is navigating — not textbook cases. The mentor shares how they handled similar situations and why; the coach asks questions that surface the learner's own reasoning and expose where it breaks down.

steps.name: Build the learner's self-observation capacity
steps.description: Both mentoring and coaching aim to develop the learner's ability to observe and adjust their own practice — not to create dependency on the mentor or coach. The relationship is working when the learner begins to ask themselves the questions the mentor or coach used to ask.

steps.name: Progressively reduce guidance as judgment develops
steps.description: A good mentor or coach tracks the learner's growing capacity and reduces direction as it grows — shifting from providing answers to asking questions, then from asking questions to debriefing the learner's own decisions. The relationship succeeds when it becomes unnecessary.

principles: Individualization is the mechanism — a mentor or coach who delivers the same guidance to every learner is a curriculum, not a mentor. The value is in the tailored response to this specific person's specific situation.

The goal is independence, not reliance — a mentoring or coaching relationship that produces a learner who still needs the mentor to make decisions has failed. The masterpiece is a learner who has internalized the mentor's better questions.
patterns.threshold: The learner can navigate a novel difficult situation in the domain — one they have not encountered before — and produce a post-action account that shows they applied the judgment the relationship was designed to develop, without needing to consult the mentor or coach first.
patterns.masterpiece: A learner whose decision-making in the domain has visibly changed — tracked across real situations over the course of the relationship — in ways attributable to the individualized guidance rather than to experience alone.

known_uses.name: Eric Parsloe — Coaching, Mentoring and Assessing (1992)
known_uses.org: Oxford School of Coaching and Mentoring
known_uses.quote: Coaching is a process that enables learning and development to occur and thus performance to improve.
known_uses.note: Parsloe's 1992 work was among the first to formally distinguish coaching from mentoring as complementary but distinct pedagogical approaches in professional development.

known_uses.name: David Clutterbuck — European developmental mentoring model
known_uses.quote: The European, development-based model of mentoring emphasizes the mentor's experience and wisdom to enable the mentee to become self-reliant and to take ownership of their personal and professional development.
known_uses.note: Clutterbuck distinguished the European developmental mentoring model (which emphasizes learner autonomy) from the US sponsorship model (which emphasizes mentor power), establishing that the goal of mentoring is the learner's independence.

known_failure_modes.note: The anti-pattern of a mentoring relationship that produces a learner who routes all decisions through the mentor rather than developing their own judgment. A mentor who provides answers rather than developing the learner's capacity to generate better answers is creating a dependency, not a more capable practitioner.

The anti-pattern of regular mentoring sessions without named development goals, which drift into general conversation and career networking. Without legible goals, neither the mentor nor the learner can tell whether the relationship is producing development.

The anti-pattern of a coach who delivers the same intervention framework to every learner regardless of their specific profile. Coaching that is not individualized to the specific learner's reasoning patterns and failure modes produces generic development indistinguishable from a group training program.

sources.title: Coaching, Mentoring and Assessing (Eric Parsloe, 1992)
sources.quote: Coaching is a process that enables learning and development to occur and thus performance to improve.

sources.title: Coaching and Mentoring Factsheet (CIPD)
sources.quote: Coaching and mentoring are development approaches based on the use of one-to-one conversations to enhance an individual's skills, knowledge or work performance.

diagram.mermaid:
flowchart TD
    A[Development goal established
baseline named] --> B[Learner navigates real situation]
    B --> C{Mentor or coach?}
    C -- Mentor --> D[Shares own experience
why they chose what they chose]
    C -- Coach --> E[Asks questions
surfaces learner's own reasoning]
    D --> F[Learner reflects
updates judgment]
    E --> F
    F --> G{Judgment
developing?}
    G -- Not yet --> B
    G -- Yes --> H[Reduce guidance
learner self-observes]
    H --> I[More capable learner
independent judgment in domain]

patterns.example_scenario: A mid-career product manager is struggling to run executive stakeholder reviews without losing the room — decisions get relitigated, sponsors check out, and nothing gets shipped. Her coach does not give her a stakeholder management framework. Instead, after each review, they debrief: 'Walk me through the moment when you felt the room shift. What had just happened? What were you tracking when it happened?' Over six sessions, a pattern emerges: the PM is providing context before stating the decision, which gives opponents time to organize objections before hearing what is being decided. The coach names it. The PM revises her structure — decision first, context second. The next review goes differently. Three months into the coaching relationship, the PM is running pre-mortems on her own presentations, asking herself the same questions the coach used to ask, and no longer needing the debriefs to catch the pattern. The masterpiece is not the framework — it is the PM's acquired capacity to observe her own practice and adjust in real time.

patterns.name: Microlearning
patterns.summary: Microlearning delivers a single, focused learning objective in a short, self-contained unit — typically under ten minutes. It can be completed in the flow of work rather than requiring dedicated training time. The canonical definition from Kapp and DeFelice (2019) centres on eliciting a specific, measurable outcome from a brief engagement. Microlearning works best as one tool in a broader learning architecture, reinforcing or extending prior instruction, and it pairs naturally with spaced repetition and retrieval practice for retention.
patterns.intent: Deliver one specific, measurable learning outcome in the shortest engagement sufficient to achieve it, accessible in the flow of work.
patterns.when_to_apply: Apply when the learner needs a specific capability update — a changed process step, a new regulation, a product feature — and cannot be taken offline for a full course. Use to reinforce prior training at spaced intervals or to deliver performance support at the moment of need. Use also to introduce a concept that will be developed later in a fuller session. Do not use as a substitute for foundational skill-building: microlearning cannot teach a complex skill from scratch, only update or reinforce one.

inputs.name: Single, scoped learning objective
inputs.description: One specific, measurable outcome the learner will be able to do after the unit — narrow enough to be meaningfully addressed in a short engagement. If the objective requires two unrelated capabilities, it is two microlearning units.

inputs.name: Media format matched to objective
inputs.description: The format — a 90-second explainer video, a two-question quiz, a single annotated diagram, a worked example with two steps — must be the minimum sufficient to demonstrate and practice the objective, not a compressed version of a longer course.

inputs.name: Delivery at the moment of need
inputs.description: Microlearning is most effective when available exactly when the learner encounters the need — embedded in a workflow tool, triggered by a task in a system, or sent ahead of a specific meeting or decision.

outputs.name: More capable learner
outputs.description: A learner who can perform the specific targeted action or make the targeted decision more accurately than before the unit.

outputs.name: Completed micro-unit
outputs.description: A short, reusable learning artifact — the microlearning unit itself — that can be re-surfaced as a spaced repetition prompt or shared across a team.

steps.name: Define the single learning objective
steps.description: Write one specific, measurable objective, such as: after completing this unit, the learner will be able to identify which of the three customer tiers qualifies for the new discount structure. If the objective cannot be stated in one sentence without conjunctions, split it.

steps.name: Choose the minimum-sufficient format
steps.description: Select the shortest format that can meaningfully demonstrate and practice the objective. A factual update may need only a 60-second video plus one confirmation question. A process step may need a three-panel annotated diagram plus a step-through simulation. Match format to objective, not to convention.

steps.name: Create the micro-unit
steps.description: Produce the content: one focused explanation or demonstration, followed by an active practice element (a question, a matching exercise, a checklist step). The practice element is what makes it learning, not just content delivery.

steps.name: Deliver at the moment of need
steps.description: Surface the unit when the learner is about to perform the relevant task. Good delivery points: in the tool they use for that task, before a meeting where the knowledge applies, or as a triggered prompt in a workflow. Just-in-time delivery maximises application and retention.

steps.name: Measure outcome and schedule follow-up
steps.description: Verify that the objective was met: did the learner answer correctly, complete the step, or apply the capability in the workflow? Schedule a follow-up micro-unit at a spaced interval to check retention and address any gap.

principles: One objective per unit: a microlearning unit that tries to cover multiple objectives covers none of them at the depth needed for the learner to act differently afterward.
Format follows objective: the length and medium of the unit should be determined by what the objective requires, not by a predetermined time box or content template.
Microlearning is a component, not a strategy: it amplifies and reinforces a broader learning architecture; it cannot replace foundational instruction for complex skills.
patterns.threshold: The learner completes the unit and correctly performs or predicts the targeted action on a real or simulated task within 24 hours of completing the unit — the micro-unit produced an observable change in behavior, not just a comprehension check.
patterns.masterpiece: A library of micro-units mapped to specific moments of need in a workflow, demonstrable by showing that a learner who encountered an edge case in production navigated it correctly because a triggered micro-unit surfaced the right guidance at the right time.

known_uses.name: Duolingo — daily lesson format
known_uses.note: Duolingo's five-minute daily lessons are the canonical consumer-facing microlearning implementation, combining microlearning with spaced repetition for retention.

known_uses.name: ATD — Microlearning: Short and Sweet (Kapp & DeFelice, 2019)
known_uses.quote: Microlearning is an instructional unit that provides a short engagement in an activity intentionally designed to elicit a specific outcome from the participant.
known_uses.note: The canonical practitioner definition that moved the field past arbitrary time-box definitions toward outcome-centred design.

known_failure_modes.note: Cutting a two-hour course into twelve ten-minute videos is not microlearning — it is a longer course in smaller pieces. True microlearning scopes each unit to a single objective, not a scheduled time slot.

A micro-unit that delivers explanation without any active element (a question, a simulation step, a checklist) is content consumption, not learning — it does not produce the behavioral change the objective specifies.

Using microlearning as the only learning intervention for a complex skill produces fragmented understanding without the foundational depth to apply isolated micro-facts coherently under real-world conditions.

sources.title: Microlearning: Short and Sweet — Kapp & DeFelice, ATD Press 2019
sources.quote: Microlearning is an instructional unit that provides a short engagement in an activity intentionally designed to elicit a specific outcome from the participant.

sources.title: Microlearning — Wikipedia: origins and definitions
sources.quote: Microlearning refers to a set of compact e-learning modules that are designed to reduce learner fatigue

sources.title: Microlearning in Health Professions Education: Scoping Review — PMC 2019
sources.quote: microlearning is considered to be a modern learning approach that attempts to address the increasing time constraints on learners by segmenting information into short, manageable pieces

diagram.mermaid:
flowchart TD
  A[Define single learning objective] --> B[Choose minimum-sufficient format]
  B --> C[Create micro-unit: explanation + active element]
  C --> D[Deliver at moment of need]
  D --> E[Learner engages - performs active element]
  E --> F{Objective met?}
  F -- Yes --> G[Schedule spaced follow-up]
  F -- No --> H[Surface corrective micro-unit]
  H --> E
  G --> I[More capable learner]

patterns.example_scenario: A SaaS company rolls out a new contract approval workflow affecting 60 account executives. Rather than a mandatory 45-minute training session, the L&D team creates three micro-units. The first is a 90-second screen-capture video showing the three new approval steps, followed by a single scenario question. The second is a two-minute annotated diagram of the exception cases, followed by two decision questions. The third is a 60-second checklist for the most common edge case, delivered as an in-app tooltip triggered the first time each AE opens the approval screen. Completion takes under five minutes total. Two weeks later, a spaced follow-up micro-unit resurfaces the exception cases with two new scenario questions. Error rates on the new workflow drop 70% compared to the previous rollout, which used a recorded webinar that fewer than half the team watched to completion.

patterns.name: Pair Programming
patterns.summary: Two developers share one machine and one keyboard — one drives (writes code), the other navigates (observes, thinks one step ahead, catches errors). The roles switch regularly. Pair programming is one of the twelve core practices of Extreme Programming (Beck, 1999) and the practice that gives XP its most visible identity. As a learning pattern, pairing transfers both explicit knowledge (how to use a library, how to structure a query) and tacit knowledge (how an experienced developer reads an error trace, where they instinctively reach first) through direct side-by-side observation. This is the same mechanism as cognitive apprenticeship but embedded in production work rather than teaching sessions. Pairs produce fewer defects — not because either programmer is smarter, but because the navigator catches errors the driver misses in real time.
patterns.intent: Accelerate skill transfer and reduce defect rate by placing two practitioners at one workstation — making the experienced practitioner's reasoning visible to the less experienced one in the context of real production work.
patterns.when_to_apply: Apply when a junior practitioner needs to absorb the reasoning patterns of a senior one faster than code review alone can deliver. It also applies when a task is complex enough that a second set of eyes in real time, not after the fact, reduces the cost of errors. Especially effective for onboarding, for debugging complex production problems, and for tasks where the cost of a defect escaping to review is high. Also applicable as a training technique for autonomous agents that can take a turn as navigator — observing human reasoning in code and asking clarifying questions.

inputs.name: Two practitioners with a shared task
inputs.description: A driver and a navigator who share a machine, a keyboard, and a real task — not a practice problem — with enough complexity that both roles are genuinely engaged rather than one person watching the other work alone.

inputs.name: Role-switching discipline
inputs.description: A commitment to swap driver and navigator roles on a regular cadence — every 20–30 minutes at minimum — so that the learning benefit is not one-directional and the navigator does not mentally disengage.

outputs.name: More capable learner
outputs.description: A less experienced practitioner who has absorbed reasoning patterns — diagnostic habits, error-reading heuristics, design instincts — that they could not acquire from reading code or attending code review.

outputs.name: Shared codebase with lower defect rate
outputs.description: Working code produced by the pair — the masterpiece — which is demonstrably more robust than code produced by either practitioner alone, because the navigator catches errors in real time before they compound.

steps.name: Establish roles and task
steps.description: Agree which person drives first and which navigates. Define the task clearly enough that both roles know what done looks like — vague tasks produce aimless pairing where the navigator has nothing to navigate toward.

steps.name: Driver codes, navigator thinks ahead
steps.description: The driver implements, narrating their choices out loud when possible. The navigator watches not just the current line but the next problem — catching typos, noting edge cases, thinking about the next design decision — without interrupting the driver's flow for minor issues.

steps.name: Navigator intervenes at decision points
steps.description: The navigator speaks up when they see an error about to compound, a design choice about to go the wrong way, or a heuristic being applied in the wrong context. Intervention is not correction after the fact — it is redirection before the cost is paid.

steps.name: Switch roles on schedule
steps.description: At a fixed interval, swap keyboard. The switch is non-negotiable — if only the more experienced practitioner drives, the less experienced one never builds the production coding muscle the pattern is supposed to develop. Role-switching is what makes the learning bidirectional.

principles: Two minds on one task is not half the speed — the navigator's real-time error catching typically outweighs the coordination overhead, especially on complex or defect-sensitive work.
The navigator must be genuinely engaged, not watching — a disengaged navigator produces no learning benefit and no error catching. The role requires active anticipation, not passive observation.
patterns.threshold: After a pairing session, the less experienced practitioner can explain — not just recall — at least one reasoning pattern they observed the more experienced practitioner apply, and can identify where they would apply it themselves in a new context.
patterns.masterpiece: A working production artifact produced by the pair, accompanied by the less experienced practitioner's narrated account of what they learned from the experienced practitioner's reasoning during the session — demonstrating that the tacit knowledge transfer happened, not just the task completion.

known_uses.name: Extreme Programming (Kent Beck, 1999)
known_uses.org: First introduced in the C3 (Chrysler Comprehensive Compensation) project
known_uses.quote: Pair programming consists of two programmers sharing a single workstation (one screen, keyboard, and mouse among the pair).
known_uses.note: Pair programming is one of the twelve core practices of XP as defined by Kent Beck in Extreme Programming Explained (1999). The Agile Alliance traces the earliest written account to the article 'Chrysler goes to Extremes.'

known_failure_modes.note: The anti-pattern of the navigator mentally checking out — browsing, thinking about other tasks — while the driver codes. A passive navigator produces no error catching and no learning; it is one person working while another watches.

The anti-pattern of the more experienced practitioner monopolizing the driver role because it is faster, leaving the less experienced practitioner as a permanent observer. Without driving, the junior practitioner never builds the production coding muscle the pattern is supposed to develop.

The anti-pattern of applying pair programming to tasks simple enough for either practitioner to complete alone without any meaningful decision points. Pairing on trivial tasks produces overhead without the defect-catching or knowledge-transfer benefit.

sources.title: Pair Programming — Agile Alliance Glossary
sources.quote: Pair programming consists of two programmers sharing a single workstation (one screen, keyboard, and mouse among the pair). The programmer at the keyboard is usually called the 'driver', the other, also actively involved in the programming task but focusing more on overall direction is the 'navigator'.

sources.title: Extreme Programming Explained (Kent Beck, 1999)
sources.quote: All production code is written with two people looking at one machine, with one keyboard and one mouse.

diagram.mermaid:
flowchart TD
    A[Two practitioners
one task, one machine] --> B[Driver codes
narrates choices aloud]
    B --> C[Navigator thinks ahead
catches errors before they compound]
    C --> D{Decision point
or error spotted?}
    D -- Yes --> E[Navigator intervenes
redirects before cost is paid]
    D -- No --> F{Role switch
time?}
    F -- Yes --> G[Swap keyboard
roles reverse]
    G --> B
    F -- No --> B
    E --> B
    B --> H[Task complete
lower defect rate + tacit knowledge transferred]
