patterns.example_scenario: Finland holds the EU Council Presidency in the second half of 2019 and decides to make AI literacy its signature digital gift to Europe. The government partners with the University of Helsinki and MinnaLearn, who have already built a six-chapter online AI course in Finnish. The programme team adds English and Swedish translations, makes the course free to anyone with an internet connection, and announces a single public goal: train 1% of EU citizens. Finland's national broadcaster and its largest employers share the course link. Within six months, over 100,000 Finns complete it — about 2% of the population. European partners localise the course into 22 languages with funding from their own governments. By 2024, over 2 million people from 170 countries have enrolled. Finnish employers begin listing Elements of AI completion as a preferred qualification for office roles. The course earns a Webby Award. The team adds a more advanced 'Building AI' module for learners who want to go beyond literacy. The masterpiece is documented: over 2 million completions, employer recognition in multiple countries, and a national AI workforce that EU researchers cite as a benchmark for digital-era public education.

patterns.name: Responsible-Use Guardrail
patterns.summary: Every training track at every Step includes an explicit module on responsible use: what not to do, what to check, and what to report. This guardrail runs cross-cutting through the Craft Path so safety norms are instilled before bad habits form. The pattern is not a single course; it is a structural commitment to embed responsible-use content at the entry point of every step of training. Without this, responsible-use rules are taught once at foundation level and then forgotten as learners advance to more capable tools. The Responsible-Use Guardrail treats safety as a recurring cost of advancement, not a one-time checkbox.
patterns.intent: Make responsible AI use a non-skippable condition of advancing to each new level of AI capability, so safety norms grow with the learner's power.
patterns.when_to_apply: Apply this pattern whenever designing a multi-step or multi-module AI training programme. It is a structural requirement, not an optional add-on. The guardrail must appear at the start of every Step — Foundation, Operator, and any Craft or Builder track — not only at the beginning of the whole programme. Also apply it as a corrective when an existing programme has responsible-use content only at the start.

inputs.name: Organisation AI use policy
inputs.description: A written, published policy stating what employees may and may not do with AI tools, covering data privacy, IP and copyright, hallucination risk, misuse detection, and escalation paths. The policy must exist before the guardrail module is written — the module is how the policy is taught, not a substitute for the policy.

inputs.name: Real incident examples
inputs.description: Anonymised examples of responsible-use failures from inside the organisation or from comparable organisations — not invented scenarios. Real examples carry more weight than hypotheticals.

inputs.name: Step-specific risk context
inputs.description: For each training step, the specific risks that learners at that level face — a foundation learner's risks (data privacy, sharing outputs carelessly) differ from an operator-level learner's risks (automating decisions, using AI in client-facing work).

outputs.name: A more capable learner
outputs.description: A learner who, at each new level of AI capability, understands the specific responsible-use risks that level introduces and has acknowledged the rules that govern their expanded use.

outputs.name: Timestamped responsible-use acknowledgement per step
outputs.description: A documented, per-learner record that they have completed the responsible-use module at each step and acknowledged the policy — the Masterpiece of this cross-cutting pattern, because it creates a complete responsibility chain as capability grows.

outputs.name: Reduced incident rate
outputs.description: A measurable decrease in accidental data leaks, copyright breaches, and misuse reports after the guardrail programme is in place across all training steps.

steps.name: Define the policy before writing the module
steps.description: The responsible-use module is only as good as the policy it teaches. Before authoring any guardrail content, the organisation must have a written AI use policy that covers at minimum: what data cannot enter the AI, who is accountable for AI-assisted outputs, what constitutes misuse, and how to report a concern. If the policy does not exist, it must be written first.

steps.name: Cover the mandatory five areas at every step
steps.description: Each guardrail module, at every step, must cover five areas. The first two are hallucination risk (what the AI can get wrong and how to check) and data privacy (what cannot go in). The next two are IP and copyright (what cannot come out without attribution or review) and misuse detection (what misuse looks like at this capability level). The fifth is escalation (how to report a concern). The coverage deepens at each step to match the expanded capability.

steps.name: Use real incident examples, not hypotheticals
steps.description: Identify at least two real examples from inside the organisation or from published AI incident reports that illustrate responsible-use failures at the current level. Present them as case studies with outcomes. Anonymise internal examples. Hypotheticals are skipped; real cases are remembered.

steps.name: Require acknowledgement, not just completion
steps.description: At the end of each guardrail module, the learner must explicitly state that they have read and understood the policy and the step-specific risks — not just click through a completion screen. This acknowledgement is timestamped and stored. The combination of completion record and explicit acknowledgement is what produces defensible documentation.

principles: Responsible use is a recurring cost, not a one-time tax — it must appear at every step because power grows at every step.
Policy first, module second — no guardrail content before a written policy exists.
Acknowledgement is not completion — a learner who finishes the module but does not confirm understanding has not met the guardrail standard.
patterns.threshold: Every learner advancing to each new step has a timestamped acknowledgement of the step-specific responsible-use module on file, and the module covers all five mandatory areas for that step level.
patterns.masterpiece: A complete responsibility chain: for every learner in the organisation, a set of timestamped responsible-use acknowledgements — one per training step completed — that together demonstrate growing, documented AI safety awareness matching the learner's growing AI capability. Produceable as a single export for a regulatory or audit request.

known_uses.name: MasterCard responsible AI principles training
known_uses.quote: Eight hours of video training on responsible AI principles (fairness, transparency)
known_uses.note: Delivered as dedicated company-wide track, not appended to a tool tutorial.

known_uses.name: AWS Certified AI Practitioner — Domain 4: Guidelines for Responsible AI
known_uses.quote: Domain 4: Guidelines for Responsible AI (14% of scored content)
known_uses.note: Responsible use is a graded domain in the foundational AWS AI cert, not an elective.

known_uses.name: Google AI Essentials — Module 4: Use AI Responsibly
known_uses.quote: 5 modules: Introduction to AI, Maximize Productivity With AI Tools, Discover the Art of Prompting, Use AI Responsibly, Stay Ahead of the AI Curve
known_uses.note: Responsible use is the fourth of five modules — not an afterthought.

known_uses.name: EU AI Act Article 4 — role-specific literacy including risk
known_uses.quote: taking into account their technical knowledge, experience, education and training and the context the AI systems are to be used in, and considering the persons or groups of persons on whom the AI systems are to be used
known_uses.note: Article 4 requires risk-context awareness, making responsible-use a legally-grounded component of operator literacy.

known_failure_modes.note: The anti-pattern of teaching responsible use only at foundation level and treating it as done. A learner who completes a foundation-level responsible-use module and then advances to operator-level automation tools faces new risks that were never covered. The guardrail must advance with the learner.

Recording module completion without a separate explicit acknowledgement step. In audit or incident investigation, completion alone does not demonstrate that the learner understood and accepted the rules. Acknowledgement is the legally and organisationally meaningful act.

patterns.example_scenario: A global consulting firm rolls out AI tools in three waves: all staff get a chat AI in Q1, client-facing consultants get a document AI in Q2, and senior managers get an AI analytics tool in Q3. The L&D lead designs three guardrail modules — one per wave — each taking 30 minutes. The Q1 module covers the five base areas for a staff-level chat AI user. These are no client data in the chat window, no assuming the AI output is fact, no sharing outputs with clients without review, and how to report a concern. Every staff member completes it and acknowledges it on day one of chat AI access. The Q2 module covers the additional risks of the document AI: summarisation errors that could affect client deliverables, citation fabrication, and the need to attribute AI-assisted sections of reports. Every consultant completes it and acknowledges it before Q2 document AI access is granted. The Q3 module covers the analytics AI's specific risks: data bias in conclusions, presentation of AI-generated charts as primary analysis, and the regulatory considerations around AI-assisted advice in the firm's practice areas. Senior managers complete it before Q3 access. Six months after the programme is in place, the firm receives a client query about their AI use policies. The L&D lead exports the full responsibility chain — three acknowledgement records per person who holds all three access levels — in under 15 minutes. No incident during the six months required an escalation.

patterns.name: Build-Along
patterns.summary: A live or cohort-based course where non-engineers ship a small, real application using AI coding tools (Cursor, Lovable, Replit, Claude Code). The session moves at app-building speed, not lecture speed — every module ends with running code.
patterns.intent: Enable a non-engineer to ship and deploy a real web application by building it live alongside an instructor, one capability at a time, using AI-first coding tools.
patterns.when_to_apply: Use when a learner wants to build an actual application — not just automate a workflow — and is willing to commit to a multi-week live or cohort schedule. The build-along is the right choice when the learner's goal is a product (a web app, a personal tool, a founder's MVP) rather than a business process automation. It is distinct from a workflow track in that the output is a deployed application with a URL, not a triggered automation.

inputs.name: Learner with an application idea
inputs.description: A non-technical participant with a concrete idea for an application they want to ship — a personal tool, a simple SaaS, a portfolio piece — that is small enough to build progressively across the course weeks.

inputs.name: AI-first coding environment
inputs.description: Access to tools that let non-engineers direct code generation: Lovable, Replit, Cursor, Claude Code, v0, or equivalent. The learner's role is to describe, review, and test — not to write code from scratch.

inputs.name: Live instructor model
inputs.description: An instructor who builds a feature live in their own project while participants replicate it in theirs. The key is simultaneous building, not watching and then trying later.

inputs.name: Community channel
inputs.description: An async channel (Discord, Slack, or cohort platform) for getting unblocked between sessions, sharing progress, and peer reviewing each other's apps.

outputs.name: A more capable learner
outputs.description: A non-engineer who understands how to direct an AI coding tool to build, modify, and debug a real application — and who has the discernment to read and explain what the generated code does before shipping it.

outputs.name: Deployed web application (Masterpiece)
outputs.description: A publicly accessible, deployed web application the learner built across the course, with documentation, version history, and prompt notes — a portfolio artefact demonstrating maker-level capability.

steps.name: Week-by-week capability stacking
steps.description: Each week adds one new capability to the same running application: week 1 might be a static page; week 2 adds a form and database; week 3 adds authentication; week 4 adds an AI feature. Building on the same app each week rather than starting fresh each module maintains context and compounds complexity realistically.

steps.name: Prompting discipline alongside tool use
steps.description: Alongside tool mechanics, the course teaches how to describe what you want precisely enough for an AI tool to produce it, how to interpret and debug generated output, and what to do when the output is wrong. This is the craft dimension that separates a build-along from a demo walk-through.

steps.name: Discernment checkpoint
steps.description: Before any significant deployment step, the learner must read the generated code for the feature being shipped and explain what at least two key functions do. This checkpoint is a formal gate, not a suggestion — it exists specifically to counter the anti-pattern of shipping code the learner cannot read.

steps.name: Capstone demo
steps.description: The final session is a live demo of each participant's flagship app to the cohort. The app must be publicly accessible, deployed, and running on real user interactions — not a localhost demo or a prototype. Prompt notes and version history are submitted alongside the app as evidence of the build process.

principles: Every module ends with running code in the learner's own project — watching the instructor build without building yourself is not participation.
Prompting discipline is the primary skill; tool fluency is secondary — tools change, the ability to describe clearly does not.
The discernment checkpoint is non-negotiable: no learner ships a feature they cannot explain. Speed without comprehension is the build-along's primary failure mode.
The capstone is a deployed app, not a slide deck or a demo video of a localhost app.
patterns.threshold: The learner can add a new feature to their application using an AI coding tool, explain what the generated code does, and deploy the updated version without instructor help.
patterns.masterpiece: A publicly deployed web application built across the full course, with at least one AI-powered feature, prompt notes documenting key build decisions, and a version history showing week-by-week progression — demonstrated live to a cohort audience.

known_uses.name: VibeCoding 101 — Campus (8-week cohort)
known_uses.quote: Stop tinkering. Start shipping with AI.
known_uses.note: 8-week live cohort on Replit; 4 working apps + flagship milestone project with prompt notes and version history. $1,500; targets non-technical professionals.

known_uses.name: Vibe Coding Essentials — Scrimba / Coursera Specialization
known_uses.quote: 91% of learners achieved a positive career outcome
known_uses.note: 5-course specialization; no coding experience required; tools: Cursor, GitHub Copilot, Claude Code, MCP; builds portfolio website, games, Chrome extensions, mobile apps.

known_uses.name: Udemy — Vibe Coding: Build Apps with Claude Code, Cursor, and Codex
known_uses.quote: Agentic Engineering & Vibe Coding basics to develop apps in record time
known_uses.note: Async; covers Lovable, Replit, Claude Code, Cursor; ships first website in minutes.

known_failure_modes.note: The anti-pattern of shipping AI-generated code without reading or testing it. In a build-along the pace is fast enough that learners are routinely tempted to deploy immediately after generation. The discernment checkpoint exists as the direct counter-move.

The anti-pattern of completing the course with an app that only runs on the learner's machine. A non-deployed app has not crossed the maker threshold — real users cannot use it, and the learner has not faced the deployment challenges that are part of the skill.

sources.quote: built for non-technical professionals who want to use AI to build real solutions
no coding experience required

with prompt notes + version history]

patterns.example_scenario: A 34-year-old product manager at a logistics company has an idea for a small internal tool: a page where drivers can submit end-of-shift reports and see a summary of their last five submissions. She joins an 8-week Build-Along cohort. Week 1: she builds a static HTML page in Lovable following the instructor. Week 2: she adds a form that writes to a Supabase table. Week 3: authentication so only her team can log in. Week 4: a Claude-powered summary that reads the last five submissions and returns a two-sentence digest. Before deploying week 4, the instructor runs the discernment checkpoint — she reads the Claude API call the tool generated and explains what the system prompt does and why the temperature is set low. She can explain it. She deploys. By week 8, the app is live, used by three drivers, and demo'd to her cohort. Her prompt notes document every major build decision. Six months later a developer colleague reviews her code and offers to extend it — the readable prompt notes are what make that handoff possible.

patterns.name: Cohort Buildcamp
patterns.summary: A 6–12 week, cohort-based program for technical or near-technical learners who want to build production-grade AI applications. Live sessions, peer projects, a capstone build, and community are the primary learning containers.
patterns.intent: Take a technically-oriented learner from understanding AI primitives to shipping and evaluating a production-grade AI application through a structured cohort sequence with peer accountability and a graded capstone.
patterns.when_to_apply: Use when a learner already has basic programming ability and wants to build serious, production-grade AI applications — RAG systems, agents, evaluations — rather than automations. The cohort buildcamp covers the full stack from LLM primitives to deployment and monitoring, which distinguishes it from the Build-Along's focus on no-code-first app shipping. It is the right choice when the learner's goal is an engineering credential and portfolio rather than a single shipped tool.

inputs.name: Technically capable learner
inputs.description: A learner with basic Python or programming ability who wants to build production AI applications and is willing to commit 5–15 hours per week for 6–12 weeks.

inputs.name: Weekly live session and async project
inputs.description: A structured weekly rhythm: 1-hour live session introducing a concept or framework, followed by 3–10 hours of async project work building a real application component that uses it.

inputs.name: Capstone project specification
inputs.description: A full end-to-end AI application the learner builds across the final 2–3 weeks, with tests, evaluation pipeline, and documentation — graded by peers or instructors on defined criteria.

inputs.name: Cohort community
inputs.description: A Discord or Slack community of 20–200 co-enrolled learners providing async unblocking, peer review, and the social accountability that sustains multi-week commitment.

outputs.name: A more capable learner
outputs.description: A builder who understands the full AI application stack: prompt engineering, retrieval-augmented generation, agent design, evaluation, monitoring, and deployment — and who can discuss trade-offs between architecture choices.

outputs.name: Portfolio-ready capstone AI application (Masterpiece)
outputs.description: A publicly deployed, tested, documented end-to-end AI application with evaluation metrics, demonstrating production-level engineering judgment — the credential-grade artefact the learner can show to an employer or use in a product.

outputs.name: Completion certificate
outputs.description: A shareable certificate from the program provider, often requiring peer review as well as graded capstone completion, distinguishing it from self-paced courses that award completion without external validation.

steps.name: Foundational modules — LLM primitives
steps.description: Weeks 1–2 cover LLM fundamentals, prompt engineering, and a simple end-to-end application (FAQ assistant, search tool). The learner builds something working by the end of week 1 to establish that the program is practical, not theoretical.

steps.name: RAG and retrieval modules
steps.description: Weeks 3–5 build a retrieval-augmented generation system: chunking, embedding, vector search, and a document Q&A interface. Each component is built separately and then integrated, teaching architectural reasoning not just execution.

steps.name: Agents and orchestration modules
steps.description: Weeks 5–8 introduce agentic patterns: tool calling, multi-step reasoning, and a working agent (coding assistant, research agent, or equivalent). Frameworks like LangGraph, smolagents, or LlamaIndex are introduced in the context of a running build, not as abstract concepts.

steps.name: Evaluation and monitoring
steps.description: Weeks 8–9 cover evaluating LLM outputs with frameworks like RAGAS, adding observability to existing projects, and understanding where human-in-the-loop review is required. Learners add an evaluation pipeline to a project built in a prior week.

steps.name: Capstone build and peer review
steps.description: Weeks 10–12 are devoted to the capstone: a complete AI application the learner specifies, builds, tests, and deploys. Peer reviews (typically 2–3 required for certificate) ensure external validation of the work. A cohort hackathon or demo day is the final event.

principles: Every week produces a working project component, not just comprehension — if a learner can describe the concept but has not built with it, the module has not been completed.
Evaluation is not optional — an AI application without an evaluation pipeline is not production-grade; the course enforces this by including evaluation as a graded module, not an afterthought.
Peer review is the unit of trust — certificates that require only passing automated quizzes do not signal production capability; peer review gates are what make the cohort credential meaningful.
Architecture reasoning matters as much as tool fluency — the learner should be able to explain why they chose a vector database over keyword search, not just how to use it.
patterns.threshold: The learner can design and build a new RAG or agent application from scratch, select appropriate evaluation metrics, and explain the trade-offs in their architecture choices to a technical peer — without relying on course templates.
patterns.masterpiece: A publicly deployed, fully documented AI application with a working evaluation pipeline, peer-reviewed and certified by the program, demonstrating production engineering judgment across the full stack from prompt design to monitoring.

known_uses.name: Maven AI Engineering Buildcamp: From RAG to Agents (Alexey Grigorev)
known_uses.quote: your own end-to-end AI application from scratch
known_uses.note: 6-week cohort; 8+ projects; capstone + internal hackathon. Founded by creator of Zoomcamp series; 100,000+ alumni.

known_uses.name: The AI Engineering Bootcamp (Dr. Greg Loughnane & Chris Alexiuk) — Maven
known_uses.quote: In just 10 weeks of building, shipping, and sharing, you supercharge your abilities.
known_uses.note: 10-week cohort; twice-weekly live sessions; covers RAG, Agents, Agentic RAG, Deep Agents, Multi-Agent; evaluation via RAGAS.

known_uses.name: Hugging Face AI Agents Course (free, certified)
known_uses.quote: Earn a certificate of completion by completing assignments.
known_uses.note: Free; 4 units + 3 bonus; hands-on HF Spaces environments; frameworks: smolagents, LangGraph, LlamaIndex; final challenge on student leaderboard.

known_failure_modes.note: The anti-pattern of completing every module by following tutorial code without being able to build a novel application. Visible when the capstone is heavily derivative of a course example. Programs mitigate this by requiring the capstone to address a domain or problem not covered in any module.

The anti-pattern of building a full AI application and treating evaluation as an optional cosmetic step. Learners who skip evaluation cannot tell whether their application is working correctly in production; this is the primary cause of deployed AI systems that degrade silently.

The anti-pattern of enrolling in a cohort buildcamp and not completing the capstone, leaving with partial knowledge but no credential. The most common cause is underestimating the weekly time commitment. Mitigation: programs that require a public commitment and schedule a capstone kickoff call in week 1 have higher completion rates.

sources.quote: Ship a Capstone AI Project — Start to Finish
covers the similarities and differences between RAG, Agents, Agentic RAG, Deep Agents, and Multi-Agent Applications
The course is composed of: Foundational Units … Hands-on … Use case assignments … The Challenge
Zoomcamps offer a similar project-based, structured learning experience but are completely free and open source.

patterns.example_scenario: A data analyst at a healthcare company has basic Python skills and wants to build AI applications. He joins a 10-week AI Engineering Bootcamp cohort on Maven. Week 1 he ships a simple FAQ assistant using the Anthropic API. Weeks 3–5 he builds a RAG pipeline over a set of internal policy documents — chunking with a fixed overlap, embedding with a sentence transformer, storing in Chroma, and serving answers via a simple FastAPI endpoint. Week 6 introduces agents; he adds a tool call that can query a SQL database when the RAG pipeline cannot find the answer. Week 8 he adds a RAGAS evaluation pipeline that runs against a held-out test set, surfacing that his chunking strategy is losing context at section boundaries — he refactors the chunking and the faithfulness score improves from 0.61 to 0.84. His capstone is a clinical trial eligibility screening agent that integrates RAG, SQL lookup, and a document-generation tool, with full evaluation metrics and a deployment on Hugging Face Spaces. Two peer reviewers certify the capstone meets production standards. He earns his certificate and uses the capstone as the main portfolio piece in his job application for an AI engineering role.

patterns.name: Anti-pattern: Vibe-Ship Without Review
patterns.summary: Shipping AI-generated code without any human review or testing cycle. The speed of vibe-coding tools tempts makers to deploy code they cannot read or verify, creating security, correctness, and maintenance blind spots.
patterns.intent: Name and prevent the pattern of deploying AI-generated code without a comprehension gate, before it causes a security or correctness failure in a production application.
patterns.when_to_apply: Recognise and intervene when a learner or practitioner in any maker-step program is shipping multiple AI-generated features per session without reading the generated code, cannot explain what a deployed function does, or treats the AI tool's output as inherently correct. This anti-pattern is most dangerous at the maker step because the tooling is fast enough to ship real harm before the learner realises anything is wrong.

inputs.name: Generated code from an AI coding tool
inputs.description: Output from Lovable, Cursor, Claude Code, or equivalent — potentially containing security vulnerabilities, unchecked user inputs, hardcoded secrets, or logic errors that are invisible to someone who has not read the code.

inputs.name: Time pressure or speed incentive
inputs.description: A course structure, competitive context, or personal excitement that rewards shipping speed over comprehension — the environmental condition that makes this anti-pattern attractive.

outputs.name: Identified risk before deployment
outputs.description: A moment where the learner or a peer review stops a deployment that contains a reviewable problem — a leaked API key, an unsanitised input, or a function that does not do what the learner assumed.

outputs.name: Discernment habit
outputs.description: A learner who, after encountering or hearing about this anti-pattern, consistently reads generated code before deploying, can explain the key functions, and runs at least a smoke test before any public deployment.

steps.name: Recognise the symptom
steps.description: The anti-pattern presents in a few ways. The learner ships multiple features per session without opening the code view, or deploys to a public URL directly from an AI tool's 'one-click deploy' without reviewing the output. It can also show up as using test credentials in production, because the learner did not read the environment variable handling the tool generated.

steps.name: Apply the counter-move: discernment checkpoint
steps.description: Before any deployment, the learner reads the diff or generated code for the feature being shipped. They must be able to explain what at least two key functions do and what user input they handle. In a Build-Along track, this is a formal gate: the learner writes a one-sentence explanation of each key function in their prompt notes before the instructor approves their deploy step.

steps.name: Structural prevention: minimum viable review step
steps.description: Program designers add a mandatory review step to the build-along template: run the app locally first, read the generated code for the feature, check for hardcoded credentials, and confirm error states are handled. The step takes 5–10 minutes per feature and is non-optional. Skipping it triggers a conversation, not a penalty — the goal is habit formation.

principles: Speed without comprehension is not a skill; it is a liability that compounds with every unreviewed deployment.
The AI tool writes the code; the learner owns the deployed result — including its security surface and its behaviour under edge inputs.
A five-minute read before deploy prevents failure modes that take days to diagnose in production.
patterns.threshold: The learner consistently reads generated code before deploying, can explain what the key functions do, and can identify at least one category of risk (hardcoded secrets, unvalidated input, error handling) to check for in any generated feature.
patterns.masterpiece: A build-along or maker program that has formally incorporated the discernment checkpoint as a deploy gate, with observable evidence that learners read and annotate generated code before shipping — measured by the presence of prompt notes that describe generated functions, not just describe what was asked for.

known_failure_modes.note: The anti-pattern of adding a discernment step to a program's checklist without ensuring learners actually read the code. If the checkpoint is 'did you review the code? yes/no' with no verification, it becomes a rubber stamp that provides false security without changing behaviour.

sources.quote: vibe coding offers intoxicating generative speed at the cost of nearly all technical control

diagram.mermaid:
flowchart TD
    A[AI coding tool generates
a new feature] --> B{Learner reads
generated code?}
    B -- No: anti-pattern --> C[Deploy directly
to public URL]
    C --> D[Silent failure:
security hole, broken logic,
or leaked secret]
    B -- Yes: counter-move --> E[Learner explains
2 key functions]
    E --> F[Check for hardcoded
credentials + unvalidated input]
    F --> G[Smoke test locally]
    G --> H[Deploy with confidence
and annotated prompt notes]

patterns.example_scenario: A founder joins an 8-week Build-Along cohort with ambitions to ship fast. By week 3 she is deploying two to three features per session directly from Lovable's publish button without opening the code view. In week 4 she adds user authentication. The AI tool generates a Supabase auth integration — working, but with the service role key hardcoded in a client-side file. She ships it without reading the code. A cohort peer running a security check on their own app notices the pattern in a shared code snippet in the Discord channel and flags it. The instructor calls a ten-minute session halt: the learner reads the auth code for the first time, finds the hardcoded key, and rotates it immediately. The incident is used as a teaching moment for the full cohort. From that session forward, the program adds an explicit discernment checkpoint: before every deploy step, learners write one sentence in their prompt notes explaining what the auth or data-handling function does. Three weeks later the same learner catches an unvalidated user-input issue in her own generated code before deploying, entirely independently.

patterns.name: AI-as-Mentor
patterns.summary: The AI tool itself coaches the learner: it asks Socratic questions, gives context-sensitive feedback, and never just hands over the answer. Learning and doing happen in the same environment, removing the gap between training and work. This matters because most AI training creates a step between learning and use — the learner studies a course, then returns to their real task with only memory to guide them. The AI-as-Mentor move collapses that gap: the learner brings a real problem to the AI, and the AI teaches them how to work with it on that exact problem.
patterns.intent: Let the AI tool teach the learner how to use it, on the learner's own real problems, without switching to a separate training environment.
patterns.when_to_apply: Apply this move when a learner has a real work problem in front of them and needs both to solve it and to build lasting AI skills. It is most effective after basic acculturation has removed the initial fear barrier. Do not apply it as the first exposure to AI for an anxious learner — a small amount of structured introduction first produces better outcomes than a purely guided discovery start.

inputs.name: Learner with a real work problem
inputs.description: A person who has an actual task — a document to draft, a data set to summarise, a decision to frame — that they want to complete, not a synthetic exercise invented for training purposes.

inputs.name: AI tool configured to guide rather than solve
inputs.description: The same AI tool the learner will use in daily work, set up (via system prompt, course scaffolding, or mode selection) to ask questions and offer hints rather than produce complete answers immediately.

outputs.name: A more capable learner
outputs.description: A person who has solved a real task using the AI and who can articulate which prompting choices they made and why — able to replicate and adapt the approach on the next task without human instruction.

outputs.name: Solved real task
outputs.description: The completed work item the learner brought to the session — the Masterpiece — produced through their own guided iteration rather than AI-authored in one step.

steps.name: Bring a real problem
steps.description: The learner identifies a task from their actual work — not a training exercise — and opens the AI tool. They describe the task in their own words to the AI.

steps.name: Let the AI ask first
steps.description: The AI (configured for Socratic guidance) responds with a clarifying question or a prompt scaffold rather than a complete answer. The learner responds to the question and sees how the AI's output improves with more precise input.

steps.name: Iterate and note what changed
steps.description: The learner refines their request based on the AI's output. After two or three rounds, they pause and write one sentence about what they did that produced a better result. This reflection is the core learning act.

steps.name: Complete the task and review
steps.description: The learner produces the final output using the AI's help, then reviews it critically: is it accurate, is it usable, what would they need to check? The Discernment step is always explicit, never assumed.

principles: The tool teaches; the human learns by doing, not watching — no passive lesson mode.
Real problems transfer; synthetic exercises do not — the learner's own work context is the curriculum.
Reflection is not optional — the one sentence written after each session is what turns task completion into skill.
patterns.threshold: The learner completes a full real task end-to-end using only AI assistance (no human tutor), and can explain in one paragraph which prompting choices led to the best output and what they would check before using the result at work.
patterns.masterpiece: A finished, real work output — a document, an analysis, a plan — produced by the learner through guided AI iteration, accompanied by a written reflection of at least three prompting lessons learned during the session.

known_uses.name: OpenAI AI Foundations — ChatGPT as tutor and practice space
known_uses.quote: A full learning experience is available directly inside ChatGPT, where learners can practice real tasks, receive feedback in context, and reflect on their work in a single environment
known_uses.note: AI Foundations course launched 2025/2026; ChatGPT acts as tutor, practice space, and feedback loop simultaneously.

known_uses.name: Khanmigo (Khan Academy AI tutor)
known_uses.quote: Unlike other AI tools such as ChatGPT, Khanmigo doesn't just give answers. Instead, with limitless patience, it guides learners to find the answer themselves.
known_uses.note: Piloted in 260+ US school districts; available free to teachers in 40+ countries via Microsoft partnership.

known_uses.name: Anthropic AI Fluency course — Claude as learning partner
known_uses.quote: This course has something for everyone, whether you're new to Claude or a seasoned AI practitioner.
known_uses.note: Free structured course; learners use Claude while taking the course about Claude — AI-as-mentor pattern embedded by design.

known_failure_modes.note: The anti-pattern of the learner rephrasing their question until the AI gives a complete answer, then copying it without understanding. The session produces an output but no skill. The reflection step is the only reliable counter.
Configuring the AI to withhold answers so aggressively that the learner becomes frustrated and gives up before the task is complete. The Socratic mode must be calibrated — hints, not silence.

patterns.example_scenario: A marketing coordinator at a retail company has to write a monthly competitive summary report. She has heard of AI but has never used it for real work. Her manager suggests she try using the company's licensed AI assistant for this month's report instead of searching and writing manually. She opens the tool and types: 'Write me a competitive summary for our Q2 report.' The AI (configured with a Socratic system prompt) responds: 'That sounds useful. Which competitors do you want me to cover, and what does your audience care most about — pricing, product features, or market positioning?' She realises she had not thought about that. She types: 'Three competitors. The audience is our sales team, who care about price and product gaps.' The AI returns a structured outline with placeholders for the three competitors. It adds a prompt asking whether she can give the price points for each competitor, or whether it should note where she would need to fill those in. She fills in what she knows and the AI produces a first draft. She reads it critically, spots one claim that does not match what she knows from a recent trade show, and asks the AI to revise that section. The final report takes 40 minutes instead of her usual three hours. She writes in her reflection: 'I learned that naming my audience changes the whole structure. I should always tell the AI who will read the output before I start.'

patterns.name: Home-Forged Training
patterns.summary: Training is built by your own subject-matter experts, not bought off the shelf. Because the examples, language, and tools match the learner's actual job, the content earns more trust and sees higher completion and retention. Off-the-shelf AI courses from vendors are generalist by necessity. They cannot reference your internal systems, your real data shapes, your tool configuration, or the actual mistakes your staff make. Home-forged courses do all of these things, and that specificity is what converts training attendance into changed work behaviour.
patterns.intent: Produce AI training that learners trust because it uses their own tools, their own examples, and their colleagues as authors.
patterns.when_to_apply: Apply this move when an organisation has identified internal staff who already use AI well and wants to scale those capabilities to colleagues. It is the right choice when generic vendor content has failed to produce behaviour change, when the organisation has unusual tools or workflows, or when the responsible-use rules are company-specific and cannot be delegated to an external provider to explain.

inputs.name: Internal AI champions
inputs.description: Employees who already use AI tools effectively in their own roles and are willing to author or co-author course material. These are the credibility anchor of home-forged content.

inputs.name: Real company workflows and data shapes
inputs.description: The actual tasks, documents, systems, and data formats that employees encounter — used as the worked examples in the course instead of generic or simulated scenarios.

inputs.name: Internal tool configuration
inputs.description: The specific AI tools the organisation has licensed, including any custom settings, approved use cases, and access restrictions — content must match what employees can actually do on their machines.

outputs.name: A more capable learner
outputs.description: An employee who has completed training built from their own work context and who can apply AI to their real tasks immediately, with no translation layer between training and job.

outputs.name: High-completion internal course
outputs.description: A course with verifiable high completion — 90% or more of enrolled staff — because the content is seen as directly relevant rather than generic. This is the Masterpiece of the Home-Forged move.

outputs.name: Internal AI qualification
outputs.description: A company-specific credential, tied to passing the course, that signals AI readiness within the organisation for role progression or tool access.

steps.name: Identify and recruit internal champions
steps.description: Find the colleagues who already use AI well — they are usually visible through their output quality or through word of mouth. Invite them to author worked examples for their own role cluster. The authoring process itself deepens their mastery and gives them social credibility within the organisation.

steps.name: Build from real workflows
steps.description: Each champion drafts two or three worked examples using actual tasks from their week — not invented scenarios. Examples show a before state (the task done the old way) and an after state (the same task with AI), including the prompt they used and what they checked before using the output.

steps.name: Gate with an internal qualification
steps.description: Set a completion gate: passing the course earns a named internal credential (e.g. 'AI Operator — Level 1'). Tie the credential to a concrete benefit: access to the organisation's advanced AI tools, eligibility for the next training track, or inclusion in the AI Champions network. The credential makes completion desirable rather than merely required.

steps.name: Iterate quarterly
steps.description: AI tools and policies change fast. Schedule a quarterly review where the original champions update worked examples to reflect current tool versions and add examples from new use cases that have emerged. Internal authors can respond to tool updates within days; a vendor course cannot.

principles: Trust comes from recognition — learners complete training they can see themselves in; generic examples are skipped.
Champions are authors, not endorsers — their hands-on involvement in writing the course is what makes it credible, not their name on a banner.
Internal qualification changes the incentive from compliance to aspiration.
patterns.threshold: 90% or more of enrolled employees complete the course, and graduates can demonstrate the target AI skill on an actual internal task — not on the training exercise — within two weeks of completion.
patterns.masterpiece: A live internal AI course, authored by employees, with 90%+ completion, a named internal qualification, and documented quarterly update cadence — showing that AI capability is spreading peer-to-peer, not only top-down.

known_uses.name: CyberAgent ChatGPT Enterprise 101 and custom internal workshop series
known_uses.quote: more than 100 employees participating in each of over ten sessions that ranged from beginner-friendly introductions such as 'ChatGPT Enterprise 101' to workshops on custom GPTs, hands-on Codex sessions, and internal hackathons
known_uses.note: ChatGPT Enterprise reached 93% monthly active user rate. Sessions were hosted by OpenAI but delivered as internal programming; the home-forged pattern is visible in the internally-customised content tracks.

known_uses.name: Anthropic AI Fluency CC-licensed framework for internal adaptation
known_uses.quote: HR and L&D leaders are implementing this framework by… adapting the Creative Commons–licensed content for internal programs
known_uses.note: Anthropic explicitly licenses the 4D framework for organisations to build their own home-forged courses on top of it.

known_failure_modes.note: The anti-pattern of relying on one or two internal champions to author all content. When those people change roles or leave, the course stops being updated and quickly becomes stale — worse than a vendor course because at least vendor courses are maintained.

Creating an internal AI credential that has no concrete benefit attached to it. If passing the course changes nothing about what the employee can access or do, completion rates drop toward the level of compliance training.

patterns.example_scenario: A 600-person insurance broker has licensed an AI assistant for all staff but usage after two months is under 20%. The HR director asks five well-known AI enthusiasts across the firm to each write two worked examples from their last month of real work. The five are a claims handler, a broker, a finance analyst, a compliance officer, and an operations coordinator. The claims handler writes one example showing how she uses the AI to draft a first response to a complex claim, including her exact prompt and the one thing she always edits before sending. The compliance officer writes an example of using the AI to flag whether a proposed policy wording conflicts with a known regulatory rule, and explains the check she runs to verify the AI's reading. All five examples are assembled into a four-hour course alongside the firm's acceptable-use policy. The course earns the internal credential 'AI Operator — Claims / Broker / Finance / Compliance / Operations' depending on which track is completed. Completion of the credential gates access to the firm's advanced AI modules launching in Q3. Within six weeks, completion is at 88%. A quarterly review three months later: the claims handler updates her example because the AI assistant has a new document upload feature that changes her workflow. The update takes her 90 minutes. A vendor-maintained course would have taken three months to reflect the same change.

patterns.name: Vendor Cert Ladder
patterns.summary: A learner follows a structured, vendor-provided certification ladder from zero AI knowledge to a credentialled, verifiable proof of literacy. The external credential signals readiness to employers and creates a shared vocabulary within teams. The six major AI platforms — Google, Microsoft, AWS, IBM, Anthropic, OpenAI — each offer a foundational certificate requiring no technical prerequisites. These certs range from free (IBM, Anthropic) to under $150 (AWS, Microsoft), take two to eight hours of self-paced work, and produce a verifiable badge. The Vendor Cert Ladder move treats these as stackable: the learner starts with one, earns the badge, and can advance to deeper tracks on the same platform or cross to another platform's offering.
patterns.intent: Give an individual learner a structured, externally credentialled path from zero AI knowledge to a verifiable proof of operator-level literacy.
patterns.when_to_apply: Apply this move when an individual learner wants structured guidance rather than self-directed exploration, or needs an externally verifiable credential for employment or progression. Also apply it when an organisation wants a vendor-neutral way to set a literacy bar without building their own course. Also apply when EU AI Act Article 4 documentation is required and an organisation chooses recognised vendor certs as the evidence base.

inputs.name: Learner at zero or low AI literacy
inputs.description: An individual who has little or no prior AI experience and wants structured learning toward a credential — no technical background required for foundational tier certs.

inputs.name: Selected foundational cert
inputs.description: One cert from the ecosystem the learner works in or aspires to: Google AI Essentials, Microsoft AI-901, AWS AIF-C01, IBM AI Literacy, Anthropic AI Fluency, or OpenAI AI Foundations — each appropriate for non-technical learners.

outputs.name: A more capable learner
outputs.description: A person who has completed structured AI literacy training and can use the certified platform's tools on real tasks, evaluate AI outputs critically, and apply responsible-use principles in their work.

outputs.name: Verifiable digital badge or certificate
outputs.description: An externally issued, publicly linkable credential tied to the learner's identity — the Masterpiece — demonstrating that a defined literacy standard has been met and providing evidence for employers or regulatory compliance.

steps.name: Select the right cert for the ecosystem
steps.description: Match the cert to where the learner will use AI. Pick Google AI Essentials for Workspace users, Microsoft AI-901 for M365 or Azure environments, and AWS AIF-C01 for cloud-first businesses. Pick IBM SkillsBuild for platform-neutral literacy, Anthropic AI Fluency for Claude users, and OpenAI AI Foundations for ChatGPT users. The worked examples in each cert are platform-specific; the right choice produces higher relevance and lower study time.

steps.name: Complete self-paced modules
steps.description: Work through each module in the selected cert. All foundational certs cover: what AI and generative AI are, how to prompt effectively, how to evaluate output quality, and how to use AI responsibly. Target two to eight hours depending on the cert chosen.

steps.name: Pass the assessment and earn the badge
steps.description: Complete the proctored or auto-graded assessment. For AWS and Microsoft, this is a separate exam booking. For Google, IBM, Anthropic, and OpenAI, assessment is embedded in the course. Pass and claim the verifiable digital badge via Credly, Google Certificates, or equivalent.

steps.name: Apply the certified skills on a real task
steps.description: Within a week of earning the badge, complete one real work task using the AI tools covered in the cert. The badge is evidence of literacy; the real task is the proof of transfer. If no real task emerges, the cert was the wrong one for the learner's job context.

principles: Match cert to ecosystem — a well-chosen cert takes half the study time of a mis-matched one and transfers twice as well.
The badge is evidence of literacy, not proof of skill — the real task after the cert is what closes the loop.
Stack from foundational to specialised — the Vendor Cert Ladder is a path, not a single step.
patterns.threshold: The learner holds a verifiable badge from a recognised AI foundational certification and can apply at least one technique from the cert to a real task in their current role within one week of completing the assessment.
patterns.masterpiece: A verifiable, publicly-linkable digital badge or certificate from a recognised AI provider, earned through assessment, tied to the learner's professional identity — demonstrating to employers, clients, or regulators that a defined AI literacy standard has been met.

known_failure_modes.note: The anti-pattern of earning a cert and never applying the skills to a real task. The badge is not the goal; changed work behaviour is. A learner who completes a cert and returns to working exactly as before has not advanced on the Craft Path.

Choosing a cert for the wrong ecosystem — for example, completing the AWS AI Practitioner when the organisation uses Google Workspace. The worked examples do not transfer and the learner finishes knowing a platform they never use.

patterns.example_scenario: A project manager at a mid-sized construction firm has been told by her director that the company will start using AI tools for site report summarisation and tender drafting within the next quarter. She has no prior AI experience and feels behind. She looks at the cert options and picks Google AI Essentials because the firm already uses Google Workspace. She completes the five modules over four evenings — about four and a half hours total. The third module on prompting is the most useful; she tries the techniques directly in her Workspace while working through it. She passes the final assessment on the first attempt and her Coursera certificate appears the same day. The following Monday she drafts the summary section of a site inspection report using Google's AI tool with a prompt she adapted from the module's worked example. It takes twelve minutes instead of her usual 45. She clips the prompt and the output and sends them to her director as evidence that the cert was worth the time. Her director asks her to run a lunch session for the other three project managers. She adds the Google Certificate to her LinkedIn profile that evening. The badge is public and verifiable. Two weeks later she receives a recruiter message referencing it.

patterns.name: Whole-Crew Baseline
patterns.summary: Roll out a common AI literacy baseline to every person in an organisation — not just technical staff. Everyone learns the same foundational vocabulary, responsible-use rules, and at least one AI tool, so the whole crew can participate in AI change. Without a shared baseline, AI adoption splits organisations into a capable minority and an excluded majority. The baseline closes that gap by treating AI literacy as a universal operating requirement, the same way reading and email literacy became universal in prior decades.
patterns.intent: Give every person in the organisation the same minimum AI vocabulary, responsible-use awareness, and at least one proven hands-on skill.
patterns.when_to_apply: Apply this move when an organisation is beginning a meaningful AI adoption programme and needs every employee, not just early adopters, to reach a common starting point. Also apply when a new regulatory obligation (such as EU AI Act Article 4) requires documented literacy for all staff who use AI in their work.

inputs.name: Defined literacy bar
inputs.description: A written statement of exactly what every employee must be able to do and know: use a named AI tool, spot a hallucination, state the responsible-use policy, and report a misuse. This bar must be set before the module is built.

inputs.name: Self-paced module with role-specific examples
inputs.description: A short course — typically three to eight hours — containing worked examples drawn from real roles in the organisation, not generic AI demonstrations.

inputs.name: Responsible-use policy
inputs.description: A published document stating what employees may and may not do with AI tools at work. The baseline module and the policy must land together; policy without training or training without policy both fail.

outputs.name: A more capable learner
outputs.description: An employee who can use at least one AI tool on a real work task, articulate the limits of AI output, and name the responsible-use rules that apply to their role.

outputs.name: Organisation-wide completion record
outputs.description: A documented completion rate across all departments — the Masterpiece — that demonstrates the baseline has actually landed, not merely been made available.

outputs.name: Responsible-use acknowledgement
outputs.description: A timestamped, per-employee record confirming they have read and understood the responsible-use policy alongside the baseline module.

steps.name: Set the minimum literacy bar
steps.description: Define, in writing, what every employee must be able to do after the baseline: name and use one AI tool, detect a hallucination, state the data-privacy rule, and know how to report a concern. This bar drives all content decisions and is the test for completion.

steps.name: Build role-specific worked examples
steps.description: For each major role cluster in the organisation, create at least one worked example that shows the AI tool solving a task the employee actually faces — not a generic demonstration. Examples are the primary reason baseline modules succeed or fail.

steps.name: Pair with responsible-use policy and gate access
steps.description: Require completion of the module and acknowledgement of the policy before granting access to the organisation's AI tools. This pairing ensures the rules and the skills land at the same moment.

steps.name: Track completion, not scores
steps.description: Measure the organisation's health by completion rate across all departments — the goal is 80% or more within six months. Use department completion rates, not individual scores, as the management metric and the evidence for regulatory compliance.

principles: Universal means everyone, not just the willing — completion is the metric, not enrolment.
Role-specific examples outperform generic demos; if the example does not look like the learner's job, the learning does not transfer.
Policy and training are one delivery, not two — they must arrive together.
patterns.threshold: At least 80% of all employees in scope have completed the module, acknowledged the responsible-use policy, and can demonstrate at least one AI-assisted work task from their own role.
patterns.masterpiece: A documented organisation-wide completion record showing the baseline literacy bar reached across all departments, with a responsible-use acknowledgement on file for every completing employee — produceable as evidence for a regulatory audit.

known_uses.note: CEO mandate: all incoming staff receive prompt engineering as a baseline literacy move.

known_uses.name: MasterCard responsible-AI hub

known_uses.note: Delivered via a new company intranet hub, launched August 2024.

known_uses.name: Elements of AI — EU 1% citizen goal
known_uses.quote: train one per cent of EU citizens in the basics of artificial intelligence
known_uses.note: Over 2 million enrolled across 170+ countries in 40+ languages; the canonical national-scale version of this move.

known_failure_modes.note: The anti-pattern of reporting enrolment numbers as if they were completion numbers. A baseline that 90% of staff are enrolled in but only 40% finish has not landed; it has been opened.
The anti-pattern of using an off-the-shelf AI literacy course whose examples do not match the organisation's tools or roles. Completion rates stay low and transfer to real work stays near zero.

patterns.example_scenario: A European financial services firm with 3,200 employees must comply with EU AI Act Article 4 by mid-2025. The L&D director commissions an internal baseline module. She starts by setting the bar in writing: every employee must be able to open the company's licensed AI assistant, complete a task from a provided list, and sign the acceptable-use policy. The content team builds five role clusters — client-facing staff, operations, finance, IT, and management — and writes one worked example per cluster using tasks from the firm's own workflows. The module takes four hours per cluster variant. All five variants are published simultaneously with the acceptable-use policy, and tool access is gated behind completion. A dashboard shows per-department completion rates updated daily. At week four, the back-office operations cluster is at 62%; the department head sends a team message asking all outstanding staff to complete by Friday. By week twelve, overall completion is 84%. The firm's compliance team exports the completion records and policy acknowledgements as the evidence package for its Article 4 documentation. Six months later a random sample of twenty completers is tested on the literacy bar items — seventeen pass without re-training.

patterns.name: All-Hands Reskilling

guardrail: do not stop at course completion — measure actual use
segment curriculum by role to avoid one-size-fits-all failure

patterns.summary: Launch a mandatory, tiered AI learning program for the entire company — not just willing early adopters — using role-segmented curricula and a pass/fail assessment gate to ensure genuine comprehension before moving people to the next tier.
patterns.intent: Reach every employee with AI capability — including the unwilling and the sceptical — by making AI learning mandatory, tiered, and gated, so no function is left behind by voluntary opt-in programmes.
patterns.when_to_apply: Apply when the scale of the org makes voluntary or cohort-based programmes insufficient, when competitive pressure demands a short timeline to broad AI literacy, or when a significant minority of employees would not self-enrol in optional training. Works best when paired with visible leadership modeling so that mandatory does not read as punitive.

inputs.name: Role-segmented curriculum design
inputs.description: Curricula structured into at minimum three role tiers — all-staff foundational, functional practitioners, and technical experts — with different content, different lengths, and different assessments per tier. A single curriculum for all roles either bores experts or overwhelms non-technical staff.

inputs.name: Assessment gate per tier
inputs.description: A pass/fail assessment at each tier that must be completed before the employee is recorded as having cleared that level. Without a gate, completion becomes attendance, and attendance does not predict use.

outputs.name: More capable org
outputs.description: An organisation where AI literacy has reached every role and function, including the sceptical majority who would not have self-enrolled, creating a shared baseline from which adoption programmes can build.

outputs.name: Tiered completion and assessment record
outputs.description: The masterpiece: a verified record of every employee's tier, completion status, and assessment result — used to direct follow-on adoption support to those who cleared literacy but are not yet using AI, and to certify org-wide readiness to external stakeholders.

steps.name: Design the tier structure and curricula
steps.description: Define three or four tiers by role type — typically all-staff (foundational, 4–8 hours), functional users (practitioner, 8–16 hours), practitioners or power users (advanced, 16–40 hours), and technical contributors (expert, beyond). Each tier has content, exercises, and an assessment specific to its audience. Do not build one tier and call it universal.

steps.name: Make the foundational tier mandatory and time-boxed
steps.description: Set a hard deadline for foundational tier completion — typically 60–90 days from launch — and communicate it as a business requirement, not a training offer. Include a pass/fail assessment. Track completion publicly by department or business unit so teams feel social accountability pressure alongside individual obligation.

steps.name: Add live cohort sessions for Q&A
steps.description: Even at scale, include live sessions — department-level workshops or live Q&A webinars — where employees can ask questions that e-learning cannot answer. BCG data shows that access to in-person training and coaching significantly boosts adoption over self-paced e-learning alone.

steps.name: Publish completion rates and use rates
steps.description: Make completion rates visible to business unit leaders on a live dashboard. Social pressure from below-average completion is a powerful driver — no business unit head wants to be the visible laggard. Add use-rate data alongside completion so the dashboard distinguishes between 'completed training' and 'is using AI.'

steps.name: Run in waves to generate social proof
steps.description: Do not launch to all staff simultaneously. Sequence waves — typically the most AI-ready function goes first — so that early-wave success stories exist by the time later, more sceptical waves launch. The sceptic is more convinced by a respected peer saying 'this worked for me' than by any corporate communication.

principles: Mandatory with an assessment gate is qualitatively different from mandatory without one — the gate creates the condition for genuine comprehension rather than attendance compliance.
Role segmentation is not optional at scale — a single curriculum fails by trying to be relevant to everyone and ending up relevant to no one.
Completion rates and use rates are different metrics — a programme that tracks only completion may be measuring nothing more than time spent watching videos.
patterns.threshold: Greater than 90% of all employees have cleared the foundational tier assessment, and a use-rate dashboard is live showing the transition from trained to actively using.
patterns.masterpiece: A verified, role-tiered completion and assessment record showing every employee's tier status, assessment outcome, and use rate — combined with a wave-by-wave retrospective that documents what drove completion and use in each wave and informs the next phase of the programme.

known_uses.name: 生成AI徹底理解リスキリング (Generative AI Comprehensive Reskilling)
known_uses.quote: サイバーエージェントの99.6%にあたる社員・全役員が受講した
known_uses.note: 99.6% pass rate among all employees and board; three-tier: Everyone, Developers, ML Engineers; started Nov 2023; lang: ja

known_uses.name: GenAI Talent Development Framework (belt system)
known_uses.quote: NTT DATA's award-winning training initiative is driving AI literacy and skills development for all employees worldwide
known_uses.note: Brandon Hall Group Gold Award Aug 2025; 200,000 employees globally; 70,000 Yellowbelt+ by Oct 2025; belt names: Whitebelt/Yellowbelt/Greenbelt/Blackbelt

known_uses.name: 1.6M Associate AI Training (Google certification)
known_uses.quote: it's offering training to 1.6 million workers instead
known_uses.note: Google AI Professional Certification; 8-hour foundational course; $1B committed to skills training by 2026

known_failure_modes.note: Anti-pattern: a single curriculum is delivered to all roles — software engineers and receptionists in the same 8-hour module. Engineers disengage because the content is too basic; non-technical staff disengage because the content is too abstract. Both groups complete the course and use AI at the same low rate they would have without it.

Anti-pattern: the programme tracks only course completion, declares success at 90% completion, and does not measure whether AI use changes. Six months later, adoption is still low and there is no data to diagnose why. Completion measures attendance; use measures impact.

patterns.example_scenario: A Japanese internet company with 6,000 employees decides that 50% of engineers must be genuinely AI-capable within 18 months. The L&D team designs a three-tier programme: Everyone (all 6,000 employees, 6-hour e-learning + original assessment), Developers (600 engineers, deeper curriculum on AI-assisted coding and prompt engineering), and ML Engineers (100 specialists, advanced track on model integration and fine-tuning). The Everyone tier launches in November with a 60-day mandatory deadline. The L&D team runs weekly department-level Q&A webinars where managers can bring real questions. Completion is displayed on an internal dashboard by team. By week eight, one business unit — advertising operations — is at 43% completion versus the company average of 71%. The advertising operations head is briefed privately; she runs two additional team sessions and completion reaches 79% before the deadline. Final completion across all employees and board members: 99.6%, all with assessment passes. The Developer tier launches next, enrolling 152 engineers who complete a more intensive curriculum and are subsequently treated as the verified cohort capable of serving as veterans in team embeddings. The masterpiece is the verified tiered record: 5,976 foundational passes, 152 Developer-tier completions, and a use-rate dashboard showing weekly AI activity rising from 8% before the programme to 61% six months after the Everyone tier closed.

patterns.name: Center of Excellence Activation

guardrail: CoE must show value to business units or become isolated bureaucracy
keep team small and focused — avoid CoE becoming a bottleneck

patterns.summary: Stand up a dedicated Center of Excellence that sets standards, accelerates scaled rollouts, and owns governance — not to do all the AI work itself but to make every business unit better at doing theirs.
patterns.intent: Create a small, authoritative central body that multiplies AI capability across business units by setting the standards they need, providing the expertise they lack, and removing the governance uncertainty that slows them down.
patterns.when_to_apply: Apply once multiple business units are running independent AI experiments and the absence of shared standards is producing inconsistent safety decisions, duplicated tooling spend, or conflicting rollout approaches. Also apply as the Acceleration-phase structural move in any three-phase AI adoption programme — the CoE is what makes phase two systematic rather than opportunistic.

inputs.name: Clear mandate separating CoE authority from business unit authority
inputs.description: An explicit, written statement of what the CoE owns — tool selection, responsible-AI review, training standards — and what it does not own — use-case prioritisation, delivery of individual solutions. Without this boundary, the CoE either becomes a bottleneck by touching everything or becomes irrelevant by having no real authority.

inputs.name: Small permanent core plus rotating fellows
inputs.description: A permanent staff of 5–15 subject-matter experts, plus a rotating cohort of fellows seconded from business units for 6–12 months. The fellows bring real-world context; the permanent core holds continuity. This structure prevents the CoE from drifting into an ivory tower.

outputs.name: More capable org
outputs.description: An organisation where business units can build and deploy AI confidently because the CoE has resolved the standards, governance, and tooling questions they would otherwise have to solve independently — at lower quality and higher cost.

outputs.name: State-of-AI dashboard and standards library
outputs.description: The masterpiece: a monthly state-of-AI dashboard showing adoption, use cases launched, and lessons learned across the org, combined with a standards library (tooling decisions, responsible-AI checklist, training standards) that every business unit can use and contribute to.

steps.name: Define the CoE mandate explicitly
steps.description: Write a one-page mandate statement covering three things: what the CoE sets (standards, responsible-AI criteria, training requirements), what it accelerates (scaled rollouts, cross-unit reuse of proven patterns), and what it governs (tool selection authority, responsible-AI review, training certification). Publish it to all business unit heads before the CoE opens its doors.

steps.name: Staff the permanent core and recruit the first fellows cohort
steps.description: Hire or internally assign 5–15 people to the permanent core — a mix of AI/ML technical depth, change management skill, and domain knowledge. Open applications for the first fellows cohort from business units; select for people who will take hard-won CoE knowledge back to their function when they rotate out.

steps.name: Build the structured intake process
steps.description: Create a lightweight but real intake process: business units submit AI initiative proposals; the CoE provides a feasibility review, a tooling recommendation, and a responsible-AI risk rating within a defined SLA (e.g., 10 business days). This makes the CoE useful rather than bureaucratic — it helps business units move faster, not slower.

steps.name: Publish the state-of-AI dashboard monthly
steps.description: Every month, publish a brief internal report: how many AI use cases are active, which reached production, what the adoption rates are, what the top lessons from the past month are. The dashboard makes the CoE's work visible, gives leadership a real pulse on transformation progress, and creates light accountability for business units.

steps.name: Plan for CoE dissolution or integration
steps.description: From day one, set a success condition that makes the CoE unnecessary. State it plainly: when every business unit has internally certified AI practitioners and AI is embedded in their standard processes, the CoE transitions from central authority to internal consultancy. Planning for dissolution prevents the CoE from becoming a permanent bureaucracy that outlives its value.

principles: The CoE exists to make business units capable, not to do the work for them — any initiative the CoE builds itself is a failure mode, not a deliverable.
Authority and service must coexist — the CoE must have real authority over standards and governance, but must exercise it in ways that help rather than slow business units, or they route around it.
Plan for dissolution from the start — a CoE that has no exit condition will fill available scope indefinitely.
patterns.threshold: Every business unit has submitted at least one initiative proposal through the CoE intake process and received a feasibility and governance response within the published SLA, and the state-of-AI dashboard is live and updated monthly.
patterns.masterpiece: A state-of-AI dashboard and standards library — updated monthly for at least two consecutive quarters — showing active use cases, adoption metrics, lessons learned, and a complete set of tooling, training, and responsible-AI standards that business units demonstrably use when launching AI initiatives.

known_uses.name: Center of Excellence — Acceleration Phase
known_uses.quote: leader-led articulation of adoption rationale, fostering organizational growth mindset, and systematic sharing of successes
known_uses.note: CoE establishment is the Acceleration phase deliverable in Anthropic's three-phase enterprise adoption framework

known_uses.name: AI Center of Excellence (tripled)
known_uses.quote: Crowe doubles size of dedicated AI team via AI Center of Excellence
known_uses.note: Three sub-teams: AI lab (exploration), AI engineering (build), AI enablement (adoption); Oct 2024; accounting firm

known_failure_modes.note: Anti-pattern: the CoE's intake and review process is slow and opaque, so business units route around it — building and deploying AI without CoE review to avoid delays. The CoE has authority in name but not in practice, and governance gaps it was created to close remain open.

Anti-pattern: the CoE becomes the default AI development team for the whole organisation — business units bring problems and the CoE builds solutions. This scales the CoE's workload linearly with the org's AI ambition and produces no distributed capability. When the CoE is overloaded it becomes the bottleneck it was meant to prevent.

sources.quote: Building a Center of Excellence
Crowe doubles size of dedicated AI team via AI Center of Excellence
AI transformation is 10% technology, 20% tools and processes, and 70% people
