patterns.example_scenario: A professional services firm with 8,000 employees has eight business units running AI experiments independently. Two units have sent client data to a consumer AI product, three have bought overlapping enterprise AI licences, and the legal team has received conflicting guidance on the EU AI Act from two different business unit heads. The CAIO proposes a Center of Excellence with a clear mandate: the CoE owns tool selection, responsible-AI review, and training standards; it does not own use-case prioritisation or solution building. A team of nine is assembled — two AI engineers, a responsible-AI specialist, a change manager, a legal expert, and four rotating fellows from business units. The CoE publishes its intake process: submit an AI initiative proposal via a standard form; receive a feasibility assessment, a tooling recommendation, and an EU AI Act risk rating within 10 business days. In the first quarter, 23 proposals come through intake. Twelve are approved with standard guidance; six get alternative tooling recommendations that save the firm €180,000 in redundant licences; three are flagged for legal review; two are declined as high-risk without adequate safeguards. The state-of-AI dashboard launches and is sent to all business unit heads monthly. By month six, all eight units are routing AI initiatives through intake, the consumer data incident has not recurred, and the dashboard shows 41 active use cases across the firm. The masterpiece is the dashboard and standards library — a living, org-wide record that makes the CoE's value visible and the governance gap it closed concrete.

patterns.name: Champion Network
patterns.summary: A structured network of volunteer 'champions' — trusted peers distributed across teams — who drive AI adoption from the ground up through coaching, use-case sharing, and real-time feedback, operating alongside (not replacing) top-down training.
patterns.intent: Scale AI adoption to every corner of the org by activating peer trust, which travels further than any executive mandate or formal training program.
patterns.when_to_apply: Apply once a critical mass of early AI users exists — enough to recruit from without depleting any single team — and when formal training has reached employees but real daily use is not following. The network addresses the gap between 'I completed the course' and 'I use AI every day.'

inputs.name: Volunteer pool of credible early users
inputs.description: People who already use AI meaningfully in their own work and are trusted by their peers — not just enthusiastic, but genuinely capable and respected. Enthusiasm without peer credibility produces advocates nobody listens to.

inputs.name: Advocate playbook and support infrastructure
inputs.description: A written playbook covering the four advocate roles (make AI practical, support peers, sustain learning, gather feedback), a dedicated community channel, and monthly check-ins with program leadership. Without infrastructure, the network disperses within weeks.

outputs.name: More capable org
outputs.description: An organisation where AI adoption reaches frontline workers and laggard teams through peer-to-peer channels that executive communication cannot reach.

outputs.name: Adoption feedback map
outputs.description: The masterpiece: a running map of where adoption is thriving, where it is stalling, and what specific confusions or blockers keep surfacing — collected by advocates and fed back to the central enablement team.

steps.name: Issue a transparent call to action
steps.description: Publish the advocate role openly: what advocates do, how much time it takes (30-60 min/week minimum), and what they get in return — access to advanced resources, recognition, and community. Transparency about commitment separates genuine volunteers from performative sign-ups.

steps.name: Recruit across breadth, not depth
steps.description: Prioritise breadth — representation across business units, seniority levels, and functions — over recruiting the most expert AI users. An advocate in the finance team who is a mid-level user is more valuable to finance colleagues than an expert advocate who sits in the AI team.

steps.name: Run structured onboarding
steps.description: Give each advocate a playbook, a community hub (dedicated Slack channel or equivalent), and a 90-day plan. The first month focuses on making AI practical in their own team; months two and three add peer support, sustained learning, and active feedback gathering.

steps.name: Equip advocates with four defined roles
steps.description: Make AI practical by sharing real examples from their own work. Support peers by answering questions in real time. Sustain learning by surfacing new tools and techniques. Gather feedback by reporting what confuses people back to program leadership. Each role is concrete; 'be a champion' is not.

steps.name: Celebrate and elevate standout advocates
steps.description: At the 90-day mark, publicly recognise advocates whose teams have measurably adopted AI. Offer standout advocates regional or topic leadership roles — this gives the network a career-relevant incentive structure and creates the next layer of program leadership.

principles: Peer trust is the mechanism — an advocate's influence comes from being a trusted colleague, not from being formally appointed; the appointment is only the activation.
Breadth of reach matters more than depth of expertise — one strong advocate in every team beats ten experts clustered in one.
Feedback is a first-class output — if the network is not producing structured reports on what confuses people, it is a broadcast channel, not a feedback loop.
patterns.threshold: Every business unit has at least one active advocate who has run at least one peer AI demonstration with their own team and submitted at least one feedback report to program leadership.
patterns.masterpiece: A documented adoption feedback map — updated monthly — showing which teams are actively using AI, which are stalling, and the top three recurring blockers, with at least one blocker resolved per quarter using the map's evidence.

known_uses.note: Documented in GitHub's internal playbook; three-phase 90-day blueprint with explicit measurement framework

known_uses.name: Claude Champions Program (recommended)
known_uses.quote: guidance on Champions Programs and Train the Trainer initiatives
known_uses.note: Included as a formal artifact in Anthropic's three-phase enterprise adoption course (Activation → Acceleration → Expansion)

known_failure_modes.note: Anti-pattern: the organisation recruits advocates but gives them no protected time, recognition, or support. Advocacy competes with job performance and loses. Within two months the network is nominally active but functionally dormant.

Anti-pattern: advocates are used only to push training announcements and tool updates downward, with no structured feedback upward. This treats the network as a mailing list, not a learning system, and eliminates the feedback-loop value that justifies the investment.

patterns.example_scenario: A 2,000-person financial services company has completed company-wide AI literacy training. Course completion is at 91%, but three months later a manager survey shows only 23% of employees use AI more than once a week. The head of L&D launches a Champion Network. She posts an open call: 30–60 minutes per week, a playbook, a peer Slack channel, and quarterly recognition for standout contributors. Sixty people volunteer from across operations, compliance, finance, and client services. Each goes through a half-day onboarding and gets a 90-day plan. In month one, advocates each run one live AI demonstration with their team using real work — not a scripted demo. In month two they start answering peer questions in the Slack channel and logging recurring confusions in a shared tracker. By the 90-day mark, weekly AI use is up to 51% across the org. The feedback tracker shows the top blocker: employees do not trust that their AI outputs are compliant with client data rules. The compliance team uses that signal to publish a one-page 'safe use' guide that the advocates share. Weekly use climbs to 67% in the following month. The masterpiece is the feedback tracker: a living document that turned adoption stall into a solvable problem.

patterns.name: Frontier Firm Leap

guardrail: transformation must redesign workflows, not just add AI tools to existing ones
anti-pattern: adding AI as a layer over broken processes

patterns.summary: Move the whole organisation past 'adoption' into a rebuilt operating model where AI and agents are native to how work is designed — not retrofitted on top. This is the org-level Threshold from orchestrator to principal.
patterns.intent: Cross the threshold from AI adoption to AI-first by rebuilding the organisation's operating model so that AI is native to how work is designed rather than layered on top of existing processes.
patterns.when_to_apply: Apply when the organisation has cleared broad AI literacy and has AI embedded in several workflows, but the operating model itself — how the org is structured, how work is sequenced, what headcount decisions are made — has not changed. Frontier Firm Leap is not a training move; it is a business model move triggered by the evidence that AI-native competitors are achieving outcomes the current model cannot match.

inputs.name: Workflow audit identifying redesign candidates
inputs.description: An honest assessment of which core workflows can be fundamentally redesigned — not augmented — with AI and agents. The audit distinguishes between workflows where AI saves 20% of time (augmentation) and those where AI changes the number of people required, the speed of the full cycle, or the quality ceiling (redesign).

inputs.name: Agentic AI deployment capability
inputs.description: The technical and operational capacity to deploy AI agents inside department-level workflows — not just individual productivity tools — including integration with existing systems, oversight design, and the change management to shift how people work alongside agents rather than beside a tool.

outputs.name: More capable org
outputs.description: An organisation that competes differently because its operating model is rebuilt around AI — achieving outcomes in speed, scale, or quality that were not achievable before, not just doing the same work faster.

outputs.name: Rebuilt operating model
outputs.description: The masterpiece: a documented rebuilt operating model for at least one function — new workflow architecture, new role definitions, new headcount model, and a measured business outcome that demonstrates the frontier performance the prior model could not achieve.

steps.name: Audit for redesign, not augmentation
steps.description: Map the organisation's core workflows and classify each into one of three types. Augmentation means AI saves time within an unchanged process. Redesign means AI changes who does the work, how many people it takes, or what the quality ceiling is. Transformation means AI changes what the function can offer as a competitive proposition. Prioritise the redesign and transformation candidates.

steps.name: Deploy agentic AI inside department-level workflows
steps.description: Move beyond individual productivity tools. Deploy AI agents that handle end-to-end steps in a workflow — claims routing, contract review triage, customer inquiry resolution — with humans overseeing outcomes rather than executing every step. This is the structural move that changes headcount and quality, not prompt libraries.

steps.name: Set a frontier transformation target
steps.description: Define a specific business metric that will prove the new model outperforms the old. Examples: a claims team that processes 40% more volume with unchanged headcount, a product team that ships twice as many experiments per quarter, or a finance team that closes the books in three days instead of ten. The metric is the test of whether transformation happened or just adoption did.

steps.name: Require business unit leaders to redesign their team's way of working
steps.description: Frontier Firm Leap cannot be delegated to an AI team or a CoE. Require each business unit leader to produce a redesigned team operating model — new workflow, new role expectations, new quality standards — reviewed by the CAIO. Leaders who produce augmentation proposals rather than redesign proposals are sent back to revise.

steps.name: Publicise frontier status as a talent and competitive signal
steps.description: When a function reaches its frontier transformation target, announce it publicly — to employees, in hiring materials, and where relevant to customers and investors. Frontier status attracts AI-forward talent and signals to the market that the organisation has crossed a threshold most competitors have not.

principles: Redesign eliminates steps — the goal is not to automate the current process but to discover which steps the current process only includes because AI did not exist.
The frontier metric is the test — if no business metric changed beyond what training alone would predict, the leap did not happen; adoption did.
Frontier status is a talent magnet — AI-forward people want to work where the operating model has already changed, not where it is still being discussed.
patterns.threshold: At least one function has reached its frontier transformation target — a measured business outcome that was not achievable under the prior operating model — with a documented rebuilt workflow, revised role definitions, and public attribution to the AI-native redesign.
patterns.masterpiece: A documented rebuilt operating model for at least one function, including the pre- and post-redesign workflow architecture, revised role definitions, new headcount model, and the measured frontier metric result — demonstrating that the function now competes differently rather than doing the same work faster.

known_failure_modes.note: Anti-pattern: a function adds AI tools to its existing workflow, measures time savings, and declares a frontier transformation. If the headcount model, the competitive offering, and the operating structure are unchanged, augmentation happened. The test is whether outcomes changed that the prior model could not achieve.

Anti-pattern: deploying agentic AI on top of a dysfunctional process rather than redesigning the process first. AI amplifies whatever process it runs on — a broken claims workflow with AI automation produces broken outcomes faster and at greater scale. The audit step must identify which processes need redesign before any agent deployment.

patterns.example_scenario: A mid-size insurance carrier has completed all three phases of a maturity-stage rollout. Literacy is at 91%, and five workflows have AI embedded with measured adoption. The CAIO now faces the question the board asked: what is the firm's competitive advantage if every insurer does what they just did? He runs a workflow audit across all functions. The claims function is classified as a redesign candidate: the current model requires a human adjuster to review every claim document before routing. An agentic AI workflow can ingest a claim, cross-reference policy terms, flag anomalies, and produce a routing decision with confidence scoring. This eliminates the manual review step for 70% of claims. The claims transformation target is set: process 40% more claims volume with current headcount and reduce cycle time from 5 days to 1.5 days on standard claims. The claims director redesigns the operating model: adjusters shift from reviewing every claim to overseeing agent output on flagged cases and handling the 30% of complex claims the agent routes to human review. The agent is deployed over 12 weeks. At the 16-week mark, standard-claim cycle time is 1.4 days and volume capacity is up 43% with unchanged headcount. The CAIO publishes the result publicly, in a LinkedIn post and a press release. Within 60 days, three experienced claims professionals apply for open roles, citing the frontier operating model as the reason. The masterpiece is the rebuilt claims operating model: a documented before-and-after workflow architecture, revised adjuster role definition, and the measured frontier metrics that prove the function now competes differently.

patterns.name: Lead from the Front

guardrail: leaders must genuinely use AI, not just endorse it

patterns.summary: Start AI training with managers and executives, not frontline staff. When leaders visibly use AI in their own work, they signal that it matters — and employee positivity about GenAI more than triples compared to low-leadership-support environments.
patterns.intent: Unlock org-wide AI adoption by having leaders learn first and model genuine use before asking anyone else to change how they work.
patterns.when_to_apply: Apply at the very start of any AI rollout, before broad deployment to frontline staff. If leaders skip the training or receive only a briefing while employees get the full programme, the signal is that AI is for workers, not for people who lead — and adoption follows that signal downward. Apply also when adoption is stalling despite good training: often the gap traces to managers who are not modelling use.

inputs.name: Leadership cohort with genuine time commitment
inputs.description: Managers and executives who will complete the same foundational AI training as employees — not a special executive summary — and who will publicly share their own AI use cases. If senior leaders are too busy to train, the programme signals that AI is lower priority than everything else on their calendar.

inputs.name: Visible use cases from leaders' own work
inputs.description: Real examples of AI output that the CEO, managers, or other leaders produced in their actual work — a draft strategy memo, a meeting summary, a research synthesis. Staged demos undermine the signal; genuine use cases amplify it.

outputs.name: More capable org
outputs.description: An organisation where AI adoption is normalised from the top, so that frontline employees adopt without waiting for permission or overcoming fear of standing out as the first person on their team to use AI.

outputs.name: Leadership AI use-case showcase
outputs.description: The masterpiece: a curated, living collection of real AI use cases from managers and executives — shared in all-hands sessions, internal newsletters, or a dedicated channel — that makes visible leadership modeling a permanent feature rather than a one-time launch gesture.

steps.name: Sequence training so leaders go first
steps.description: Structure the rollout so managers and executives complete AI literacy before their direct reports. This is a scheduling commitment, not just a communication. If the manager cohort has not finished when reports start their training, the effect is lost.

steps.name: Require the same training, not a briefing
steps.description: Leaders complete the identical foundational training programme as employees — same length, same exercises, same assessment. A special executive briefing communicates that real learning is for others. Pfizer's CEO Albert Bourla completing the same course as thousands of colleagues is the model.

steps.name: Ask leaders to share their own AI use cases publicly
steps.description: After training, request that every leader produces and shares at least one real AI use case from their own work — in an all-hands meeting, a Slack message, or a team email. The share should be genuine: the actual prompt, the actual output, the actual result. If it takes 30 minutes to prepare, it is still worth it.

steps.name: Brief managers on coaching direct reports through uncertainty
steps.description: Give managers a short playbook on how to respond when direct reports raise concerns about AI: fear of job loss, worry about accuracy, discomfort with change. Managers who cannot hold this conversation convincingly signal that the concerns are unresolved rather than addressable.

steps.name: Make manager modeling a sustained practice
steps.description: Do not treat leader use-case sharing as a launch event. Build it into regular management routines: a standing agenda item in team meetings to share one AI use from the past week, or a monthly internal newsletter with one executive use case. Sustained visibility is what creates norm change.

principles: Genuine use is the signal — an executive who endorses AI but visibly does not use it communicates that it is important for others, not for people at their level.
Sequence is the mechanism — training leaders first is not a courtesy; it is the structural condition that makes role modeling possible before adoption pressure reaches frontline staff.
Employee positivity about AI tracks leadership support more than any other single variable; the investment in leaders multiplies through every person they lead.
patterns.threshold: Every manager with direct reports has completed foundational AI training and has shared at least one genuine AI use case from their own work with their team.
patterns.masterpiece: A leadership AI use-case showcase — a living collection of real AI outputs produced by managers and executives, publicly accessible to all staff — that has been updated at least once per quarter since launch and has at least one contribution from every level of management.

Employees read the gap instantly — the signal is 'AI is important for you, not for me' — and adoption stalls precisely among the people who take cues from leadership behavior.

Anti-pattern: senior leaders receive a condensed briefing rather than the full training programme. This creates a knowledge gap — managers cannot answer real questions from direct reports — and signals that the full training is for workers, not leaders.

patterns.example_scenario: A 5,000-person consulting firm is rolling out an AI research assistant to all staff. The rollout plan starts with all-staff training in week one. The Chief People Officer reviews the BCG data showing that leadership support triples employee positivity about AI, and restructures the plan: the executive team and all people managers complete training in weeks one and two. The CPO sends the CEO a one-page brief on why this matters. The CEO agrees and publicly completes the training, then shares a Slack message in the company's all-hands channel showing an actual AI output he produced: a synthesis of competitor moves ahead of a partner meeting, with the prompt he used. The message says: 'It took me 12 minutes. Here is what it produced. Here is how I used it.' Fifteen managing directors post their own examples within 48 hours. By the time frontline staff start training in week three, AI is already normalised as something leaders visibly do — not just something workers are asked to learn. Post-training use rate at 60 days is 71%, versus a control division that followed the original all-staff-first sequence at 38%. The masterpiece is the leadership showcase channel: 47 genuine use cases from 23 leaders, updated weekly, that serves as the org's most credible AI adoption signal.

patterns.name: Maturity-Stage Rollout

guardrail: treat as change management, not training rollout
avoid stalling in literacy — most orgs over-invest in literacy and under-invest in domain transformation

patterns.summary: Roll out AI capability org-wide in three ordered phases — literacy (everyone understands AI), adoption (AI embedded in daily workflows), domain transformation (AI rewrites how whole functions compete) — treating each as a change-management effort, not a course catalogue.
patterns.intent: Build durable, org-wide AI capability by sequencing through three distinct maturity phases, each of which requires different leadership moves and different measures of success.
patterns.when_to_apply: Apply at the start of any org-wide AI transformation initiative as the governing frame for the full programme. Also apply as a diagnostic tool when an org that has completed literacy training is not seeing adoption: the maturity model makes the stuck phase visible and directs the right intervention. Do not compress all three phases into a single programme — the skills and leadership moves required are different at each stage.

inputs.name: Org-wide baseline assessment
inputs.description: A measurement of where the organisation currently sits across the three dimensions — what percentage of staff have foundational literacy, what percentage actively use AI in workflows, and whether any functions have redesigned their operating model around AI. Without a baseline, the programme starts at the wrong phase.

inputs.name: Named phase owners with change management authority
inputs.description: A leader for each phase who owns both the learning agenda and the workflow or org-design changes the phase requires. A programme owner who only controls training delivery cannot execute phases two and three, where the real change happens in processes and role definitions.

outputs.name: More capable org
outputs.description: An organisation that has moved through all three phases and has AI embedded in daily work and in how key functions compete — not just in the awareness and tool access of individual employees.

outputs.name: Maturity progression record
outputs.description: The masterpiece: a documented progression record showing where the org stood at each phase gate — literacy percentage, workflow adoption rate, and domain transformation initiatives launched — used to direct ongoing investment and to demonstrate transformation progress to the board.

steps.name: Phase 1 — Build literacy across all staff
steps.description: Every employee reaches a defined foundational level: they understand what AI can and cannot do, they have used an AI tool at least once for a real task, and they can identify one relevant use case for their role. The delivery mechanism is short e-learning, all-hands demos, and leadership storytelling — not deep technical training. The measure is completion with assessment pass, not just attendance.

steps.name: Phase 2 — Embed AI in specific workflows
steps.description: Select three to five workflows per business unit and redesign them with AI embedded — not as an optional add-on, but as a required step in the new process. Change role definitions, incentives, and standard operating procedures, not just tool access. This phase is messier than literacy: it requires managers to redesign how work gets done, which is harder than sending staff to training.

steps.name: Phase 3 — Redesign how functions compete
steps.description: Identify one or two functions where AI enables a fundamentally different competitive position. Examples: a legal team that reviews five times as many contracts, a product team that ships twice as fast, or a finance team that closes the books in days rather than weeks. Pair technical and functional experts to design the new operating model. Most orgs reach phase three only in isolated pockets; treat reaching it at all as a significant achievement and study those pockets to replicate.

steps.name: Use maturity assessments at each phase gate
steps.description: Before advancing from one phase to the next, run a structured assessment: what percentage of staff have cleared the literacy threshold, what percentage of target workflows have AI embedded with measured adoption, how many domain transformation initiatives have launched. Phase gates prevent the common failure of calling the literacy phase complete when 60% of staff have attended a session.

steps.name: Treat the whole arc as change management
steps.description: Assign a change lead — not just an L&D manager — who owns the narrative, the workflow redesign agenda, and the leadership engagement strategy for all three phases. The training is only the content delivery; the change management is what makes it stick.

principles: Each phase requires different leadership moves — literacy needs communicators, adoption needs process redesigners, transformation needs business model thinkers. The same leader rarely excels at all three.
Most orgs stall in literacy — they over-invest in courses and under-invest in the workflow redesign that phase two requires; call the stall explicitly and intervene.
Phase three is the competitive differentiator — it is where AI transforms outcomes rather than augmenting effort, and most peers will not reach it.
patterns.threshold: The org can demonstrate measurable AI use embedded in at least three production workflows — not pilots, but changed standard operating procedures — and at least one function has defined a new competitive capability that AI enables.
patterns.masterpiece: A maturity progression record — board-ready — showing baseline, phase-gate results, current position, and next phase investment plan, with at least three named workflow transformations and one function-level competitive capability change as evidence.

known_uses.note: Published Dec 2025; three dimensions: AI literacy, AI adoption, AI domain transformation

known_failure_modes.note: Anti-pattern: the org declares literacy-phase success at 70–80% completion and invests in more literacy content when adoption is the actual problem. Literacy produces awareness; it does not produce use. Once diminishing returns set in on literacy investment, the programme must pivot to workflow redesign.

Anti-pattern: treating the three phases as a training programme — more courses at each level — rather than as a change management effort that requires workflow redesign, process change, and leadership accountability at phases two and three. McKinsey's language: 'Companies that treat upskilling as a training rollout miss the larger point.'

patterns.example_scenario: A 20,000-person insurance company starts an org-wide AI programme. The CPO runs a baseline: 12% of staff have used an AI tool for real work, 3% use one daily, and no workflow has been formally redesigned around AI. The programme is structured in three phases with hard gates. Phase one delivers an 8-hour foundational curriculum to all staff over 12 weeks; the gate is 90% pass rate on the assessment. The company reaches 87% in week 14 — insufficient; targeted coaching runs for two more weeks and reaches 91%. Phase two targets five high-volume workflows: claims triage, policy renewal communication, compliance review preparation, actuarial data synthesis, and customer complaint routing. Each workflow gets a dedicated redesign team — a business analyst and an AI engineer — who redesign the standard operating procedure with AI embedded. Adoption is measured by tool-use events in the workflow, not by training completion. After 16 weeks, four of five workflows show greater than 70% AI-assisted completion; the fifth (actuarial synthesis) requires a model accuracy improvement before it clears. Phase three launches in claims and compliance: the claims function redesigns its processing model to handle 40% more volume without additional headcount; the compliance team launches an AI-first contract review programme. The maturity progression record, updated quarterly, shows the board exactly where the org stands, where investment went, and what competitive change has been demonstrated. The masterpiece is that record: a documented journey from 12% individual use to two function-level competitive transformations.

patterns.name: Practice Guild
patterns.summary: A standing internal community — guild or Center of Excellence — that owns AI best practices, shares use cases, and anchors the 'AI-first' way of working across all teams. It acts as both knowledge hub and governance body.
patterns.intent: Create a permanent internal home for AI knowledge, governance, and peer learning so capability compounds org-wide rather than staying trapped in isolated teams.
patterns.when_to_apply: Apply when the org has enough early AI activity across multiple teams that knowledge is being reinvented in silos, governance questions are being answered inconsistently, and there is no shared place to escalate responsible-AI concerns or share what works.

inputs.name: Named executive sponsor and facilitator
inputs.description: A senior leader who owns the guild's mandate and a dedicated facilitator who runs the rhythm — open sessions, curated content, and escalation handling. Without both, the community stalls within months.

inputs.name: Cross-team participation mandate
inputs.description: Representatives or liaisons from every major business unit, so the guild reflects real use cases across the org rather than becoming an IT or innovation-team echo chamber.

outputs.name: More capable org
outputs.description: An organisation where AI knowledge, tooling decisions, and governance standards are shared and compound over time rather than being reinvented per team.

outputs.name: Living AI practice library
outputs.description: The masterpiece: a curated, internal toolkit of prompt libraries, decision guides, use-case templates, and responsible-AI review checklists that every team can access and contribute to.

steps.name: Stand up the body with a clear mandate
steps.description: Appoint a named guild leader and define the three-part mandate explicitly: explore new AI capabilities, share proven patterns, govern responsible use. Publish the mandate so business units know what the guild is for and what it is not.

steps.name: Divide into sub-teams by function
steps.description: Split into at minimum three working streams — exploration (lab), engineering (build), and enablement (adoption). Each stream has a coordinator and a standing meeting cadence. This prevents the guild from collapsing into a single recurring meeting that discusses everything and decides nothing.

steps.name: Run regular open sessions
steps.description: Hold recurring open sessions — biweekly or monthly — where any team can demo a use case, share a failure, or raise a governance question. Use-case demos are the lifeblood of the guild; abstract AI conversations kill engagement.

steps.name: Publish and maintain the practice library
steps.description: After every session, capture what was shared in a format others can reuse: a prompt template, a decision guide, a 'what not to do' note. The library is the persistent output; the session is only the mechanism for generating it.

steps.name: Act as the escalation point for governance
steps.description: Make the guild the named place where teams bring AI use cases that raise responsible-use concerns — data privacy, bias, regulatory risk. The guild reviews, advises, and escalates to legal or compliance when needed. This governance role is what justifies the guild's standing authority.

principles: The guild is a knowledge hub and a governance body — combining both prevents the hub from becoming a club and the governance body from becoming a bureaucracy.
Use-case demos drive attendance and learning more than any structured curriculum; make sharing real work the core ritual.
The practice library outlasts any individual member — invest in keeping it current or the guild's value disappears when key people leave.
patterns.threshold: Every business unit has at least one person who attends guild sessions and can explain the guild's current governance position on at least one active AI topic.
patterns.masterpiece: A practice library — accessible to all staff — containing at minimum five reusable prompt templates, three decision guides, and one responsible-AI review checklist, all produced and used by teams outside the guild core.

known_uses.quote: The AI CoE will be multidisciplinary across the firm's portfolio of services… the AI enablement team helps clients educate their personnel, innovate new processes, and deploy solutions within their organizations.
known_uses.note: Expanded Oct 2024; tripled AI lab and engineering team; named firmwide AI leader

known_uses.name: Communities of Practice (pillar 8)
known_uses.quote: Dedicated forums for peer-to-peer learning, knowledge sharing, and collaborative problem-solving.
known_uses.note: One of eight foundational pillars in GitHub's internal AI-powered workforce playbook

known_failure_modes.note: Anti-pattern: the guild shares knowledge but has no actual authority over tool selection, responsible-AI review, or training standards. Without authority it becomes a discussion club that business units ignore when convenience conflicts with best practice.

Anti-pattern: the guild runs good sessions but captures nothing in a reusable form. When key members leave, institutional knowledge leaves with them. The library is the persistence mechanism; sessions without library work are ephemeral.

patterns.example_scenario: A 4,000-person professional services firm has AI experiments running in three separate business units — tax, audit, and advisory — with no coordination. Each unit has picked a different tool, written its own prompt conventions, and answered data-privacy questions differently. The CISO raises a flag: two units have sent client data to a consumer-tier AI product. The firm's new AI Managing Principal charters a Practice Guild with three sub-teams. The lab team tests and recommends a single enterprise-licensed platform. The engineering team builds a shared prompt library covering the top-ten use cases across units. The enablement team runs monthly open sessions where practitioners demo real workflows. Within six months, all three units are using the same platform, the data-privacy incident has not recurred, and the practice library contains 23 reusable prompt templates that new hires reference in onboarding. The guild's monthly session becomes the most-attended internal event in the firm. The masterpiece is the living library: a curated, version-controlled collection of patterns that accumulates without depending on any single person's continued presence.

patterns.name: Seed the Veterans

guardrail: veterans must have genuine AI depth, not just enthusiasm
avoid creating a dependency that blocks team self-sufficiency

patterns.summary: Take people who already use AI well — early adopters from a pilot or power-user cohort — and physically embed them in new teams for a fixed sprint. The team learns by watching, asking, and copying, not by sitting through a course.
patterns.intent: Transfer working AI capability to new teams through direct peer observation and co-working rather than through any form of instruction.
patterns.when_to_apply: Apply after a pilot has created a pool of genuinely capable AI users and a new wave of teams is starting to receive access. The gap this pattern closes runs from 'I have the tool and I completed the training' to 'I use the tool fluently in my daily work'. Embed veterans in teams where daily work is sufficiently similar to the veteran's background for the modeling to be meaningful.

inputs.name: Pool of verified AI-capable veterans
inputs.description: People who have come through a pilot or extended use period and demonstrably do real work with AI — not just enthusiasts, but people who can show finished work they produced with AI assistance and explain their process.

inputs.name: Receiving teams with genuine work to do
inputs.description: Teams that have active, non-trivial work underway — not teams set up for a training exercise. The veteran's modeling is most powerful when the team can immediately try what they just watched on something that actually matters.

outputs.name: More capable team
outputs.description: A team that has moved from tool access to genuine daily use through direct observation of a peer doing real work with AI, not through instruction.

outputs.name: Team AI use pattern
outputs.description: The masterpiece: a documented set of AI use patterns specific to the team's actual work — prompt templates, workflow integration points, known pitfalls — built by the veteran and the team together during the embed, and retained by the team after the veteran leaves.

steps.name: Run a pilot to create the veteran pool
steps.description: Before any embedding, run a 4–8 week pilot with a motivated cohort to produce people with genuine AI depth. The pilot's output is the veteran pool; without it, embedding spreads enthusiasm rather than capability.

steps.name: Assign one veteran to each receiving team
steps.description: Place one veteran in each new team for a 2–4 week resident expert period. The assignment is a role: not to teach, but to work alongside the team and show AI in action on real work. The veteran uses AI visibly, explains their reasoning out loud, and invites the team to try the same move immediately.

steps.name: Demonstrate with real work, not slides
steps.description: The veteran's daily practice is the curriculum. Show the prompt, show the output, show the iteration, show the failure. Narrate the thinking: 'I am asking it to do this because… this output is not quite right because… I would change the prompt by…' This is the move the course could not deliver.

steps.name: Hold daily open-problem sessions
steps.description: Schedule a 20-minute daily slot where any team member brings a real current problem and the veteran solves it with AI in real time, narrating every step. The team watches and immediately tries variants. The problem must be real; synthetic exercises kill the modeling effect.

steps.name: Document the team's emerging AI use patterns
steps.description: By week two, start capturing what is working in the team's context — prompt templates for their specific task types, workflow integration points, pitfalls discovered. This documentation is co-owned by the team, not just the veteran. Before the veteran leaves, the team should be able to maintain and extend it.

steps.name: Rotate veterans across teams
steps.description: After the embed period, move the veteran to the next team. Do not extend the embed beyond its window — a team that retains a veteran indefinitely never develops self-sufficiency. Rotation also prevents veteran burnout and ensures skill spreads broadly.

principles: Showing real work is the mechanism — observing a peer solve a real problem with AI transfers more capability than any hour of instruction because the learner sees the full process, including failures and corrections.
Self-sufficiency is the exit condition — the embed is successful when the team can extend the patterns the veteran left without needing the veteran's presence.
Rotation is mandatory — a veteran who stays too long creates a dependency and stops spreading capability to teams still waiting.
patterns.threshold: The team can run a daily open-problem session without the veteran, using AI to solve a real problem from their work, and can extend or update the team AI use pattern document without external help.
patterns.masterpiece: A team AI use pattern document — built collaboratively with the veteran during the embed — containing at minimum five prompt templates for team-specific tasks, three workflow integration points, and two known pitfalls with workarounds, maintained and updated by the team after the veteran has left.

known_uses.name: 生成AI徹底理解リスキリング — Developer Cohort as Seed Pool
known_uses.quote: 日々のちょっとした困りごとを生成AIで解決しようとする文化が根付いた
known_uses.note: 152 certified Developer-tier engineers by March 2024; 99.6% of all employees completed foundational tier; target: 50% of engineers AI-capable by end 2025; lang: ja

known_uses.name: AI Advocates as embedded team experts
known_uses.quote: AI advocates are volunteer champions who are part coach, part translator, and part feedback loop.
known_uses.note: Advocates embedded per team; explicitly positioned as in-team peer mentors, not central trainers

known_failure_modes.note: Anti-pattern: the veteran embed extends indefinitely because the team finds it convenient to keep asking the veteran rather than developing their own fluency. The team becomes dependent rather than capable, and the veteran cannot move to the next team. Hard embed-end dates prevent this.

Anti-pattern: the veteran models excellent AI use for the team but nothing is captured in writing during the embed. When the veteran leaves, the team reverts to pre-embed behaviour within weeks because there is no artefact to anchor the learning.

patterns.example_scenario: A media company's content production team has just been given access to an AI writing assistant after a successful pilot in the technology team. The content team has 18 people — writers, editors, and social media managers. They completed mandatory AI literacy training at 100%, but three weeks after tool access, usage logs show only three people used the tool more than five times. The head of content asks for a veteran from the technology team pilot. A senior developer who used AI daily during the pilot moves into a dedicated desk in the content bullpen for three weeks. On day one she opens the AI tool and writes a real blog post draft in front of the team, narrating every step. She shows the brief she gave it, why the first draft was too generic, how she refined the prompt, and what she kept versus what she rewrote. Day two she runs the first open-problem session: the social media manager brings a real brief for a campaign announcement. The veteran solves it live in 14 minutes. The social manager immediately tries a variant for a different campaign and produces a usable draft in 20 minutes. By week two the team has produced nine prompt templates for their specific content types. The veteran documents them in a shared Google Doc before leaving. At the end of the three weeks, the team's weekly AI use rate is at 81%. The masterpiece is the shared prompt library: nine templates, four pitfalls noted with workarounds, and three workflow integration points — all built by the team, maintained by the team, and still in use six months after the veteran left.

patterns.name: Teach the Master
patterns.summary: Coach a small cohort of internal people to become AI trainers, then let them cascade learning to every team they touch — one trainer multiplies into dozens of colleagues without scaling the central enablement budget linearly.
patterns.intent: Multiply the reach of a small central enablement team by creating certified internal trainers who carry consistent, quality-controlled AI learning to every function they serve.
patterns.when_to_apply: Apply when the organisation is large enough that a central team cannot reach all staff directly, when budget constraints prevent hiring external trainers at scale, or when learners respond better to domain-familiar colleagues than to generic AI instructors. Works best when at least a small pool of internally credible candidates with communication skill exists.

inputs.name: Candidate pool of communicators
inputs.description: People selected for curiosity and communication skill — not primarily for existing AI expertise. A great communicator who knows 70% of the material and can run an engaging session beats a deep expert who cannot hold a room.

inputs.name: Master trainer curriculum
inputs.description: A deeper curriculum than the one masters will eventually teach: facilitation skills, scenario design, how to handle common misconceptions, and the certification standard they must meet before they are allowed to teach others.

outputs.name: More capable org
outputs.description: An organisation with distributed training capacity — internal trainers embedded in every function who can upskill their colleagues without central budget growing linearly with headcount.

outputs.name: Certified trainer cohort
outputs.description: The masterpiece: a named group of certified internal AI trainers, each with a training delivery record and a territory of responsibility, whose existence constitutes a durable, self-sustaining learning infrastructure.

steps.name: Select masters on curiosity and communication, not expertise
steps.description: Target 5–15% of the workforce as candidate masters. Screen for people who explain things clearly, ask good questions, and are respected peers — not just the most enthusiastic AI users. Domain knowledge in AI can be taught; facilitation instinct cannot be fully trained in a short programme.

steps.name: Deliver the master trainer curriculum
steps.description: Teach masters more than they will teach: the reasoning behind each concept, common misconceptions and how to address them, facilitation techniques for sceptical audiences, and how to design scenarios from real work rather than from generic AI examples.

steps.name: Certify before deployment
steps.description: Require every master to pass a certification before they train anyone else. Certification includes a live facilitation assessment observed by a senior enablement lead. This is the quality gate that makes 'certified' mean something and prevents low-quality delivery from undermining the programme.

steps.name: Deploy masters to run department-level sessions
steps.description: Assign each certified master a territory — a department, function, or geography — and a delivery target (e.g., all colleagues in territory reach foundational literacy within 90 days). Masters run workshops, lunch-and-learns, and one-on-ones as needed to hit the target.

steps.name: Run a feedback loop back to the centre
steps.description: Require masters to report recurring questions, misconceptions, and blockers after each session. The central team uses this signal to update the curriculum, patch misconceptions, and identify emerging topics. The loop prevents masters from drifting away from the current state of knowledge.

principles: Certification before deployment is load-bearing — a trainer who delivers inconsistent or inaccurate content at scale does more damage than no training at all.
Select for communication instinct, not AI depth — the master trainer programme can build knowledge; it cannot build the ability to hold a room.
The feedback loop from masters to the centre is what keeps the cascade current; without it the programme produces trainers who slowly fall behind the state of the technology.
patterns.threshold: Every certified master has delivered at least one full training session independently, received a post-session assessment, and submitted one feedback report identifying a recurring misconception from their cohort.
patterns.masterpiece: A certified trainer cohort with a documented training delivery record — total sessions delivered, total learners reached, top recurring misconceptions captured and resolved — proving that the cascade has functioned and the central investment has multiplied.

known_uses.ecosystem: German SME policy / chambers of commerce

known_uses.name: Driving enterprise adoption of Claude — Train the Trainer
known_uses.quote: Train the Trainer enablement programs
known_uses.note: Acceleration phase (days 30-90) of Anthropic's three-phase enterprise adoption framework

known_failure_modes.note: Anti-pattern: deploying internal trainers before any quality gate. Without certification, delivery quality varies wildly — the poorest sessions create active resistance to AI adoption rather than building it.

Anti-pattern: masters train their territories but report nothing back to the centre. The central curriculum becomes stale, misconceptions propagate unchecked, and the programme gradually delivers out-of-date content with no mechanism for correction.

patterns.example_scenario: A 10,000-employee manufacturing company needs to upskill its global workforce on AI — factory floor supervisors in Germany, logistics coordinators in Poland, and procurement teams in the UK. The central L&D team is seven people. Hiring external AI trainers for every site and language is cost-prohibitive. The L&D director runs a Teach the Master programme. She selects 40 candidates across all sites — not the most technically advanced people, but respected colleagues who give clear explanations and are trusted by peers. Over four weeks, the candidates complete a master trainer curriculum. It covers AI fundamentals, common workplace use cases, facilitation for sceptical rooms, and how to handle the question of whether AI will take people's jobs. Every candidate must pass a live facilitation assessment before being certified. Thirty-four pass; six re-take after coaching. The 34 certified masters then run workshops at their sites over the following eight weeks. Each master reports back after every session: the top recurring question from German supervisors is about data leaving the factory floor; from Polish logistics teams it is about AI accuracy for shipment tracking. The central team updates the FAQ guide and pushes it to all masters within a week. By the end of the programme, 8,200 employees have received training delivered by a certified peer from their own site and language group. The masterpiece is the certified cohort and its delivery record: 34 trainers, 8,200 learners reached, four curriculum updates driven by feedback.

patterns.name: Tie Reward to Proof

guardrail: measure real use, not just course completion

patterns.summary: Make AI skill-building tangible by connecting it to pay, promotion, or career-track eligibility. When credentials or demonstrated AI use flow into performance reviews and advancement criteria, adoption becomes a rational self-interest choice for every employee.
patterns.intent: Make AI capability development a self-interested rational choice for every employee by embedding it in the performance and career systems that already govern their advancement.
patterns.when_to_apply: Apply when the org has provided training and tools but voluntary adoption has plateaued — typically when early adopters are active but the majority is waiting to see whether AI use is genuinely expected or just encouraged. Also apply from the start of any large-scale rollout to signal that AI fluency is a permanent expectation, not a temporary initiative.

inputs.name: Defined AI fluency levels
inputs.description: A tiered definition of AI capability — at minimum foundational and practitioner levels — with explicit descriptions of what each level means in practice, not just which course to take. Levels without definitions cannot be measured or gamed correctly.

inputs.name: Performance system hooks
inputs.description: Formal integration points in the annual performance review, promotion criteria, or role grade definitions where AI fluency appears as a named factor — not a suggestion, but a stated criterion that reviewers use.

outputs.name: More capable org
outputs.description: An organisation where AI skill development is not a discretionary extra but a rational career investment — leading to broader and faster adoption than voluntary programmes can achieve.

outputs.name: Certification and advancement record
outputs.description: The masterpiece: a transparent, org-wide record of AI certification levels by role and department, updated annually, that shows where the org stands on a defined capability ladder and drives targeted investment in the tiers and departments that lag.

steps.name: Define AI fluency levels tied to role grades
steps.description: Establish at minimum three levels — foundational, practitioner, expert — and write a one-paragraph description of what each level means in the context of actual job performance, not just course completion. Tie levels to role grades or pay bands explicitly: foundational expected of all, practitioner expected of grade X and above, expert for specified roles.

steps.name: Embed an AI fluency development goal in performance reviews
steps.description: Add an AI fluency development goal to the company-wide annual performance template, visible to every employee and every reviewer. This makes the expectation structural rather than voluntary — every employee has a standing goal, and every manager reviews it.

steps.name: Partner with external certifiers for credential credibility
steps.description: Use external certification programmes — Google AI Professional Certification, AWS AI Practitioner, or equivalent — to give credentials external validity that internal certificates lack. External validity reduces gaming and makes the credential portable enough that employees value it beyond their current employer.

steps.name: Make certification count toward promotion eligibility
steps.description: State explicitly in promotion criteria that reaching practitioner-level certification is a factor in eligibility for certain roles. Not the only factor — this prevents the anti-pattern of gaming — but a named one. Promotions that happen without it need to note an exception.

steps.name: Track actual use alongside certification
steps.description: Pull tool event data — prompts run, documents generated, time saved — alongside certification records. An employee who holds a practitioner certificate but uses AI zero times a week has gamed the system. Tracking use closes the gap between credential and behaviour.

principles: Embedding in the performance system converts AI adoption from a cultural aspiration to a rational career move — cultures change faster when individual incentives align with the direction.
Measure actual use, not just course completion — the only reliable proof of capability is doing, and the only reliable proof of doing is evidence of use.
External certification gives the credential weight that internal certificates cannot provide — employees care more about something that means something outside the firm.
patterns.threshold: Every employee has a named AI fluency development goal in their performance review, and at least 80% of employees at practitioner-eligible grades have achieved or are actively pursuing practitioner certification.
patterns.masterpiece: A transparent org-wide certification and use dashboard — updated quarterly — showing certification levels by role and department, actual AI use frequency by tier, and year-over-year movement up the fluency ladder, used by the executive team to target investment in lagging departments.

known_uses.name: AI Professional Certification for 1.6M Associates
known_uses.quote: At Walmart, we know the future of retail won't be defined by technology alone — it will be defined by people who know how to use it. — John Furner, CEO Walmart US

known_uses.note: Pfizer AI Academy; company-wide performance integration; CEO Bourla completed same training

known_uses.name: GenAI Belt Certification System
known_uses.quote: With the rapid advancement of Generative AI, talent development has become a social imperative. By utilizing the framework we have established, we can efficiently cultivate highly skilled professionals. — Yutaka Sasaki, President and CEO
known_uses.note: Four-tier belt system (Whitebelt/Yellowbelt/Greenbelt/Blackbelt); 200,000 employees globally; won Brandon Hall Gold Award 2025; lang: ja/en

known_failure_modes.note: Anti-pattern: employees learn to pass the certification assessment without changing how they work. When certification is the only metric and it disconnects from actual use data, it measures test-taking skill rather than AI capability. The fix is tracking actual use alongside certification.

Anti-pattern: an AI fluency development goal is added to the performance template but no reviewer is trained to assess it and no criteria exist for what 'met' versus 'not met' means. The goal becomes boilerplate that everyone marks green regardless of real progress.

talent development has become a social imperative. By utilizing the framework we have established, we can efficiently cultivate highly skilled professionals.

patterns.example_scenario: A 15,000-person insurance company has run company-wide AI literacy training with 88% completion, but manager surveys show only 31% of employees use AI tools more than twice a week six months after training. The CHRO reviews the incentive structure: there is no consequence for not using AI and no reward for using it well. She proposes three changes. First, AI fluency is added as a named development goal in the annual performance template for all employees, with three defined levels (foundational / practitioner / expert). Second, promotion to senior analyst and above requires practitioner-level certification — either the company's internal badge or the AWS AI Practitioner external certification, for which the company pays. Third, the people analytics team pulls weekly AI tool event data per employee and creates a use-rate dashboard visible to managers. Within six months of implementation, practitioner certification completions jump from 1,200 to 4,800. Weekly AI use above twice per week climbs from 31% to 59%. Three departments where use remained below 40% are identified on the dashboard; their heads are briefed and offer targeted coaching. The masterpiece is the certification and use dashboard: a quarterly snapshot that drives specific targeted investment rather than blanket re-training.

patterns.name: Upskilling as Change Management

guardrail: change management budget must accompany every AI tool rollout — minimum 20% of initiative budget (Deloitte research)
do not delegate to HR as a training rollout; keep it leadership-owned

patterns.summary: Treat company-wide AI skill-building as a change management programme — with a named owner, dedicated budget, leadership accountability, and workflow redesign — not as a course catalogue or an optional L&D line item.
patterns.intent: Make AI transformation stick by treating the human side as a structured change programme with its own owner, budget, and measures — because without that, every tool rollout produces compliance without capability.
patterns.when_to_apply: Apply before any AI tool is purchased or deployed — the change programme design should precede tool selection, not follow it. Also apply when an existing AI rollout has stalled despite good tools and adequate training: the stall is almost always a change management gap, not a training gap. The pattern re-frames the problem and redirects investment.

inputs.name: Named change lead with authority over workflow redesign
inputs.description: A leader who owns both the learning agenda and the process redesign agenda, with authority to require business units to change how work gets done — not just an L&D manager who can recommend training. Without authority over workflow, the change lead can only facilitate discussion about change, not make it happen.

inputs.name: Dedicated change management budget
inputs.description: A budget line for change management as a percentage of the AI technology budget — separate from the tool cost and the training cost. Deloitte research positions a minimum of 20% of every AI initiative budget going to change management, training, and user adoption activities. If the org treats change management as zero-cost or absorbed into L&D, the programme will be under-resourced at every critical moment.

outputs.name: More capable org
outputs.description: An organisation that has changed how it works — not just what tools it has access to — because the change management programme drove workflow redesign alongside skill building, producing use rather than awareness.

outputs.name: Change programme charter and EBIT impact record
outputs.description: The masterpiece: a change programme charter — named owner, budget, workflow redesign targets, and measured milestones — plus a running EBIT impact record that documents what business outcomes changed as a result of redesigned workflows, not just training completion rates.

steps.name: Name a change lead, not a training lead
steps.description: Appoint a change lead — distinct from the L&D manager — who owns the learning programme, the workflow redesign agenda, and the leadership engagement strategy. This person is accountable for business outcomes (adoption rate, workflow change, EBIT impact), not learning outputs (course completion, assessment pass rate). The distinction is the pattern.

steps.name: Set the change management budget as a percentage of the AI budget
steps.description: Before tool procurement, allocate a named change management budget — minimum 20% of the total AI initiative spend. Document this as a line item, not as absorbed overhead. When change management competes with tool costs for the same budget, tool costs win; the only protection is structural allocation.

steps.name: Use leadership to tell the change story before tool deployment
steps.description: Before any tool reaches employees, have the CEO and senior leadership communicate: why this change is happening, what will change, what will stay the same, and how the organisation will support people through the transition. The change story is not a training announcement — it is a narrative that addresses the emotional reality of change.

steps.name: Pair every tool rollout with a process redesign sprint
steps.description: Do not deploy any AI tool into an unchanged workflow. For every tool deployment, run a concurrent process redesign sprint — a 2–4 week workshop with the business unit — that rewrites the workflow to make AI use a required step, not an optional enhancement. McKinsey's finding: workflow redesign has the biggest effect on EBIT impact from AI.

steps.name: Measure workflow change and EBIT impact, not course completion
steps.description: The programme's dashboard tracks three things: what percentage of target workflows have been redesigned, what the AI use rate is in those redesigned workflows, and what business outcome has changed (cycle time, cost per unit, quality rate). Course completion is a leading indicator at most; it is not the measure of the programme's success.

steps.name: Celebrate and publicise early workflow wins
steps.description: When a team redesigns a workflow and achieves a measurable improvement, publicise it broadly and quickly — in all-hands sessions, internal newsletters, and manager communications. Early wins sustain momentum through the difficult middle of a change programme, when the excitement of launch has faded and the hard work of changing habits is underway.

principles: AI upskilling is a change management effort, not a training rollout — the moment the programme is handed to L&D as a course catalogue, the workflow redesign agenda is effectively abandoned.
Budget allocation is the honesty test — if change management has no budget line of its own, the programme's real priority is tools, not people.
Workflow redesign is the mechanism — the EBIT impact from AI tracks to workflow change, not to training completion; McKinsey's finding holds consistently across industries.
patterns.threshold: Every AI tool deployment is paired with a concurrent process redesign sprint, and the programme dashboard tracks workflow change rate and EBIT impact alongside course completion.
patterns.masterpiece: A change programme charter with a named owner, explicit budget allocation, workflow redesign targets, and a running EBIT impact record — updated quarterly — showing the business outcomes that changed because of redesigned workflows, not just the training that was delivered.

known_uses.name: Redefine AI upskilling as a change imperative
known_uses.quote: Companies that treat upskilling as a training rollout miss the larger point: it is a change management effort.
known_uses.note: McKinsey Organization Blog, Dec 2025; prescriptive framework for treating AI upskilling as change management

known_uses.name: AI transformation is 70% people (10-20-70)
known_uses.quote: AI transformation is 10% technology, 20% tools and processes, and 70% people.
known_uses.note: BCG's 10-20-70 framework; foundational framing used across BCG AI@Scale engagements

known_failure_modes.note: Anti-pattern: the change management responsibility is assigned to the L&D team with no additional authority or budget. The L&D team can deliver training but cannot redesign workflows or hold business unit leaders accountable for changing how work is done. The programme produces course completions and no measurable business change.

Anti-pattern: AI tools are purchased, negotiated, and deployed before any change management programme is designed. Change management is then retrofitted as an explanation for why adoption is low. Retrofitted change management almost never achieves the adoption rates of change management that preceded deployment.

sources.quote: Companies that treat upskilling as a training rollout miss the larger point: it is a change management effort.
AI transformation is 10% technology, 20% tools and processes, and 70% people.
It is not a question of technology, it is a question of organizational ability to adjust and transform itself through AI.

patterns.example_scenario: A 12,000-person logistics company buys an enterprise AI assistant licence for all staff. The initial plan is to have the L&D team deliver a four-hour mandatory training module and then let departments adopt at their own pace. The CHRO pauses the rollout and proposes a different framing. She appoints a Chief Transformation Officer — not the L&D lead — as the change lead for the AI rollout, with authority to require workflow redesigns from business unit heads. She negotiates a change management budget equal to 25% of the tool licence cost. Before any employee sees the tool, the CEO holds a company-wide town hall: why the company is changing how it works, what roles will look like in three years, and what support the organisation will provide. The CTO then runs concurrent process redesign sprints with each business unit as the tool rolls out. The freight scheduling team redesigns its daily workflow so AI generates the first-draft schedule, which schedulers review and override; the prior workflow had schedulers building the schedule from scratch. At the 90-day mark: freight scheduling cycle time is down 38%, and schedulers report spending 60% more time on exception handling — which they say is the part of the job that actually requires their judgment. The programme dashboard tracks three numbers: workflow redesign completion (6 of 9 target workflows redesigned), AI use rate in redesigned workflows (74%), and cost-per-shipment change (down 12% in redesigned workflows). Course completion is tracked but is not the headline. The masterpiece is the change programme charter — naming the change lead, the budget, the workflow targets, and the EBIT impact record that shows which outcomes changed and why.

patterns.name: Agent-Native Startup Cohort
patterns.summary: A track — currently an identified gap, not a verified program — for a multi-week cohort that explicitly teaches founding and operating a business whose core production capacity runs on an AI agent stack. No such global English-language program was found as of mid-2026. Major accelerators (Google for Startups, Antler, YC, Techstars) accept and support AI-product startups but focus their curriculum on building AI products for external customers, not on training founders to run their own operations via agents. KAIST OverEdge (Korean, government-funded) is the only nationally-scoped program matching this definition.
patterns.intent: Run a multi-week cohort that trains founders to design, build, and operate a business whose core production functions are handled by an AI agent stack, from idea to first revenue.
patterns.when_to_apply: Apply this track when a program operator wants to produce agent-native founders at cohort scale — not just AI-literate entrepreneurs. Use it when the operator has access to agent-architecture mentors, real business problem contexts, and the infrastructure to support cohort-level live agent builds. As of mid-2026, program operators considering this track must build it themselves; no off-the-shelf version exists in English.

inputs.name: Cohort of 10-20 founders with business ideas
inputs.description: Founders with enough domain knowledge to identify agent-replaceable functions in a specific market or industry.

inputs.name: Agent-architecture mentor faculty
inputs.description: Practitioners or researchers with direct experience designing and operating multi-agent systems in commercial settings — the critical missing resource in most existing accelerators.

inputs.name: 12-week structured curriculum
inputs.description: A week-by-week program covering agent-stack scoping, first agent build, orchestration, revenue milestone, and governance — as described in the AI-First Venture Build move but delivered in cohort format with peer accountability.

inputs.name: AI infrastructure access
inputs.description: Cloud credits, API access, and tool licenses so cohort founders can build and run real agent stacks without cost barriers blocking experimentation.

inputs.name: Investor and commercial partner network
inputs.description: Investors who understand agent-native businesses and commercial partners willing to be first customers for cohort agent stacks — both are required to make the revenue milestone stage credible.

outputs.name: Agent-native founders
outputs.description: A cohort of founders who can independently design, deploy, and operate multi-agent stacks as their primary business operating model.

outputs.name: Portfolio of agent-operated startups
outputs.description: 10-20 early-stage companies, each with a live agent stack handling at least one department's work and a documented path to first revenue — the collective masterpiece of the cohort.

outputs.name: Agent-native playbook
outputs.description: A documented body of decisions, failure modes, and governance patterns from the cohort that can inform future cohorts and the broader practitioner community.

steps.name: Weeks 1-2: Agent-stack scoping
steps.description: Each founder maps their target business across core functions and identifies which are agent-replaceable versus human-required. Cohort workshop format with cross-founder critique. Output: a committed agent replacement roadmap for each founder.

steps.name: Weeks 3-4: First agent build with real customers
steps.description: Each founder builds their first production agent and tests it with real customers or users. Agent-architecture mentors provide technical review. Peer cohort gives feedback on product fit and agent scope.

steps.name: Weeks 5-8: Orchestration and department replacement
steps.description: Founders add orchestration layers and progress toward full department replacement. Weekly cohort reviews compare cost-per-outcome data across the group. Shared failure modes are documented and circulated as cohort learning.

steps.name: Weeks 9-12: Revenue sprint and investor readiness
steps.description: Founders drive to first revenue with the agent stack as the core narrative. Investor readiness sessions frame the agent stack as the business's defensible operating model, not just a cost-saving feature. Cohort culminates in a demo day where live agent stacks are shown — not slide decks.

principles: Agent stack first, team second — the cohort's goal is companies whose agent architecture is the moat, not a feature.
Revenue is the proof gate — no founder advances past week eight without a live agent handling real commercial traffic.
Cohort learning amplifies individual builds — failure modes discovered by one founder become curriculum for all.
Demo day shows live systems, not decks — investor conversations are grounded in running agent stacks, not projections.
patterns.threshold: Every cohort founder can demonstrate a live agent stack handling at least one production business function with real customer traffic, and has reached or credibly projected a first revenue milestone.
patterns.masterpiece: A cohort-level portfolio of agent-native startups, each with a running multi-agent stack, first revenue, and a documented governance model — presented to investors at a demo day that shows live systems.

known_uses.name: KAIST OverEdge (nearest analog, Korean only)
known_uses.quote: 1인 창업의 한계를 넘어서는 순간, OverEdge
known_uses.note: Korean, government-funded, just launching — closest real-world match but not a global cohort

known_failure_modes.note: The anti-pattern of the cohort drifting into training founders to build AI products for customers rather than training them to run their own operations on agents. This is the pattern every major existing accelerator has fallen into; it requires active curriculum correction.

The anti-pattern of advancing founders to investor readiness without a revenue milestone gate. Agent-native pitch decks without live revenue data are indistinguishable from any other AI startup narrative; the principal step requires the real thing.

Staffing the cohort with AI product managers or ML engineers rather than practitioners who have operated multi-agent stacks in commercial settings. The missing mentor type — the agent-native operator — is what makes this cohort hard to run in 2026.
